Construction Period Draw: During the D&C Period, the Collateral Agent shall, unless an Event of Default 
has occurred and is continuing or will occur as a result of such transfer, upon receipt  of a Construction Account 
Withdrawal Certificate, and subject to compliance with "—Conditions Precedent to all Construction Draws" below, 
make a Construction Period Draw. 
Releases from Project Accounts (Construction Period): On or before the Project Final Completion Date, 
and provided no Event of Default has o ccurred and is continuing or would result therefrom, the Collateral Agent 
shall transfer funds in the Construction Account in the following order and priority: 
(a) First, from the Construction Account, to pay the Collateral Agent, the Bond Trustee, and the 
Rating Agency (on the dates set forth in their respective fee letters, where applicable) on account of their respective 
fees and any expenses in connection with the performance of their obligations under the Finance Documents and 
Project Documents; 
(b) Second, from the Construction Account (or s ub-account thereof) on each Bonds Payment Date, to 
the Bond Trustee in accordan ce with the Bond Indenture, to pay the required payments on the Senior Debt 
(including, if applicable, any Redemption Price), and provided that if there are insufficient funds to pay all such 
amounts, they shall be paid on a pro rata basis; 
(c) Third, from the Construction Account on the da te of each Construction Period Draw, upon receipt 
of the documents set forth under "—Conditions Precedent to all Construction Draws," the amounts set forth in 
respect of such Construction Period Draw to the D&C Contractor, any amounts due to the D&C Contractor pursuant 
to the D&C Contract, as set forth in the LTA's certificate;  
(d) Fourth, from the Construction Account (and if any liquidated damages are due and owing by the 
D&C Contractor to the Borrower, from the Construction Liquidity Reserve Account) to the Operating Account, the 
amount of Project Costs (without double counting) certified by the Borrower as  being payable in accordance with 
the Finance Documents, which funds shall thereafter be used only for payment of such Project Costs;  
(e) Fifth, upon achievement of Project Final Completion: 
(i) provided no Event of Default has occurred and is continuing, to transfer from the 
Construction Liquidity Reserve Account to the Cons truction Account, the amount required to fund the D&C 
Contractor Final Completion Payment; 
(ii) thereafter, provided no Event of Default has occurred and is continuing, to transfer from 
the Construction Account to the Operating Account the amount required under "—Project Accounts—Description of 
Project Accounts—Operating Account"; and 
(iii) thereafter, provided no Event of Default has occurred and is continuing, to transfer to the 
Revenue Account the balance, if any, of all cash and Qualified Investments then held in: 
(A) the Construction Account (after applica tion of paragraphs (c) and (e)(i) above); 
(B) the Construction Liquidity Reserve Account;  
(C) the Equity Contribution Sub-Account; and 
(D) the Equity Commitment Account.  
Conditions Precedent to all Construction Draws. The Borrower will be entitled to only one (1) Construction 
Period Draw on each Monthly Transfer Da te in accordance with th e Financial Model with the exception of (a) the 
first month when two (2) draws are allowed (on the Financial Closing Date and at the end of that month) and (b) the 
last draw, if applicable.

Long Stop Deadline;

Documents and the Project Documents to  which the Borrower is a party are true and correct in all material 
respects (other than any representations and warranties that are given as of a specific date);

District 
 The District 
 The District of Columbia was created in 1791 by act of Congress (and Presidential proclamation ) and has 
served as the capital of the United States of America since 1800. Under Ar ticle I, Section 8 of the United States 
Constitution, Congress has exclusive le gislative authority over the District as the Nation’s Cap ital. Since January 2, 
1975, the District has been governed in accordance with the Home Rule Act. Under the Home Rule Act, the District 
is governed by an elected Mayor and elected Council. With limit ed exceptions the District may not obligate or 
expend funds absent annual Congressional appropriation. 
 The D istrict is a unique governmental entity, combining state, county, an d munic ipal characteristics. 
Functions performed by the District government in clude public safety, police, fire, corrections, consumer and 
business regulatory affairs, public works (highwa ys, streets and traffic control, and sanitation), human services 
(health, w elfare, and employment assistance), leisure services (recreation and libraries), economic development 
(planning, zoning, urban renewal, and housing), public education, and general administration. 
 Notwithstanding the Home Rule Act’s delegation to the District of authority for self -government, Congress 
reserves the right to e xercise its Constitutional authority as the legislature for  the District by enacting legislation on 
any subject, whether within or without the scope of legislative power granted to the C ouncil by the Home Rule Act, 
including legislation to amend or repeal any law in force in the District prior to or after enactment of the Home Rule 
Act and any act passed by the Counc il. Such legislative authority is subject to Constitutional limitations o n the 
powers of the United States government. 
The Home Rule Act provides, with exceptions for emergency legislation, acts authori zing general 
obligation revenue anticipation notes a nd acts authorizing the renewal or refunding of bond anticipation no tes, that 
no act passed by the Council and approved either by the Mayor or t hrough veto override by the Council shall take 
effect until the expiration of a period of thir ty (30) legislative days (for acts on civil matters) or sixty ( 60) legislative 
days (for acts on criminal matters) after transmittal to Congress.  During such periods, Congress and the President 
may disapprove an act of the Council by enacting a joint resolution of C ongress approved by the President, in which 
event the act will not become effective.  Congress, from time to time, at the request of the District, has enacted 
legislation waiving the legislative layover period for certain District legislation.   
126 
Disapproval of an act of the Council by Congress has occurred infrequently.  Congress, however, has made 
revisions to the District’s budget as adopted by the Council and generally has conditioned its approval of the 
District’s budget on compliance by the District with a variety of Congressional mandates. 
District of Columbia Office of Public-Private Partnerships 
 T he OP3 is charged with building collaborations between the private sector and District government to 
complete major infrastructure projects an d other progr ams t hrough long-term, performa nce-based procurements. 
OP3 was established p ursuant to the Public -Private Partnership Act of 2014 to expand the utiliz ation of public -
private partnerships in the District by streamlining the procurement pr ocess, serving a s an in-house consultant and 
liaison between District agencies and private sect or partners. OP3 's mission is to deliver infrastructure and other 
government services sought by District residents with the best value -for-money while also exp anding economic 
opportunities for the District. 
The District Department of Transportation 
 DDOT was created pursuant to the Department of Transportation Establishment Act of 2002, which 
became effective on May 21 , 2002. Prior to DD OT’s creation, many of its functions w ere performed by th e 
District’s Department of Public Works. DDOT is an agency within the executive branch of the government of the 
District and works to improve the District’s economic competitiveness and quality of life by planning, coordinating, 
and operating the transpo rtation system, and m anaging and maintaining the transportation infrastructure, to ensure 
the safe, efficient movement of people, goods, and information along public -rights-of-way (D.C. Law 14-134; D.C. 
Official Code § 50- 921.01). In addit ion to numerous a ctions taken in furtherance of its mission, DDOT has worked 
closely with the Federal Highway Administration ( "FHWA") over the years on many federal -aid highway 
endeavors. 
Office of the Chief Technology Officer 
OCTO is the central  technology organization of  t he District of Columbia Government. OCTO develops, 
implements, and maintains the District' s technology infrastructure; develops and  i mplements major e nterprise 
applications; establis hes and oversees technology policies and stan dards for the District; pro vides technology 
services and support for Distri ct agencies, and develops technology solutions to improve services to businesses, 
residents and visitors in all areas of District government.

Plenary Americas  
Plenary Group  ( Canada) Ltd. ("Plenary Grou p") was established i n 2005 in North Amer ica with offices 
across Canada, as well as in Los Angeles, Tampa and Denv er, including a number of site offices that man age the 
construction and operation of its concessions.   
Plenary Group was acquired by Caisse de depot et placement du Quebec (" CDPQ") in March 2020. CDPQ 
is a long -term institutional investor that manages funds primarily for public and parapublic pension and insurances 
plans. As of December 31, 2021,  CDPQ held $ 420 billion CAD in net assets. At acquisition by CDPQ, Plenary  
Group commended doing business under the name "Plenary Americas."  
Plenary Americas has extensive e xperience in the development, financial structu ring and long term asset 
management of P3 infrastructure in North America and generally.  It is currently a l eading sponsor in North America 
and Australia, with over $35 billion in public private partnership transactions completed since 2005. 
Plenary A mericas specializes in the delivery of publ ic infrastructur e through public -private partnership 
arrangements.  Taking the role of developer, financier, owner and manager, Plenary Americas provid es dedicated 
leadership through all cycles of a project  – deal development, financing, construction and o perations management.  
This ensures continuity of knowledge, resulting in the effective resolution of issues and focused management o f key   
127 
relationships.  Importantly, this principal role covers both the de velopment and long term concession period, wit h

ADVISOR REPORTS 
Technical Advisor Report 
The LTA provides cost mana gement, project monitoring and P3 advisory services.  BTY has expertise in 
conducting due diligence on large sc ale capital projects across global geographie s and ma rket sectors.  B TY 
provides planning, construction and d evelopment services through its offi ces across Canada, the U.S . and Europe.  
BTY provides support i n capital investment to clients in the health ca re, public services, ed ucation, research, 
transportation, data and te lecommunications, leisure, retail, resid ential and com mercial sectors.  BTY has a staff 
complement of over 150, including 100 professionals and technical staff. 
Most recently, BTY has served as the lenders' technical advisor for several significant North American P3s.  
Examples of P3 projects in  the U.S. for which BTY served on the preferred proponent’s team include: LaGuardia 
Central Terminal Building Replacement , New York, New York;  SH288 Project, Houston, T exas; Pennsylvania 
Bridges Repla cement Project, Various, Pennsylvania; UC Merced 2020 Project, Merced, California; I 4 Ultim ate 
Project, Orlando, Florida; Long Beach Civic Center Project, Long Beach, California; and US36 Project, Broomfield, 
Colorado. 
General.  The Technical Advisor Report includes an a ssessment of the capabilities and experience of  the 
Borrower and it s sponsors, the D&C C ontractor, its  members, and the Asset Management C ontractor.  It a lso 
provides a commercial commentary on provisions of the Project Agreement that is focused solely on potential areas 
of technical risk and th eir mitigation, as well as si milar technical and commercial commentary on the provisions of 
the D&C Contract and the Asset Management Contract. BTY reviews and assesses the permits and approvals for the 
Project and the Borrower’s plans to acquire those permits and to address any existing site conditions.  It also reviews 
the proposed designs, the projected schedule, the projected capital costs, and the projected  asset management costs 
of the Project prepared by the Bor rower, collectively the D&C Work  and Asset Management Work. This Technical 
Advisor Report summary should be read in full with the Technical Advisor Report.    
Borrower.  BTY assessed  the Borrower's team cred entials and ability to undertake the Project, including 
individual member's relevant project experience.  BT Y concluded that the Borrower team members have extensive 
experience in  this role on numerous North Am erican P3 projects. P lenary and KDC  (through t hemselves and 
affiliates) specifically have a track record of working on successful P3 projects t ogether including the Metrolinx 
East Rail Maintenance Facility, SH 183 Managed Lanes and W aterloo Light Rapid Transit Project. Plenary and  the 
Asset Management Contractor have worked on numerous pur suits together, and are curr ently delivering the Library 
and Archives Canada’s Gatineau 2 Project together. 
Project Agreement.  BTY reviewed the Project Agreement, specifically provisions relating to the contract 
period, conditions precedent, access and grant of right, review of subm ittals, governmental approvals , utilities, 
hazardous materials, scope of design and construction, change procedures, self-monitoring provisions, compensation 
events, relief events, payments , handback provisions, representations and warranties, default and termination terms 
and processes and dispute resolution.  BTY co ncluded that such provisions are standard, appropriate and in-line with 
similar agreements in the United States. 
Preferred Propon ent Agreement.  BTY reviewed a draft of the D&C Contract specifically: 
• the D&C Contractor's back-to-back design and construction obligations,  
• the D&C Contractor's responsibility for the performance of the D&C Work,  
• the D&C Contractor's performance security obligations and maximum liability provisions,  
• scope of construction, site responsibilities and site conditions,  
• the D&C Contractor's obligations to obtain all permits and approvals,    
 
133 
 
• supervision and reporting requirements,  
• the D&C Contractor's obligations in the event of a delay,  
• the D&C Contractor's obligations to complete the D&C Work,  
• liquidated damages, contract price payment mechanisms, and 
• compensation and relief events and dispute resolution provisions.  
BTY concluded that such provisions are standard, appropr iate and in -line with simila r agreements in the 
United States. 
BTY’s due dilig ence includ ed the review of the  Asset Management Contract , specific ally regarding the 
Asset Management Servi ces, provi sions relating to Asset Management Cont ractor, the Asset  Management 
Contractor’s responsibility for the perfo rmance of the services, the Asset Ma nagement Contractor's performance 
security obligations, hazardous materials, the  maintenance requirements, procedures relating to managing,  reporting 
and implementing Renewal Work, step -in provisions, invoicing structure  and payment arrangements, compensation 
and relief events, H andback Requirements, default terms and processes and di spute resolution prov isions.  B TY 
concluded that such provisions are standard, appropriate and in-line with similar agreements in the United States. 
Regulatory Approvals.  BTY concluded that the following conclusions regarding regulatory approvals: 
• The Borrower’s risk related to regulatory approvals has been pass ed through t o the D&C Contrac tor 
through the Preferred Proponent Agreement;  
• The District shall grant the Borrower access to the Project Site on the Commercial Closing Date; 
• The Borrower acknowledges that the District holds acc ess rights to observe and inspect the Work and 
monitor compliance with obligations under the Project Ag reement in a manne r that does not interfere 
with the Borrower’s Work or health and safety policies and procedures; 
• Utility Owners and third parties are res ponsible for applying for  permits to modify or add attachments 
or fixtures to one or more P oles throu ghout the Term, as  requested by the relevant Governm ental 
Authority or Licensor; 
• The risk related to obtaining all Governmental Approvals is transferred to  the Borrower, except for  
District-Provided Approvals, which are defined as the NEPA Document; 
• The Borrower must adhere to the zoning classifications outlined in the Technical Provisions; 
• Regulatory approval requirements are consistent with  similar projects in the United States an d pose no 
significant challenge to the Borrower; and 
• The Borrower’s risk r egarding permitting obligations is considere d to be low. The Project has limited 
permitting obligations compared to similar projects across No rth America. The majority of the 
permitting obligations are completed at early stages leading up to NTP3 an d not c ritical to the 
schedule. The  District has outlined a rapid review process for the review of permits and design 
submission. 
Design Review.  B TY concluded as follows:  
• The technical stan dards are reas onable in natur e. No onerous requirements were iden tified, and the 
D&C Contractor is comfortable with the requirements for the Proje ct. The proposed design solution is   
 
134 
 
reasonable, utilizes well unders tood means and methods and does not inclu de any new or  u nproven 
approaches;  
• The design solution does not include any Alternative Technical Concepts;  
• The desi gn requirements for the compo nents are market, utilizing well understood technologies and 
installations pract ices. The material component s pecifications are transparent  and prescriptive, with 
catalog-like specification sheets. The D istrict has provided clear design expectations that have enabled 
the Borrower to adequately progress the design for this stage of procurement. T he suggested 
components outline d in the specification included reputable brands, which are sourced from well-
known manufacturers; 
• The review periods ascribed in the  Project Agreement are rapid compared to similar periods seen in 
other governmental approval processes. We consider the rev iew periods to be a major mitigant, as to 
reduces the design  approvals durations but it also dem onstrates the D istrict’s will to accelerate the 
Project schedules; and 
• The submittal approval process is clearly establis hed under t he Technical Provisions. BTY considers 
the approval process to be in line with North American P3 projects. 
Schedule Review.  B ased on review of  a then-current version of the construction schedule, BTY outlines 
the following salient conclusions:  
• The Long Sto p Deadline is considered s hort at six ( 6)-months d uration compared with rec ent P3 
projects in North Am erica, where we typically se e twelve (12)-month longstop date. However, given 
the (i) shorter construction schedule relative to other P3 projects, and (ii) th e absence of a critical path 
after the ach ievement of t he first NTP3 a nd the D&C Contractor’s ability to accel erate the schedule, 
and (iii) the simplicity of the D&C Work, based on the schedule requirements outlined in the Technical 
Provisions, the 6-month longstop date is appropriate;  
• The substantial completion and f inal c ompletion framework outlined in the  P roject Agreement are 
considered to be reasonable and typical for North American P3 projects;  
• The D&C Contractor has demonstrated a good understanding of the requir ements and has proposed a 
suitable schedule for this stage of the Project; and 
• Given the s imple complexity of the Work and the flexibil ity of the D&C Contractor to adjust 
productivity, we  consider the schedule risks to be minimal. The Project Baseline Schedule is  w ell 
structured with well implied logic, based on a review of the PDF submit with the Borrower’s Technical 
submission and through discussion with the D&C Contractor. We consider the Project can be delivered 
within the timeframe allowed by the D&C Contractor.

Nevertheless, such a failure must be r eported in accordance with Rule 15c2- 12 and must be considered by any 
broker, dealer or mu nicipal securities dealer before recommending the purchase or sale of the Series 20 22 Bonds in 
the s econdary market.  Consequently, such a failure may ad versely affect the transferability a nd liquidity o f the 
Series 2022 Bonds and their market price.

Income Tax Bonds.   The notice was filed on  June 8, 2021, but was due to be filed no later than April 8, 2021.  The 
foregoing description of an instance of non- compliance by the District with its con tinuing disclosure undertakings 
should not be construed as an acknowledgement by the District that such instance was material.

LEGAL INVESTMENT IN DISTRICT OBLIGATIONS 
Section 486 of th e Home Rule Act  (D.C. Of ficial Code § 1 -204.86) provides that, notwithstanding any 
restriction o n the investment of funds by fiduciaries contained in any  other Dis trict of Columbia  law, domestic 
insurance companies, d omestic insurance associations, ex ecutors, administrators, guar dians, trustees, and other 
fiduciaries within the District of Co lumbia may legally invest any sinking funds, moneys, trust funds, or  other funds 
belonging to them or under or within their contr ol in any bonds issued in accordance wi th the Home Rule Act.  
Section 486 of the Home Rule Act also provides that all federal b uilding and loan associations and federal savings 
and loan associations and b anks, trust companies, building and loan association s and savings and loan association s, 
domiciled in the District of Columbia, may purchase, sell, underwrite, and deal in, for th eir own account or for the 
account of others, all bonds or notes issued in accordance with the Home Rule Act.

TAX M
ATTERS 
Series 2022A Bonds 
In the opinion of O rrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP, Bond Counsel to  the Bond Issuer ("Bond Counsel"), 
based upon an analysis of existing laws, regulations, rulings and court decisions, and assuming, among o ther 
matters, the accuracy of certain representations and compl iance with certain covenants, interest on the Series 2 022A 
Bonds is excluded from gross income for federal income tax purposes under Section 103 of the Code, except that no 
opinion is expressed  as to the status of interest on any Series 2022A Bond for any peri od that such Series  2022A 
Bond is held by a " substantial user" of the facilities financed or refinanced by the Series 2022A Bonds or by a 
"related person" within the meaning of Sec tion 147(a) of the Code.  Bond Counsel observes, however, that interest 
on t he Series 2022A Bon ds is a specific preference item for purposes of the federal alternative minimum tax.  
Interest on the Series 2022A Bonds is exempt from District of Columbia taxation, ex cept estate, inheritance and gift 
taxes.  Bond Counsel expresse s no  opinion regarding any other tax consequences related to  the owne rship or 
disposition of, or the amount, accrual or receipt of interest on the Series 2022A Bonds.  A complete copy of the 
proposed form of the opinion of Bond Counsel is set forth in Appendix H hereto. 
To the extent the issue price of any maturity  of the Series 2022A B onds is less than the amount to be paid 
at maturity of such Series 2022A Bonds (excluding amounts stat ed to be interest and payable at least annually over 
the term of such Se ries 2022A Bonds), the difference constitutes " original issue discount," the accrual of which, to 
the extent properly allocable to each beneficial owner thereof, is treated as interest  on the S eries 2022A Bonds   
140 
 
which is excluded from gross income for federal income tax purposes a nd exempt from District of Columbia 
taxation, except estate, inheritance and gift taxes.  For this purpose, the issue price of a particular maturity of the 
Series 2022A Bonds is the first price at which a substantial amount of such maturity of the Series 2 022A Bonds is 
sold to the publ ic (ex cluding bond houses, b rokers, or similar persons or organizations acting in the capacity of 
underwriters, placement agents o r wholesa lers).  The original issue discount with respect to any maturity of  the 
Series 2022A B onds accrues daily over the ter m to maturity of such Series 2022A Bonds on the basis of a constant 
interest rate compounded semiannually (with straight- line inte rpolations between compounding dates).  The 
accruing original issue discount is added to the adjusted basis of such Series 2022A Bonds to determine taxable gain 
or loss upon disposition (including sale, redemption, or payment on maturity) of such Series 2022A Bonds .  
Beneficial owners of the Series 2022A Bonds should consult t heir own tax advisors w ith respect to the tax 
consequences o f ownersh ip of Series 2022A Bonds with original issue discount, including the treatment of 
beneficial owners who do not purc hase such Series 2022A Bonds in the original offering to the public at the f irst 
price at which a substantial amount of such Series 2022A Bonds is sold to the public. 
Series 2022A Bonds purchased, whether at original issuance or otherwise, for an amount hi gher than their 
principal amount payable at maturity (or, in some cases,  at their earlier call date) ("Premium Bonds") will be treated 
as having amortizable bond premium.  No deduction is allowable for the amortizable bond premium in the case of 
bonds, lik e the Premi um Bonds, the interest on which is excluded from gross income  for federal income tax  
purposes.  However, the amount of tax-exempt interest received, and a beneficial owner’s basis in a Premium Bond, 
will be reduced by the amount of amortizable bond premium properly allocable to such beneficial owner.  Beneficial 
owners of Premium Bonds should consult their own tax advi sors with respect to the pro per treatment of amortizable 
bond premium in their particular circumstances. 
The Code imposes various restrictions, conditions and requirements relating to the exclusion from  gross 
income for federal income tax purposes of interest on obligations such as the Series 2022A Bonds.  The Bond Issuer 
and the Borrower have made certain representations and covenan ted to co mply with certain restrictions, conditions 
and requirements des igned to ensure that in terest on the Series 2022A Bonds will not be i ncluded in fe deral gross 
income.  Inaccuracy of these representations or failure to comply with these covenants may  result in interest on the 
Series 2022A Bonds being included in gross in come for federal income tax purposes, possibly from th e date of 
original issuance of the Series 2022A Bonds.  The opinion of Bond Counsel assumes the accuracy of these 
representations and compliance with these covenants.  Bond Counsel has not undertaken to  determine (or to infor m 
any person) whether any actio ns taken (or not taken), or events occurring (or not occurring), or any other matters 
coming to Bond Counsel’s attention after the  date of is suance of the Series 2022A Bonds may adversely affect the 
value of, or the tax statu s of interest on, the Series 20 22A Bonds.  Acc ordingly, the opinion of Bond Counsel is not 
intended to, and may not, be relied upon in connection with any such actions, events or matters. 
Although Bond Counsel is of the opinion that inte rest on the Series 2022A Bonds is excluded from gross 
income for federal incom e tax purposes and that interest on the Series 2022A Bonds is exempt from District of 
Columbia taxation, except estate, inheritance and gift taxes, the ownership or disposition o f, or the accrual or receipt 
of amounts treated as interest on, the Series 2022A Bonds may otherwise affect a beneficial owner’s federal, state or 
local tax liability.  The nature and exten t of these other tax consequences depends upon the particular t ax status of 
the benefi cial owner or the beneficial ow ner’s other ite ms of income or deduction.  Bond Counsel expresses no 
opinion regarding any such other tax consequences. 
Current and future legislative proposals, if enacted into law, clarification of th e Co de or court decisio ns 
may cause interest on the Series 2 022A Bonds to be subje ct, directly or indirectly, in whole or in part, to federal 
income taxation or to be subject to or exempted from state income taxation, or otherwise prevent beneficial owners  
from realizing the ful l current benefit of the tax st atus of such interest.  The introduction or enactment of any such 
legislative proposals or clarification of the Code or court decisions may  also affect, perhaps significantly, the market 
price for, or m arketability of, the Se ries 2022A Bonds.   Prospective purchasers of t he Series 202 2A Bonds should 
consult their own tax advisors regarding the potential impact of any pending or propos ed federal or state tax 
legislation, regulations or litigation, as to which Bond Counsel expresses no opinion. 
The opinion of Bond C ounsel is  based on cur rent legal authority, covers certain matters not directly 
addressed by such authorities, and represent s Bond Co unsel’s judgment as to the proper treatment of the Series 
2022A Bonds for federal inco me tax purposes.  It is not bin ding on the Int ernal Revenue Service ( "IRS") or the   
141 
 
courts.  Furthermore, Bond Counsel cannot give and has not given any opinion o r assuran ce about the future 
activities of the Bond Issuer or the Borrow er or about the effect o f future changes in the Code, the ap plicable 
regulations, the interpretation thereof or the enforcement thereof by the IRS.  The Bond Issuer and the Borrower 
have covenanted, however, to comply with the requirements of the Code. 
The IRS has an ongoing pro gram of auditing tax -exempt obligations to det ermine whether, in the view of 
the IRS, interest on such tax- exempt obligations is includable in the gross income o f the owners thereof for federal 
income tax purposes.  Bond Counsel is n ot obligated to defend the owners regarding the tax- exempt status of the 
Series 2022A Bonds in the event of an audit examination by the IRS.  Under current procedures, beneficial owner s 
would have little, if any, right to participate in the audit examination p rocess.  Moreover, b ecause achieving judicial 
review in connection with an audit examination of tax-exempt bonds is difficult, obtaining an independent review of 
IRS positions with which the Bond Issuer or the Borrower legitimately disagrees may not be practicable.  Any action 
of the IRS, including but not limited to selection of the Series 2022A Bonds for audit, or the course or result of such 
audit, or an audit of bonds presenting  similar tax issues may affect the market price for, or the marketabilit y of, the 
Series 2022A B onds, and may cause the Bond Issuer,  the Borr ower or the b eneficial owners to incur significant 
expense. 
Payments on the Series 2022A Bonds generally will be su bject to U.S. information reporting and  possibly 
to "backup withholding. " Under Section 3406 of  the Code and applicable U.S. T reasury Regulations issued 
thereunder, a non- corporate beneficial owner of Series 2022A Bonds may be subject to backup withholding  with 
respect to "reportable payments," which include interest paid on the Series 2022A Bonds and the gross proceeds of a 
sale, exchange, redemption, retirement or other disposition of the Series 2022A Bonds. The payor will be required to 
deduct and withho ld the pr escribed amounts if (i) the payee fails to furnish a U.S. taxpa yer identification number 
("TIN") to the payor in the manner  required, (ii) the IRS notifies the payor that the TIN furnished by the payee is 
incorrect, (iii) there has been a "notified payee underreporting" described in Section 3406(c) of the Code or (iv)  the 
payee fails to cer tify under penalty of perjury t hat th e payee i s not subject  to withholding under  Section 
3406(a)(1)(C) of the Code. Amounts withheld under the backup withholding  rules ma y be refunded or credited 
against a beneficial owner’s federal inco me tax liability, if any, provided that  the required inf ormation is timely 
furnished to the IRS. Certain beneficial owners (including  among others, corporations and certain tax -exempt 
organizations) are not subject to backup  withholding. The failure to  comply with the backup  withholding rules may 
result in the imposition of penalties by the IRS. 
Series 2022B Bonds 
In the opinion of Bond Counsel, based upon an analysis of existing laws, regul ations, rulings and court 
decisions, and assuming, among other matt ers, the accuracy of certain representations and complia nce with certain 
covenants, interest on the Series 2022B Bonds is exempt from District of Columbia taxation, except estate, 
inheritance and gift taxes.  Bond Counsel observes that interest on the Series 2022B Bonds is not excluded from 
gross income for federal income tax purposes under Section 103 of the Code.  Bond Counsel expresses no opinion 
regarding any other tax consequences relat ed to the ownership or disposition of, or the amount, accrual or receipt of 
interest on the Series 2022B Bonds.  A complete  copy of the proposed form of the opinion of Bond Counsel is set 
forth in Appendix H hereto. 
The following discussion summar izes cert ain U.S. federal income tax considerations generally applicable 
to holders of the Ser ies 2022B Bonds that acquire the ir Series 2022B Bonds in the initial offering.  The discussion 
below is based upon laws, regulations, rulings, and decisions in ef fect and available on the date hereof, all of which 
are subject to change, p ossibly with retroactive effect.  Prospective investors should note that no ru lings have been 
or are expected to be sought from the IRS with respect to any of the U.S. federal tax consequences discussed below, 
and no assurance can be given that the IRS  will not take contrar y positions.  Further, the follo wing discussion does 
not deal with U.S. tax consequences applicable to any given investor, nor does it address the U.S. tax consid erations 
applicable to all categories of investors, some of which may be subject to special ta xing rules (regardless of whether 
or n ot such investors cons titute U.S. Holders), such as certain U.S. expatriates, banks, REITs, RICs, insurance 
companies, tax -exempt org anizations, dealers or traders in securities or currencies, par tnerships, S corporati ons, 
estates and trusts, investo rs tha t hold th eir Series 20 22B Bonds as part of a hedge, straddle or an integrated or 
conversion transaction, investors whose " functional currency" is not the U.S. dollar,  or certain taxpayers that are 
required to prepare cer tified financial statements or file financial statements with certain regulatory or governmental   
142 
 
agencies. Furthermore, it does not address (i) alternative mini mum tax consequences, (ii) the net investment income 
tax imposed under S ection 1411 of the Code, or (iii) the indirect effects on persons who hold equity interests in a 
holder.  This summary also does not consider the taxation of the Series 2022B Bonds under state,  local or non-U.S. 
tax laws.  In addition, this summary general ly i s limited to U.S. tax considerations applicable to  investors that 
acquire their Series 2022B Bonds pursuant to this offering for the issue price that is applicable to such Series 2 022B 
Bonds (i.e., the price at which a substantial amount of the Series 2022B Bonds are sold t o the public) and who will 
hold their Series 2022B Bonds as "capital assets" within the meaning of Section 1221 of the Code.   
As used herein, "U.S. Holder " means a benefi cial owner of a Series 2022B Bond that for U.S. federal 
income tax purposes is an ind ividual citizen or resident of the Uni ted States, a corporation or other entity taxable as 
a corporation created or organized in or under the laws of the United S tates or any state thereof (including the 
District of Columbia), an esta te the income of which  i s subject to U.S. federal income tax ation reg ardless of it s 
source or a trust where a court within the United States is able to exercise primary supervision over the 
administration of the trust and one or more United States persons (as defined in the Cod e) have the authority to 
control all substantial decisions of the trust (or a trust that has made a valid election under U.S. Treasury Regulations 
to be treated as a domes tic trust). As used herein, "Non-U.S. Holder" generally means a beneficial owner of a Series 
2022B Bond (other than a  partnership) that is not a U.S. Holder.  If a partnership holds Series 2022B Bonds, the tax 
treatment of such partnership or a partner in su ch partnership generally will depend upon the status of the p artner 
and upon the ac tivities of the partnership.  Pa rtnerships holding Series 2022B Bonds, and partners in such 
partnerships, should consult their own tax advisors regarding the tax consequences of an investment in the Series 
2022B Bonds (including their status as U.S. Holders or Non-U.S. Holders). 
Prospective investors should consult their own tax advisors in determining the U.S. federal, state, local or 
non-U.S. tax consequences to them from the purchase, ownership and disposition of the Series 2022B Bonds in light 
of their particular circumstances. 
U.S. Holders 
Interest.  Interest on the Series 2022B Bonds generally will be taxable to a U.S. Holder as ordinary interest 
income at the  time suc h amounts are accrued or received, in accordance with the U.S. Hold er’s method of 
accounting for U.S. federal income tax purposes. 
To the extent that the issue price of any maturity of the Series 2022B Bonds is less than the amount to  be 
paid at maturity o f such Series 2022B Bonds (excluding amounts stated to be inter est and payable at leas t annually 
over the term of such Seri es 2022B Bonds) by mor e than a de minimis  amount, the difference may constitute 
original issue discount ("OID").  U.S. Holders of Series 2022B Bonds will be required to include OID in income for 
U.S. federal income tax purposes as it accrues, in acco rdance with a constant yield method based on a compounding 
of interest (which may be before the receipt of cash payments attributable to su ch income).  Under this method, U.S. 
Holders generally will be requ ired to include in i ncome increasingly greater amo unts of OID in successive accrual 
periods. 
Series 2022B Bonds purchased for an amount in excess of the principal amount payable at  maturity (or, in 
some cases, at their earlier call date) will be treated as  issued at a premium.  A U.S. Holder of a Series 2 022B Bond 
issued at a premium may make an election, applicable to all debt securities purchased at a premium by such U.S. 
Holder, to amortize such premium, using a constant yield method over the term of such Series 2022B Bond. 
Sale or Other Taxable Disposi tion of the Series 2022B Bon ds.  Unless a nonrecognition provision of the 
Code applies, the sale, exchange, redemption, retirement (including pursuant to an offer by the Bond Issuer) or other 
disposition of a Series 2022B Bon d will be a taxable event for U .S. federal income tax purposes.  In such event, in 
general, a U.S. Holder of a Series 2022B Bond will recognize gain  or loss equa l to the difference between (i) the 
amount of cash plus the fair market value of property recei ved (except to the extent attri butable to accr ued but 
unpaid interest on the Series 2022B Bond, which will be taxed in the manner described above) and (ii) the U .S. 
Holder’s adjusted U.S. federal income tax basis in the Series 2022B Bond  (generally, the purchase price paid by the 
U.S. Holder for the Series 2022B B ond, decreased by any amortized premium, and increased by the amount of any 
OID previously included in income by such U.S. Holder with respect to such Series 2022B Bond). Any  such gain or 
loss generally will be capital gain or loss.  In the case of a non-corporate U.S. Holder of the Series 2022B Bonds, the   
143 
 
maximum marginal U.S. federal income tax rate appl icable to  a ny such gain will be lower than the maximum 
marginal U.S. fed eral income tax rate ap plicable to ordinary income if such U.S. holde r’s holding period for the 
Series 2022B Bonds exceeds one year.  The deductibility of capital losses is subject to limitations. 
Defeasance of the Series 2022B Bonds .  If the Bond Issuer d efeases any Series 2022 B Bond, the Series 
2022B Bond may be deemed to be retired for U.S. federal income tax purposes as a result of the defeasance.  In that 
event, in general, a holder will re cognize taxable gain or loss equal to the difference between (i ) the amount realized 
from the deemed sale, exchange o r reti rement (less any accru ed qualified stated interest which will be taxable as 
such) and (ii) the holder’s adjusted U.S. federal income tax basis in the Series 2022B Bond.  
Information Reporting and Backup Withholding.  Payments on the Series 2022B Bonds gene rally will be 
subject to U.S. information reporting and possibly to " backup withholding."  Under Section 3406 of the Code an d 
applicable U.S. Treasury Regulations issued thereunder, a non -corporate U. S. Holder of the Se ries 2022B Bonds 
may be subject  to ba ckup withholding at th e current rate of 24% with respect to " reportable payments, " which 
include interest paid on the Series 2022B Bonds and the gross proceeds of a sale, exchange, redemption, ret irement 
or other dispos ition of the Series 2022B Bonds .  The  payor will be require d to deduct and withhold the prescribed 
amounts if (i) the payee fails to furnish a U.S. taxpayer identification number ("TIN") to the payor in the manner 
required, (ii) the IRS notifies the payor t hat the TIN furnished by the p ayee i s incorre ct, (iii) the re has been a 
"notified payee underreporting " described in Section 3406(c) of the Code or  (iv) the pay ee fails to certify under 
penalty of perjury that the payee is not subject to withholding under  Section 3406(a)(1)(C) of the Code.  Amounts 
withheld under the backup withholding rules may be refunded or credited against the U.S. Holder’s feder al income 
tax liability, if any, provided that the required information is timely fur nished to the IRS.  Cert ain U.S. holders 
(including among others, corporations and certain tax-exempt organizations) are not subject to backup withholding.  
A holder’s failure to comply with the backup withholding rules may result in the imposition of penalties by the IRS. 
Non-U.S. Holders 
Interest.  Subject to the  discussions below under the headings "Information Reporting and Backup 
Withholding" and "Foreign Account Tax C ompliance Act  (" FATCA")—U.S. Holders and Non -U.S. Holders, " 
payments of principal of,  and interest on, any S eries 2022B Bond to a Non -U.S. Holder, other than (1) a con trolled 
foreign corporation described in Section 881(c)(3)(C) of the Code, and (2) a bank  which acquires such Series 2022B 
Bond in consideration of an extension of credit made pursuant to a loan ag reement entered into in the ord inary 
course of business, will not be subject to any U.S. federal withholding tax provided that the beneficial owner of the 
Series 2022B Bond  provides a certification completed in compliance with applic able statutory and regul atory 
requirements, which requ irements are di scussed below  under the heading " Information Reporting and Backup 
Withholding," or an exemption is otherwise established.  
Disposition of the Series 2022B Bonds . Subject to the discussion s below under the headi ngs "Information 
Reporting and Backup  Withholding" and "Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act (" FATCA")—U.S. Holders and 
Non-U.S. Holders, " any gain re alized by a N on-U.S. H older upon the sale, exchange, redemption, retirement 
(including pursuant to an offer b y the Bond Issuer or a deemed retire ment due to defeasance of the Series 2022B 
Bond) or other disposition of a Series 2022B Bond generally will not  be subject t o U.S. fe deral income tax, unless 
(i) such gain is effectively connected  with the conduct by suc h Non-U.S. Holder of a trade o r business within the 
United States; or (ii) in the case of any gain realized by an individual Non -U.S. Holder, such holder is pre sent in the 
United States for 183 days or more in the taxable year of su ch sale, exchange, redem ption, retirement (including 
pursuant to an offer by the Bond Issuer) or other disposition and certain other conditions are met. 
Information Report ing and Backu p Withhol ding. Subject to the discussion below under the heading 
"Foreign Ac count Tax Complianc e Act ( "FATCA")—U.S. Holders an d Non -U.S. Hold ers," under current U.S. 
Treasury Regulations, payments of principal and interest on any Series 2022B Bonds to a holder that i s not a United 
States person will not be subject to any backu p withholding tax requi rements if the beneficial owner  of the Series 
2022B Bond or  a financial institution holding the Series 2022B Bond on behalf of the beneficial owner in the 
ordinary course of its trade or business provides an appropriate certification  to the payor and the payor does not have 
actual knowledge that the certification is false.  If a beneficial owner provides the certification, the certification must 
give the name and address o f such owner, state that such owner is not a United States pers on, or, in the case of a n   
144 
 
individual, that such owner is nei ther a citizen nor a r esident of the United States, and the owner must sign the 
certificate under penalties of perjury.  The current backup withholding tax rate is 24%. 
Foreign Account Tax Compliance Act ("FATCA")—U.S. Holders and Non-U.S. Holders   
Sections 1471 through 1474 of the Code impose a 30% withholding tax on certain types of payments made 
to foreign fina ncial institu tions, unle ss the foreign financial institution enters into an agreement with the U.S. 
Treasury to, among other things, undert ake to identify accounts held by certain U.S. persons or U.S. -owned entities, 
annually report certain information abo ut such accou nts, and withhold 30% on payments to account holders whose 
actions prevent i t from complying wit h these and other reporting re quirements, or unless the fo reign financial 
institution is otherwise exempt from those requirements.  In addition, FATCA imposes a 30% withholding tax on the 
same types of payments to a non -financial fo reign entity unless the  entity certifies that it does not ha ve any 
substantial U.S. owners or the entity furnishes identifying information regarding each substantial U.S. owner.  Under  
current guidance, failure to comply with the additional certification, information reporting a nd other specified 
requirements imposed under FATCA could result in the 30% withholding tax being imposed on payments of interest 
on the Bonds.  In general, with holding und er FATCA currently applies to payments of U.S. source interes t 
(including OID) and, u nder current guidance, will ap ply to certain "passthru" payments no earlier than the date that 
is two years after publication of final U.S. Treasur y Regulations defining t he term "foreign passthru payments. "  
Prospective investors should consult their own tax advisors regarding FATCA and its effect on them.  
The foregoing summary is included herein for general information only and does not discuss all aspects of U.S. 
federal taxation that may be relevant to a particular holder of Ser ies 2022B Bonds in ligh t of the holder’s particular 
circumstances and income tax situation.  Prospective investors are urged to consult their own tax advisors as to any 
tax consequence s to them  f rom the purchase, ownership and disposition of Series 2022B B onds, including the 
application and effect of state, local, non-U.S., and other tax laws. 
RATING 
The Series 2022 Bonds have been as signed an initial rating of "A3" ( with a stable outlook)  by M oody's.  
The delivery of an equivalent final rating from such rating agency is a condi tion to the issuance of the Series 2022 
Bonds.  T he rating of Moody's  reflects only th e views of such organization and an y desired ex planation of the 
significance of such rating sho uld be obtained from Moody's at the following address: Moody's Investors Se rvice, 
Inc., 7 Work Trade Cent er, New York, NY 10 007.  Generally, a rating ag ency bases its rating on inform ation and 
materials furnished to it and on investigations, studies and as sumptions by such rating agency.  A rating is not a 
recommendation to buy, sell or hold the Series 2022 Bonds.  There is no assurance that such rating will continue for 
any given period of time or will not be revised downward, suspended or withdrawn entirely by the rating agency, if, 
in its judgment, circumstances so warrant.  Any such lowering, suspension or withdrawal of the rating might have an 
adverse effect upon the market price o r marketability of the Series 2022 Bonds.  The Underwriters, the Bond Issuer 
and the Borrower undertake no responsibility after the issuance of the Series 2022 Bonds to assure the maintenance 
of the rating or to oppose any revision or withdrawal thereof.

"Determination of Taxability" means the occurrence of any of the following: (a) either the Bond Trustee or 
the Bond Issuer has received writ ten notice from the Borrower, supported by an opinion of nationally recognized 
bond counsel, that an Event of Taxability has occurred, (b) either the Bond Truste e or the Bond Issuer has r eceived 
written notice that the Commissioner of Internal Revenue or a ny District Director of Internal Revenue has issued a 
statutory notic e of deficiency, or document of substantia lly similar import due to th e occurrence of an Event of  
Taxability, or (c) the Bond Issuer has received written notice from the Bond Trustee that  the Bond Trustee has been 
advised by any Bondholder of the Bonds or  former Bondholder of Bonds that the In ternal Revenue Service has 
assessed as includible in the gross income of such Bondholder the interest on such Bondholder’s Bond or Bonds due 
to the o ccurrence of an Event of Taxability; provided, however, t hat in resp ect of clauses (b) and (c) above, a 
Determination of Taxability shall not be deemed to have o ccurred unless and until the Borrower has been notified of 
the allegation that an Event of Taxa bility has occurred and either (i) the Borrower fails to commence a c ontest of 
such allegation in good faith and by appropriate le gal or administrative proceeding within sixty (60) days f ollowing 
such notification; or (ii) the Borrower does commence such co ntest within such time, but thereafter fails to pursue it 
diligently, in good faith and by appropriate legal or admin istrative proceeding to a final order or judgment by a court 
or administrative body of competent jurisdiction; or (iii) such contest result s in a final order of judgment of a court 
or administrative bod y of competent jurisdiction to the effect that an Event of Tax ability has occurred and the time 
for any appeal of such order or judgment has expired.

"Equity IRR"  means the nominal post-tax internal rate of return on Equity Investment (on a cash- on-cash 
basis) over the full Term calculated using the Base Case Financial Model, at the disc ount rate that, when applied to 
the Distributions gives a net present value equal to the  net present value of the Equity Investment. For the purposes 
of this definition: (a) the phr ase "post-tax" refers only to  U.S. federal, state and local income tax liab ility of the 
Borrower (or, if the Borrower is a pass- through entity for tax purposes, it s Equity Members) and specifically 
excludes (i) any foreign income tax and ot her tax of any k ind, and (ii)  any federal, st ate or local withholding tax, 
including any ta x that the Borrower is obligated to withhold on Distributions (whether actual or constructive) or  
other payments or allocations to Equity Members or holders of debt o f or equity inte rests in an Equity Member 
under 26 U.S.C. §§ 1441 – 1446, despite 26 U.S.C . § 1461; (b) in calculating the Equity IRR, a single level of 
corporate income taxes fo r a regularly taxed, U.S.-organized, domestic C corporation should be taken i nto account; 
and (c) the phrase "cash-on-cash" basis means, with respect to the calculation of a financial return, the calculation of 
such financial return on the basis of cash actually rec eived in relation to cash actually invested (as opposed to cash 
committed).

A-56 
"Refinancing Gain" means an amount equal to the greater of zero and {(A-B)-C}, where: 
A = the net pre sent value using the Base Case Eq uity IRR as the discounting rate of the 
Distributions projected immediately prior to the Refinancing (taking into account the effect of the 
Refinancing (including any breakage costs of any prior refinancing) and using the B ase Case 
Financial Model as updat ed (including as to the p erformance of the Project up to the date of  the 
Refinancing) so as to be current immediately prior to the Refinancing) to be made over t he 
remaining term of the Project Agreement following the Refinancing; 
B = the net present value  using the Base Case Equi ty IRR as the discounting rate of the 
Distributions projected immediately prior to the R efinancing (but without taking into account the 
effect of the Refinancing and using the Base Case Financial Mo del as updated (including as to th e 
performance of the Project up to the date of the Refinancing) so as to be current immediately prior 
to the Ref inancing) to be made over the remaining term of th e Project  Agreement following the 
Refinancing; and 
C = any adjustment required to raise the P re-Refinancing Equity IRR to the Base Case Equity 
IRR (if the Pre-Refinancing Equity IRR is lower than the Base C ase Equity IRR, the adjustment is 
calculated as t he amount that, if received  by Equity Members at the estimate d date of the 
Refinancing, would i ncrease the Pre- Refinancing Equity IRR to be the same as the Base C ase 
Equity IRR).

"Right to Cure" means, within five (5) Business Days of the applicabl e Calculation Date, the Sponsors, or 
any other direct or indirect ow ner of the Borrower, shall have the right to make an y equity contribution to the  
Borrower (which shall be in addition to the Equity Commitment) (the " Cure Right"), and upon the receipt by t he 
Borrower of cash (the " Cure Amount ") pursuant to the exercise of  the Cure Right, the Senior DSCR and/or the 
Projected Senior DSCR shall be recalculated with the effect of a pro forma adjustment of increasing Net Cash Flow, 
solely for the purposes of me asuring the  S enior DSCR and/or the Projected Senior DSCR and not for  any other 
purpose, by an amount equal to the Cure Amount. If, after giving effe ct to the fore going recalculations, the 
Borrower shall then be in compl iance with the Senior DSCR and the Pr ojected Sen ior DSCR requirements, the 
Borrower shall be deemed to have satisfied such requirements as of the relevant d ate of determination with the same 
effect as though there had been no failure to comply therewith at such date, and the applicable breach  or default that 
had occurred shall be deemed cured. 
The Cure Amount  sh all be (at the option of the Borrower) held in t he Revenue Account and form p art of 
the Collateral and be applied in accordance with the Flow of Funds.  
For the avoidance of doubt, the R ight to Cure is available to the Borrower to remedy a Default or Ev ent of 
Default pursuant to clause (o)  of "Event of Default "  in "APPENDIX D—CERTA IN PROVISIONS OF THE 
COLLATERAL AGENCY AGREEMENT—Events of Default and Term ination—Events of Default. "

twithstanding the foregoing, the Borrower shall not be permitted to exe rcise a Cure Right more than

APPENDIX B 
SUMMARY OF CERTAIN PROVISIONS OF THE 
PROJECT AGREEMENT 
between the  
District of Columbia and  
Plenary Infrastructure DC LLC 
The following is a summary of sel ected provisions of t he Project Agreement relating to the Project and is 
not a f ull statement of the terms of  such agreement. Accordingly, the following summary is qualified in its entirety 
by reference to the Project Agreement and is subj ect to the full text of such agreement. A copy of the  P roject 
Agreement is available, free of charge, upon requ est from the Bo rrower or the  Bond Trust ee.  Unless otherwise  
stated, any ref erence in this Official Statement to any agreement mean s such agreement and all schedules, exhibits 
and attachments thereto, as amended, supplemented or otherwise modified and in effect as of the date hereof. 
Certain terms of the Project Agreement will be amended on or prior to the Financial Closing Date and this 
Official Statement sets out the terms of the Project Agreement as they will be in  effect on the Fin ancial Closing 
Date. 
Principal Rights and Responsibilities of the Borrower and the District 
 Concession 
Pursuant to the Project Agreement, the District has granted to the Borrower the exclusive right to, subject to 
the terms and conditions of the Pro ject Agreement, (a) develop, design, construct and finance the Project, and (b) to 
undertake Asset Management Services with respect t o the Project, in  each case, in accordance with the terms of the 
Project Agreement. 
 Term 
The P roject Agreement will take effec t on t he Commercial Clos ing Date and co ntinue until the 15th 
anniversary o f the Commercial Clo sing Date  or the earlier  termination of the P roject Agreement pursuant to the 
terms thereof. 
 Ame ndments In Conjunction with Financial Closing Date 
The Project Agreement will be amended on or before the Financial Closing Date to reflect any adjustments 
or amendments necessary to conform the Project Agreement with amendments to the Base Case Financial Model as 
updated as a result of Financial Close. 
 Governmental Approvals and Utilities 
The Borrower is s olely responsible for securing and obtainin g all governm ental approv als require d in 
connection with its performance under the Project Agreement, except in respect of District-Provided Approvals and, 
in all cases, subject t o the terms of the Project Agreement . With respect to  District-Provided Approvals, which are 
set fort h in the P roject Agreement, the  District is r equired to obt ain t he benefit of eac h such a pproval for the  
Borrower so as to ensure that the Borrower has the use and benefit of such approval no later than the deadlines set  
forth in the Project Agreement. 
However, the Borrower is responsible for obtaining amendments or modifications to any District-Provided 
Approvals necessary t o reflect the Borrower' s Fina l Des ign and/or means and methods should the Final Design 
and/or means and methods deviate from the basis upon which the District-Provided Approvals were initially granted 
by the rel evant Gover nmental Entity. I n the event  th at any modifications are not per mitted by  the G overnmental 
Entity, the Borrower will be responsible, at its own risk of delay and cost, for revising its Final Design or means and 
methods as necessary to satisfy the requirements and conditions of the rel evant Governmental Entity. The Borrower

is required to perform its obliga tions under the P roject Agreement in compliance with all Governmental Approvals 
at all times.

Provisions.  
B-7 
(ii) Each of the following components of th e Improved S treet Light N etwork has completed a 
period of continuous performance of not less than thirty (30) consecutive days: 
 (A) One (1) Street Light Bundle in each Ward of the District; 
(B) with resp ect to e ach type of netwo rk communications technolo gy incorporated 
into the RMCS (includ ing, as appl icable, m esh network, s tar network, and 
cellular network), ten  p ercent ( 10%) of  the t otal numbe r of Ligh ting Units  
controlled by each such network communications technology; 
(C) T en percent (10%) of the tota l number of " Posttop" Lighting Units included 
within the Improved Street Light Network; and 
(D) Ten percent (10%) of the total number of "Cobrahead" Lighting Units included 
within the Improved Street Light Network. 
For purposes of the foregoing, "continuous performance" means that, for a peri od of not 
less than t hirty (30) con secutive days, th e rel evant co mponent of the Im proved S treet 
Light Netwo rk operat es such t hat n o Noncom pliance Points would ac crue if  the 
performance requi rements set  forth in the Tech nical Provisions  and mechanism for 
calculating payments to the Borrower unde r the Project  Agreement were in effect during 
such thirty (30)-day period.

successfully completed for the Improv ed Stre et Light N etwork in accor dance w ith the  
Technical Provisions.  
(iv) All ancillary items including equipment, supplies, spare parts and manuals are in place. 
(v) The Borrower has delivered to t he District all manufacturer warranties in accordance with the 
Technical Provisions. 
(c) All demobilization from the relevant Project Site is complete, including the removal of temporary 
work and equi pment used in the perf ormance of the D&C Work, but not requ ired for  the Asset 
Management Services.

Suspension of Conversion Work and/or Smart City Work 
The District may at any time suspend, in whole or in part, the Conversion Work and/or Smart City Work by 
written order to the Borrower. Any such written ord er will be supported by the D istrict's reaso ns for the required 
suspension of the Conversion Work and/or Smart City Work. 
Except where any suspension of the Conversion W ork and/or Smart City Work by the D istrict is made in 
response to: 
(a) any failu re by the Borrower to comply with the Project  Agreement (but o nly to the ext ent such 
failure is not the dir ect result of a Co mpensation Event or Relief Event), any applicable Law  o r 
Governmental Approval where such failure would have a material adverse effect on the Project; or 
(b) the existence of  conditions unsafe for workers, other  Project p ersonnel or the ge neral public , 
including failures to comply with Safety Standards or perform Sa fety Compliance, arising from 
the failure to pe rform the Con version Wo rk and/or Smart City Work in accordance wit h the 
Project Agreement or applicable Law (but only if such conditions do not arise as a direct result of 
a Compensation Event or Relief Event),  
B-9 
any such s uspension order shall constitu te a C ompensation Event and any suspension order made in response to 
matters referred to above shall cease t o apply as soon as the relev ant matter has been rectified or remedied to the 
reasonable satisfaction of the District.

accordance with ( a) Good Indust ry Prac tice, ( b) all applicable Law, ( c) the requirements o f all G overnmental 
Approvals, and (d) the Technical Provisions and all other requirements of the Project Agreement. 
As securi ty for  t he D& C Work, the Borrower shall furnish, or cause the D &C Contractor to fur nish, a 
performance b ond and paym ent bond each  i n an a mount e qual to 100%  o f the DBC Price. Th e Borrower shall 
maintain, or cau se the D&C Contractor to maintain, each of the performance bond and t he payment bond until the 
second (2nd) ann iversary of the Project Final Completio n Date. SEE APPENDIX C – SU MMARY OF CERTAIN 
PROVISIONS OF THE D&C CONTRACT – Performance Security for a summar y of certain requirements related 
to the Bonds.

following the District's issuance of NTP3. Thereafter, t he Borrower shall carry out the Asset Management Services 
with respect to each Street Light Bundle from the Street Light Bundle Substantial  Completion Date for the relevant 
Street Light B undle until the Te rmination Date . The Borrower sh all carry out the Asset Management Services in  
accordance with Good Industry Practice, all applicable Law, the requirements of all Governme ntal Approvals, a nd 
the Technical Provisions and all other requirements of the Project Agreement. 
The Borrower a cknowledges that any Go vernmental Entity emp owered to enforce all applicable Law  is 
free to enter each Project Site at any and all time s to carry out  i ts law enforc ement duties.  No provision of the 
Project Agreement is intended to surrender, waive or limit any law enforcement powers of any Governmental Entity, 
and all such police powers are expressly reserved. 
The District may at any time suspend, in whole or in part, the Asset Management Services by written order 
to the Borrower. Any such written order will be supported by the District's reasons for the required suspension of the 
Asset Management Servic es. Except wher e any s uspension of  the Asset Management Se rvices by the D istrict 
pursuant to the suspension of the Asset Management Services provisions set out in the Project Agreement is made in 
response to: 
(a) any failure by the Borrower to comply w ith the Project Agreement (but only to th e extent such 
failure is not th e direct  result of a Compe nsation Event or R elief Event), any applic able Law or 
Governmental Approval where such failure would have a material adverse effect on the Project; or 
(b) the existen ce o f conditions unsafe f or w orkers, other P roject p ersonnel or the general public, 
including failures to co mply with Safety Standards o r perform S afety Compliance, ari sing from 
the failure to per form the Asset M anagement Services in acco rdance with t he Project Agreement 
or applicable Law (but only if such condition does not aris e as a direct result of a Compensation 
Event or Relief Event), 
any such sus pension order sh all constitute a Comp ensation Event and any susp ension ord er made in 
response to matters r eferred to in clause s (a) and (b) above, shall cease to ap ply as soon as th e relevant matter has  
been rectified or remedied to the reasonable satisfaction of the District.

Contracting and Labor Practices 
The Borrower has ob ligations in the P roject Agreemen t wi th regard to ( a) maintaining certain key 
personnel, (b) carrying out  n on-discrimination and equal opport unity employment policies an d requirements, ( c) 
complying with the D istrict's DBE program, ( d) maintaining legally re quired prevailing w ages and ( e) providing

Payments to the Borrower 
 Consideration for the Work.   As consider ation f or the  B orrower's per formance of t he W ork and  all 
obligations related thereto, the District will pay the Borrower (i) the Milestone Payments and (ii) the Availability 
Payments, as described below. 
 
Milestone Payments.  Pursuant to the te rms of the P roject Agreement, the Borrow er is eligible to receive, 
subject to the invoic ing process set forth t herein, two Milesto ne Payments for the Conversion Work and two 
Milestone Payments for the Smart City Work. The two Milestone Payments for the Conversion Work are payable in 
the amounts and at the tim es as f ollows: ( a) $21,970,000 the final day of the Ca lendar Q uarter in  which the 
Borrower has achieved St reet L ight Bundle Substant ial C ompletion for more th an 50% of the total numb er o f 
Lighting U nits expected to  be in the  Improved Street L ight Network upon Pro ject Fina l Completion and ( b) 
$22,220,000 the final da y o f the Calendar Quar ter in which Project Final Completion is achie ved. T he two 
Milestone Payments for the Smart City Work are payable in the amounts and at the times as follows: (a) $2,075,000 
the final day of the Calendar Quarter in which the p ercentage of Smart City Bundles that have reached Smar t City 
Bundle Substantial Completion first exceeds 50% and (b) $2,075,000 the final date of the Calendar Quarter in which 
all Smart City  Bundles have  r eached Smart City Bundle Substantial Completion. The Mileston e P ayments ar e 
subject to adjustment under the Project Agreement. 
Availability Pay ments.  In a ccordance with the terms o f the Project Agreem ent, and com mencing with the 
achievement of Project Final Completion and continuing for the term of the P roject Agreement, the District will pay 
to the Borrower the Availability Payments, the proceeds of which are expected to be used to , among other things , 
pay project capital c osts and satisfy principal and interest payments owed in respect to the Series 2 022 Bonds. The 
calculation of the gross Availability Paym ent in each Calendar Quarter is based on a number of var iables. The net 
Availability Payment is calculated for each Calendar Quarter and may be adjusted for certain Unavailability Events, 
where roadways and other public spaces adjacent to a Project Site do not meet availability and use requirements, and 
the accumulation of Noncompliance Points, which  are assesse d if th e Borrower ca uses a N oncompliance Event 
(essentially a failure to meet certain performance standards in the Pro ject Agreement). The Borrower is entitled to 
submit an in voice to the  District each Calendar Qu arter setting out the net amount of the Availability Payment due 
for such Calendar Q uarter. The A vailability Pa yments constitute the primary  source o f revenues f rom the Project 
and such payments are expected to exceed amounts required to pay scheduled debt service on the Series 2022 Bonds 
and maintenance and renewal costs of the Project during the Term. 
Refinancing Gain 
The District shall be entitled to receive a fifty  percent (50%) share of any Ref inancing Gain arising from a 
Qualifying R efinancing; pr ovided that the District shall not withhold or delay  i ts conse nt to a Qualifying 
Refinancing in order to obtain a greater than fifty percent (50%) share of the Refinancing Gain. The Borrower shall 
obtain the District's prior written consent to any Qualifying Refinancing and promptly provide the District with full 
details of any p roposed Qualifying Refinancing, including a copy of the proposed f inancial model relating to it (if  
any) and the basis f or the assumptions used i n the proposed financial model. The D istrict shall (be fore, during and 
any time after an y Refinancing) have unr estricted rig hts o f audi t over a ny fi nancial m odel and documentati on 
(including any aspect  of the calculation of the Ref inancing Gain) u sed in co nnection with the Refinancing whethe r 
the Refinancing is a Qualifying R efinancing o r not . The Dis trict shall ha ve the right t o re ceive its s hare of any 
Refinancing Gain as eithe r a single  payment in an amount less t han or equal to any Di stribution made on or  about 
the date of Refinancing, a reduction in the Availability Payment over the reminder of the Term, or a combination of 
these two choices.  
B-11 
Following t he Borrower's deliver y of the de tails regardin g t he proposed Qualifying Refi nancing, the  
District and the Borrower will work together in good faith to agree on the amount and timing of  the Refinancing 
Gain and the amount of the District's share.

Procedure for Relie f a nd Compensation. Subject to the late prov ision of notice requirements set out in the 
Project Agreement, in connection with any claim made by the Borrower for a C ompensation Event, the Borrower  
must (among other things): 
(a) submit a  n otice that complies with the requirem ents of the Project Agreement to the D istrict 
promptly (and in any event w ithin thirty (30) d ays) after the date that the Borrower first became 
aware that the r elevant Compensation E vent had occurred and wo uld have the effect that is the 
subject of the Borrower's claim; 
(b) promptly, and in any event wit hin sixty (60) days, after the Borrower first becomes aware of the 
relevant Compensation Event, the Borrower must give full details of th e relevant Compensation 
Event and the extension of time and/or any compensation cl aimed, including an enumerated list of 
items that includes but is not limited to: 
(i) with respect to any claim for an extension of time, a T ime Impact Analysis demonstrating the 
relevant Compensation Event wi ll result in an identifiable and measurable disrupt ion to the 
Work affecting a critical path activity; 
(ii) details o f the ste ps the Borrower has taken or will take to mitig ate the effect of th e 
Compensation Event; 
(iii) the type and am ount of insurance th at may be a pplicable and amounts that ha ve been or are 
anticipated to be collected under such insurance;  
(iv) evidence reasonably satisfactory to the District dem onstrating that (A) no other concurrent o r 
unrelated delay to a critical path activity that is the Borrower's responsibility has occurred that 
has contributed t o the del ay f or which the extensio n of time,  r elief from obligations o r 
compensation for Change in Costs and Finance Costs (as applicabl e) is being sought and ( B) 
any Fin ance Costs claimed by the Borrower will b e incurred as a direct result of the 
Compensation Event; and 
(c) demonstrate to the reasonable satisfaction of the District that: 
(i) the Compensation Event was the direct cause or is reasonably likely to be the direct cause of: 
(A) a d elay in achieving Pro ject Final Completion by the G uaranteed Project Fi nal 
Completion Date or (following th e Guaranteed Project Fi nal Completion Date, further delay 
in achieving Project Final Completion; 
(B) t he Borrower's inability t o comply with  its obligations under the Proj ect Agreement; 
and/or 
(C) the Borrower incurring a Change in Costs and/or Finance Costs; and 
(ii) the Change in C osts, Finance Costs, extension of time, and/or relief from o bligations under 
the Project  Agreement clai med, could not  reasonably be mitigated or rec overed without 
material cost by the Borrower acting in accordance with Good Industry Pra ctice, including by 
re-sequencing, reallocati ng, or redeployi ng its for ces to other portions of the  Conversion 
Work and/or Smart City Work.

position, the Borrower or the District (as applicable) shall pay to the other Party a one-off lump-sum payment, so as 
to ensure that the Borrower is left in a no better and no worse position.

and the District, the Borrower's sole remedy in relation to any C ompensation Event shall be the op eration of the
