Ladies and Gentlemen: 
We have acted as bon d counsel to the District of Columbia (the  " Bond Issuer ") in connection with issuance of 
$144,485,000 aggregate principal amount of District of Columbia Priva te Activity Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street 
Lighting Project), Series  2022A (Tax-Exempt Bonds, AMT ) (Green Bonds)  (the "Series 2022A Bonds "), and 
$15,210,000 aggregate principal amount of District of Columbia Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street Lighting 
Project), Series 2022B (Federally Ta xable) (Green Bonds) (the "Series 2022B Bonds, " and together with the Series 
2022A Bonds, the " Series 20 22 Bonds "), pursuant to the prov isions of the District of  Columbia Home Rule Act, 
approved December 24, 1973 (PL 93-198; 87 Stat. 774; D.C. Official Code §§ 1-201.01, et seq. (2001)), as amended 
(the "Home Rule Act") and the Trust Indenture, dated as of Ma y 1, 2022 (the "Indenture"), between the Bond Issuer 
and U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association , as bond trustee (the " Bond Trustee").  The Indenture provides 
that the Series 2022 Bonds are issued for the purpose of making a loan of the proceeds the reof to Plenar y 
Infrastructure D C LLC (the "Borrower") pursuant to a Loan Agreement, dated as of May 1,  2022 (the "Loan 
Agreement"), between the Bond Issuer and the Borrower.  Capitalized terms not otherwise defined herei n shall have 
the meanings ascribed thereto in the Indenture. 
In such connection, we have reviewed the Indenture, the Loan Agreement, the Tax Certificate and Agreement, dated 
the date hereof (the “Tax Certificate”), delivered by the Bond Issuer  and the Borr ower, the Collateral Agency 
Agreement dated as of  May 5, 2022,  between U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association, as the collateral 
agent, U.S. Bank National Association, as the securities intermediary and the Bond Trustee (the “Collater al Agency 
Agreement”), opinions of counsel to the  Bond Issuer, the Bond Trust ee and the Borrower and others, certificates of 
the Bond Issuer, the Bond Trustee, the Borrower and others, and such other documents, opinions and matters to the 
extent we deemed necessary to render the opinions set forth herein. 
The opinions expressed herein are based on an analysis of existing laws, regulations, rulings and court decisions and 
cover certain matters not dir ectly addressed by such authorities.  Such opinions may be  affected by actions taken or 
omitted or events o ccurring after original delivery of the Series 2022 Bonds on  the date hereof.  We have not 
undertaken to determine, or to inform any person, whether any su ch actions are taken or omitted or events do occur 
or any other mat ters come to our attention after o riginal deliver y of the Series 20 22 Bonds on the date hereof .  
Accordingly, this letter speaks only as of its date and is not intended to, and may not, be re lied upon or otherwise 
used in connection with any such actions, events or matters. Our engagement with respect to the Series 2022 Bonds 
has concluded with the ir issuance, and we disclaim any obligation to update this letter.  We have assumed the 
genuineness of all documents and signatures provided to us and the due and  legal execution and delivery thereof by, 
and validity against, any parties other than the Bon d Issuer.  We have assumed, without undertaking to verify, the  
H-2 
accuracy of the factual matters represented, warranted or certified in the documents , and of the le gal conclusions 
contained in the o pinions, referred to in the secon d paragraph hereof.  Furthe rmore, we have assumed compliance 
with all covenants and agreements contained in the Indenture,  t he Loan Agreement , the Tax Certificate  and the 
Collateral Agency A greement, including (without limi tation) covenants and agreements compliance with which is 
necessary to assure that future act ions, omissions or events will not cause interest on the Series 2022A Bonds to be 
included in gro ss income for federal income tax  purposes.  We call attention to th e fact that th e rights and 
obligations under the Series 2022 Bonds, the Indenture, the Loan Agreement and the Tax Certificate and their 
enforceability may be subject to bankruptcy, insolv ency, receivership,  reorganization, arrangement, fraudulent 
conveyance, moratorium and other laws rel ating to or affecting creditors’ rights, to the application of equitable 
principles, to the exercise of judicial discretion in appropriate cases.  We express no opinion with respect to any 
indemnification, contribution, liquidated damages, penalty (including any remedy deemed to constitute or having the 
effect of a penalty), right of set- off, arbitration, choice of law, choice of forum, choice of venue, non -exclusivity of 
remedies, waiver or  severability provisions contained  in the foregoing documents, nor do we express any opinion 
with respect to the state or quality of title to or interest in any of the real or personal property described in or as 
subject t o the lien of the I ndenture or the  accuracy or sufficiency of the des cription conta ined therein of, or the 
remedies available to enforce liens on, any such property.  Our services did not include financial or other non -legal 
advice.  Finally, we undertake no  responsibility for the accuracy, completeness or fairness of the Official Statement 
or other offer ing material relating to the Series 2022 Bonds and express n o opinion or conclusion with respect 
thereto. 
Based on and subject to the foregoing, and in rel iance thereon, as of the date hereo f, we are of the following 
opinions: 
1.  The Bon d Issuer is a body  politic and corporate duly created and organized and validl y existing for 
municipal purposes under the Constitution of the United States of America and the Home Rule Act, wi th corporate 
power and authority to enter into an d perform its obligations under the Indenture and the Loan Agreement, to issue 
the Series 2022 Bonds, to apply the proceeds of the Series 2022 Bonds in the manner described in the In denture and 
to pledge and assign the Trust Estate to the Bond Trustee under the Indenture. 
2. The Series 2022 Bonds have been duly authorized and issued in accordance with the Home Rule Act an d 
constitute valid and binding special obligations of the Bond I ssuer pa yable as to principal, premium, i f any, and 
interest solely from t he revenues and receipts pledged thereto pursuant to the Indenture.  The Series 2022 Bonds do  
not constitute a debt or pledge of the faith and credit of the Bond Issuer, do not make a pledge of or involve the faith 
and credit or taxing power of the Bond Is suer, do not constitute a debt of  the Bond Issuer and do not constitute a 
lending of the public credit of the Bond Issuer for a private undertaking prohibited by section 602(a)(2) of  the Home 
Rule Act. 
3.  The Indenture has been duly executed and delivered  by, and constitutes the valid and binding obligation 
of, the Bond Issuer , enforceable against the Bond Issuer in accordance with its terms .  The Indenture cre ates a valid 
pledge, to secure the payment of th e principal of a nd interest on the Series  2022 Bonds, of the Tru st Estate, subject 
to the provisions of the Indenture permitting the application thereof for the purposes and on the terms and conditions 
set forth in the Indenture. 
4.  The Loan Agreement has  been duly exec uted and delivered by, and  constitutes a valid an d binding 
agreement of, the Bond Issuer, enforceable against the Bond Issuer in accordance with its terms.  
H-3 
5.  Interest on the Series 2022A Bonds is excluded from gross income for federal income tax purposes  
under Section 103 of the In ternal Revenue Code of 1986 (the " Code"), except that no opinion  is expressed as to the 
status of interest on any Series 2022A Bond for any period that such Series 2022A Bond is held by a " substantial 
user" of the facilities financed or refinanced by the Series 2022A Bonds or by a " related person" within the meaning 
of Section 147(a) of the Code. We observe, however, that interest on the Series 2022A Bonds is a specific 
preference item for purposes of the federal alternative mini mum tax.  Interest on the Se ries 2022 Bonds is ex empt 
from all taxation by the District of C olumbia, except estate, inheritance and gift taxes.  We express no opinion 
regarding other tax consequences related to the ownership or disposition o f, or the amoun t, accrual or receipt of 
interest on, the Series 2022 Bonds.

1.0 Executive Summary 
 Introduction 
A Request for Proposal (“RFP”) has been issued by the District of Columbia Office of Public-Private Partnerships (“OP3” or 
the “District”), in conjunction with the District of Columbia Department of Transportation (“DDOT”) and the Office of the 
Chief Technology (“OCTO”) for the (1) design, construction, financing and maintenance (“DBFM”) of the Improved Street 
Light Network and (2) the installation, testing and commissioning of the Smart City Improvements  (the “Project”).  
Following the receipt and evaluation of proposals, the District selected Plenary Americas US Holdings Inc. (“Plenary”), 
Kiewit Development Company (“KDC”) and Phoenix Infrastructure Group, LLC (“Phoenix,” and collectively with Plenary 
and KDC, the “Sponsors”) to enter into a Project Agreement (“PA”) for the Project. The Developer is a special purpose 
vehicle, Plenary Infrastructure DC (the “Developer”), for the purpose of accepting the PA obligations to design, build, 
finance and maintain the Project over the 15-year Term commencing at the Commercial Closing Date and ending at the 
earlier of: the Expiry Date; or the Early Termination Date. 
The Project is being procured through a Public-Private Partnership (“P3”) method, which is now a common method of 
procurement in North America.  P3s have been gaining significant traction and acceptance in the United States in recent 
years as a viable method to deliver large capital construction projects.  
BTY US LLC (“BTY” or the “LTA”) is the Lenders’ Technical Advisor on the Project.

 Summary of Key Contracts 
Contract Intent 
Project Agreement 
Contract between the District and the Developer detailing the acceptance of DBFM 
responsibilities by the Developer, responsibilities retained by the District, and the 
payment mechanism. The PA is similar to previous North American DBFM 
agreements, and in general the risk allocation is typical of North American P3 
projects. 
D&C Contract Fixed lump-sum price transfer of the Developer’s design, construction and 
commissioning, risk to the D&C Contractor. 
Asset Management Contract 
(“AM Contract”) 
Fixed lump-sum price transfer of the Developer’s Asset Management and lifecycle 
responsibilities and risk to the Asset Management Contractor. 
Interface Agreement Agreement among the Developer, D&C Contractor and Asset Management 
Contractor to clarify cross-over responsibilities and potential interface issues. 
Finance Documents 
Agreements between the Developer, District, Bond Trustee and the Lenders 
detailing financing terms: including the Loan Agreement; Trust Indenture; and 
Collateral Agency Agreement. Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project

considered market positions. 
The status rating for the following table should be interpreted as follows:  
Draft or Pre-Bid Report Final Report  
Project aspect that requires further assessment, 
information or mitigation prior to the Final Report. 
A red item indicates points to be considered with 
substantial Project impact. 
Project aspect with a red rating should be considered by the 
creditor as a significantly higher than expected risk profile 
that could have significant impact on the Project from a 
technical perspective. 
R 
Project aspect that requires further assessment, 
information or mitigation prior to the Final Report, 
but is not anticipated having significant Project 
impact. 
Project aspect with an amber rating should be noted by the 
Creditor as a higher than expected risk profile but should not 
have significant impact on the Project from a technical 
perspective. 
A 
Project aspect that has an acceptable risk profile 
and therefore acceptable from a technical 
perspective. 
Project aspect with a green rating is considered typical or an 
acceptable Project risk or suitable full technical mitigation 
has been received to ensure minimal technical Project 
effects.

3.4 Design Review 
Procedure  
• The review periods ascribed in the PA are rapid compared to similar 
periods seen in other governmental approval processes. We consider 
the review periods to be a major mitigant, as to reduces the design 
approvals durations but it also demonstrates the District’s will to 
accelerate the Project schedules; and 
• The submittal approval process is clearly established under the 
Technical Provisions. We consider the approval process to be in line 
with North American P3 projects.

3.5 Design Review 
Procedure 
• The technical standards are reasonable in nature. No onerous 
requirements were identified, and the D&C Contractor is comfortable 
with the requirements for the Project. The proposed design solution is 
reasonable, utilizes well understood means and methods and does not 
include any new or unproven approaches; and 
• The design solution does not include any Alternative Technical 
Concepts; and 
• The design requirements for the components are market, utilizing well 
understood technologies and installations practices. The material 
component specifications are transparent and prescriptive, with 
catalog-like specification sheets. The District has provided clear design 
expectations that have enabled the Developer to adequately progress 
the design for this stage of procurement. The suggested components 
outlined in the specification included reputable brands, which are 
sourced from well known manufacturers.

Approvals

throughout the Term, as requested by the relevant Governmental 
Authority or Licensor;

to the Developer, except for District-Provided Approvals, which are 
defined as the NEPA Document;

the Technical Provisions; 
• Regulatory approval requirements are consistent with similar projects 
in the United States and pose no significant challenge to the 
Developer; 
• The District acts as both the Authority Having Jurisdiction (“AHJ”) and 
the P3 owner for this Project, which mitigates potential permitting 
delays driven by the AHJ; 
• The Developer’s risk related to regulatory approvals has been passed 
through to the D&C Contractor through the D&C Contract; and 
• The Developer’s risk regarding permitting obligations is considered to 
be low. The Project has limited permitting obligations compared to 
similar project across North America. The majority of the permitting 
obligations are completed at early stages leading up to NTP3 and not 
critical to the schedule. The District has outlined a rapid review 
process for the review of permits and design submission. Permitting 
requirements post NTP3 are limited to street use permits which are of 
minimal risk.

Report Ref Item Summary  
 
7 Construction 
Schedule 
• The 6-month Long Stop Deadline is short compared with recent P3 
projects in North America, where we have typically seen a 12-month 
longstop date. However, given the (i) shorter construction schedule 
relative to other P3 projects, and (ii) the absence of a critical path after 
the achievement of the first NTP3 and the D&C Contractor’s ability to 
accelerate the schedule, and (iii) the simplicity of the D&C Work, based 
on the schedule requirements outlined in the Technical Provisions, the 
6-month longstop date is appropriate; 
• The Developers risk related to project schedule has been transferred 
through the D&C Contract to the D&C Contractor;  
• The Substantial Completion and Final Completion framework outlined 
in the PA are considered to be reasonable and typical for North 
American P3 projects; 
• The D&C Contractor has demonstrated a good understanding of the 
requirements and has proposed a suitable schedule for this stage of 
the Project; and 
• Given the simple complexity of the Work and the flexibility of the D&C 
Contractor to adjust productivity, we consider the over schedule risks 
to be minimal. The Project Baseline Schedule is well structured with 
well implied logic, based on a review of the PDF submittal with the 
Developer’s Technical submission and through discussion with the D&C 
Contractor. We consider the Project can be delivered within the 
timeframe allowed by the D&C Contractor.

8 D&C Contract 
Price 
• The D&C Contractor developed pricing in-house using a bottom-up 
approach with direct quotes from subcontractors, supplier and 
manufacturers. In addition, the D&C Contractor performed a formal 
request for qualification (“RFQ”) process where multiple vendors both 
regional and prominent vendors submitted price quotes for the 
materials as to minimize costs over the 15-year period; 
• The D&C Contractor has performed similar street lighting projects 
globally and has a strong internal pricing expertise. The D&C 
Contractor has acquired direct quotes from subcontractors, 
manufacturers and suppliers to supplement their internal data. The 
accuracy of the pricings is reflected by their recent experience. This 
provides additional comfort that the Project can be executed for the 
proposed price; and 
• The level of detail required to substantiate Payment Application to the 
D&C Contractor and the timelines ascribed are achievable. The overall 
payment process is comparable to similar payment process seen across 
North American P3 projects. We consider the risks related to payment 
process to be insignificant.

8.8 D&C Contractor 
Replacement 
Scenarios  
• We consider the proposed security package to be in line with recent 
market precedence on recent P3 projects in North America; 
• We consider the available security package as a whole to be sufficient 
to cover the total costs of replacing the D&C Contractor in an unlikely 
event of the D&C Contractor Default under the D&C Contract; 
• The proposed Construction Liquidity Reserve Account provides 
sufficient cash coverage of the short-term premium identified under 
each scenario of our D&C Contractor replacement analysis; and 
• In the instance of a D&C Contractor default, the Developer has 
capitalized 100% of costs (including debt service, Asset Management 
Services costs, and other costs) from the Guaranteed Project Final 
Completion Date to the Long Stop Deadline, providing additional 
liquidity to the Project.

3.0 Project Description 
 Introduction 
The District has issued an RFP for the Project, which entails the design, build, installation, finance and maintenance of 
upgrades to the entire existing streetlight network in the District of Columbia. The existing streetlight network consists of 
72,051 primary and alley streetlights, select overhead guide signs, lighting for approximately 30 “Welcome to 
Washington, D.C.” signs and lighting for navigation, underpasses, bike trails and tunnels.  
The Developer is responsible for the following:  
• The conversion of existing non-LED District-owned lights to LED technology;  
• The design and installation of remote monitoring and control systems for the streetlight network;  
• Upgrading poles and associated infrastructure;  
• The delivery of long-term asset management of the streetlight system, in accordance with the Project 
Agreement;  
• The installation of smart city technology (“SCT”) in addition to the District’s fiber optic network (“DC -Net”) for 
the delivery of wi-fi and sensor technologies; and  
• The financing of the improvements to the District’s existing streetlight network and SCT installation.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
13 
 
DC-Net provides wireless services to all Government constituents throughout the District and is managed by OCTO and 
will serve in the District’s planned SCT solutions. The Project’s procurement efforts will be led by OP3. OP3 will be 
working closely DDOT and OCTO throughout the procurement process.  
The electricity for the current streetlight network is supplied by Washington Gas and El ectric (“WGES”) and distributed 
through the Potomac Electric Power Company (“Pepco”) network. The distribution network consists of overhead (50%) 
and underground powerlines (50%). Pepco and Verizon both own portions of the poles (37% and 9% respectively) and 
retain operations and maintenance responsibilities for their respective poles. The District retains ownership of the 
remaining 54% of the poles. All arms and fixtures are owned by DDOT and asset management responsibilities for these 
items are included in the Project.  
 Project Sites 
The Project Sites include any real property necessary for the completion Project Work. This includes temporary and 
permanent easements. The Project Sites may also run over, on or under the Public Space or private property.  
 
The Project is located in Washington, D.C. In general, the Project excludes assets under the jurisdiction of the 
Governmental Entities, political subdivisions of the District of Columbia include National Park Service, the Architect of the 
Capitol, DC Parks and Recreation Department, Department of General Services, District of Columbia Housing Authority, 
and Foreign embassies.  
The picture below demonstrates each of the District of Columbia’s eight Wards.  
 Existing Street Lighting Network 
The inventory of the Existing Street Light Network, as reported in the “DDOT Streetlight Conditions Assessment Final 
Report” (the “Condition Assessment”) dated November 2020, includes 72,051 Lighting Units, including 37,407 District-
owned Poles, 28,276 Poles owned by owned by Pepco and 6,358 owned by Verizon (Exhibit 19 of the PA). The Existing 
Street Light Network includes the Lighting Units located in underpasses, on the underside of bridges that pass over 
roadways and in tunnels within the Project Limits. 
The Condition Assessment broke out and assessed a number of fixtures (pole shaft, brackets and arms, luminaires , etc. 
each an “Element”) and each was assigned a rating, from Severely Damaged (rated “0”) to Excellent (“5”). The District 
provides reliance on the completeness and accuracy of the report. The table below demonstrates the results of the 
condition review by Element.

numbers and details for each Element supported with images from inspections.  
 
 Design Solution Overview 
The Design, Conversion and Construction requirements are detailed in Technical Provisions (“TP”) section 7. The Asset 
Management (“AM”) Work requirements are detailed in TP section 10. 
The Developer’s risk related to the Street Lighting Network during the design and construction phase is transferred to the 
D&C Contractor. The Developer’s risk related to the Street Lighting Network during post Project Final Completion is 
transferred to the Asset Management Contractor. 
The Project does not include any Alternative Technical Concepts (Section 6 of the PA).  
Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
16 
 
The Design Work is to be completed in accordance with the District of Columbia Streetlight Policy and Design Guidelines 
issued by DDOT, are the core design guidelines driving the design selection. These guidelines include all functional 
classifications as recommended by the Illuminating Engineering Society of North America and American Association of 
State Highway and Transportation Officials (“AASHTO”).  
The additional standards are outlined in Section 13.2 of the Technical Provisions. 
The Project can be broken down into two main design components being:  
• Street Light Improvements design; and 
• Asset Management Information System (“AMIS”) and Remote Monitoring and Control system (“RMCS”) design. 
Street Light Improvement Design 
The Developer has performed benchmark testing in labs on each type of luminaire and run models combining different 
types of poles and luminaires to ensure compliance with the Technical Provisions. Their findings hav e been cross 
referenced with manufacturer reports and tests.  
Based on the provided Condition Assessment commissioned by DDOT the Street Light Improvement, as part of the 
Conversion Work the Developer is required to increase the condition rating for all components of the Existing Street Light 
Network rated “poor” or worse to a fair or better condition level. The requirements for each condition level rating are 
clearly defined, and in numerous cases visuals are included in the Technical Provisions to clarify  define the acceptable 
condition ratings.  
The key components to the street lights include the following:  
• Pole Shaft, which can be comprised of: aluminum, cast iron, composite, metal, steel or wood. The District 
currently uses 29 styles of various heights, with the most common height being 28 ft in height in both single arm 
and double arm orientation; 
• Brackets and Arms, which includes 16 separate arm styles, the majority of which are a part of standard arm 
manufacturing practise; 
• Luminaires, which include 15 styles of Lighting Units in various beam distributions currently used in the District; 
• Glare Shield; 
• Handhole; 
• T-Base/ Base Cover; 
• Anchor Bolts; and 
• Foundation Bolts. 
The design requirements for the components are market, utilizing well understood technologies and installations 
practices. The material component specifications are transparent and prescriptive, with catalog like specification sheets. 
The District has provided clear design expectations that have enabled the Developer to adequately progress the design 
for this stage of procurement.   
The suggested components outlined in the specification included reputable brands, which are sourced from well known 
manufacturers who have supplied a multitude of municipalities across North America.  
AMIS and RMCS Design 
The RMCS is designed to provide real-time, high-speed, unrestricted remote monitoring and control of the Improved 
Street Light Network. The RMCS will display network infrastructure and status and allow configuration of monitoring and 
control devices, for: scheduling, lighting controls, real-time Automatic Vehicle Location, GPS location tracking and energy 
consumption. The RMCS software will be able to remotely control the Lighting Units through RMCS nodes (“Nodes”) 
installed within each lighting fixture via a cellular backhaul network on a 24/6/365 availability basis, excluding loss of 
power events (in which case a traditional photocell would kick-in to govern lighting levels). The Nodes are wireless two-
way communicating components controlled via DALI. Nodes are capable of dimming between 100% to 10% capacity on a 
programmable basis. Nodes are equipped with the following features: GPS, tilt sensor, programmable schedules, loss of 
communication functionality, surge protection, data logging, energy measurement, on and off integrated relay and 
programmable for encrypted communications. The Nodes include a standard warranty of 10 years.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
17 
 
The AMIS will serve as a centralized database where all Asset Management Services are managed. The consortium 
intends to use Cityworks 7 as its AMIS, which is the same system in use by the District, which will facilitate integration 
with the District’s instance of Citywork 7.  
The AMIS will be fully hosted, including all data storage, within the continental U.S. Cloud enabled mass data storage. The 
system will include a minimum 128 AES Encrypted Communication. The system will include an incident response 
procedure and provide protection from unauthorized users via security systems, fire walls and any other components. 
The server data will be stored from unauthorized asses and included system redundancy and backup server as part of a 
disaster recovery plan. 
Smart City Work Design 
The Developer will be required to deploy 239 total Wireless Access Points (“WAPs”), comprised of 91 Root Access Points 
(RAPs) and 148 Mesh Access Points. The WAP models and warranty specifications outlined in the Technical Provisions are 
prescriptive and suitable for the Project. See image below for an example of a WAP fixture.  
 
 
 Design Review Procedure 
The design review procedure is outlined in Section 7 of the PA. The Developer must not commence or permit the 
commencement of any Conversion Work and/or Smart City Work that is the subject of, governed by, or dependent upon 
a Reviewable Submittal until it has been submitted such Reviewable Submittal to the District  (Section 7.2 of the PA); and 
i) District has provided its approval or consent to the relevant Discretionary Submittal or Non-Discretionary 
Submittal;  
ii) Should the District not respond to a Non-Discretionary Submittal within five Business Days the status is 
considered to be approved; and 
iii) With respect to an R&C Submittal, the R&C Submittal is considered approved after the expiration of the 
time period during which the District is entitled to raise comments. 
In accordance with the District’s grounds for objection or comment, the District may submit:  
Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
18 
 
• Compliance Comments, which are required to be amended prior to approval; and  
• Preference Comments, which reflect concern regarding interpretation or preference. The Developer is expected 
to make Reasonable Efforts to accommodate all Preference Comments.  
If the Developer does not address a Compliance Comment or Preference Comment within 30 days after receipt, the 
Developer is obligated to provide the District with a written explanation as to why the modifications or accommodations 
were not addressed. Should the District disagree with the Developer’s explanation, the Parties will attempt in Good Faith 
to resolve the Dispute. Should Good Faith negotiation fails, the Parties will proceed in accordance with the Dispute 
Resolution Procedure (Section 7.3 of the PA).  
The District is entitled to review and comment on, or to affirmatively approve, a Submittal, within ten Business Days after 
the date the District receives an accurate and complete Submittal. Should a resubmission of a Submittal be required, the 
subsequent review period will be reduced to five Business Days (Section 7.5 of the PA).  
The review periods ascribed in the PA are rapid compared to similar periods seen in other governmental approval 
processes. We consider the review periods to be a major mitigant, as to reduces the design approvals durations but it 
also demonstrates the District’s will to accelerate the Project schedules.  
The submittal approval process is clearly established under the Technical Provisions. We consider the approval process to 
be in line with North American P3 projects.

working in offices across the continent.

• The Developer team members have extensive experience in this role on numerous North American P3 projects.

• The strong experience of the Developer’s consortium members on projects where street light networks were 
installed or upgraded to an LED network is a positive indicator that it is capable of providing the best solution to 
the Project;  
• Overall and based on the team members successful track record on similar  streetlighting and streetlighting 
conversion projects, and with the simplicity of the operations scope, the Developer and Asset Management 
Contractor should have no issues carrying out its responsibilities to ensure a smooth project delivery ; 
• The Asset Management Contractor is currently performing a similar role on the Michigan Freeway Lighting 
project which is the only other P3 street lighting project in US; and 
• The D&C Contractor and Asset Management Contractor are both ENGIE entities, providing the team added 
integration during design and operation and improved risk transfer.

5.0 Regulatory Approvals 
 Introduction  
The Project Sites are defined as any real property necessary for the performance for the Work, including temporary and 
permanent easements. The Project Sites may run under, on or over the Public Space or private property (Exhibit 1 of the 
PA).  
The Project Sites are bundled in collective groups for the performance of the Smart City Work (Smart City Bundles, 
together with Street Light Bundles collectively are the Project Bundles). Project Bundles will not include both Street Light 
Improvements and Smart City Improvements in the same Project Bundle. Project Bundles consists of a set of Lighting 
Units or Smart City Work. The Project Bundles have minimum requirements (i.e., have independent utility, must be 
completed within 14 calendar days, etc.) (Section 1.5.5 of the Technical Provisions). 
The District grants the Developer the exclusive right to develop, design, construct and finance the Project and carry out 
the Asset Management Services. Access to the Project Site will be provided to the Devel oper on the Commercial Closing 
Date to fulfill their Project obligations outlined in the PA. The District is required to provide Access to the Developer at 
their own cost. The District must not materially interfere with the Developer’s Work related obligat ions (Section 8 of the 
PA).  
The Developer is required to coordinate with all third parties that require Project Site Access.  The Developer shall take 
reasonable efforts to: 
• Coordinate its Work to ensure that it does not interfere with the District’s right of entry; and 
• Provide the District with assistance for inspections, including entry to relevant facilities, systems, registers, 
manuals, records, plans and programs. 
 Access and Inspection Rights for the District and Other Persons 
The District is granted Access to the locations on the Project Site where Conversion Work is ongoing, to carry out the 
following activities (Section 8.4 of the PA):  
• Observe and inspect the Work; 
• Monitor the Developer’s compliance with the PA obligations, Technical Provisions, Applicable Law and 
Governmental Approvals; and 
• Exercise any right or perform any obligation that the District has under the Agreement.  
When exercising their rights to Access and inspect the Project Site, the District  shall do so in a manner that:  
• Does not unreasonably interfere with the Developer’s Work or exercise of its rights under the Agreement; and 
• Complies with the Developer’s Access and work health and safety policies and procedures.  
We highlight that the Project does not include any federal government lands and all such lands are outside the Project 
limit lines and are not the Developer’s responsibility.  
 Accommodation of Additional Attachments and Fixtures 
Utility Owners and third parties are expected to apply for permits to modify or add attachments or fixtures to one or 
more Poles throughout the Term. The permits may be requested from the relevant Governmental Authority or Licensor 
after the Financial Closing Date. The Developer is required to assist the Utility Owners and third parties to obtain the 
permits through the following actions (Section 8.5 of the PA):  
• Ensure each applicant has the required information regarding the Existing Street Light Network and the 
Improved Street Light Network; and Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
26 
 
• Use Reasonable Efforts to coordinate work schedules with such applicants to avoid the applicant’s activities 
interfering with the Work. 
Any damages, interruptions to, or interference with, the Work caused by any Utility Owner or third party would 
constitute a Compensation Event under the PA. 
Given the minimal Developer permitting obligations, the risk relating to permitting are limited when compared to other 
P3 projects across North America. The majority of the permitting works are related to design approval which are 
concluded prior to NTP3. Following NTP3 the Developer, any further permitting obligations are limited to traffic closure 
and other “street use” applications with the District, which will use pre-qualified procedures established and sign-off by 
the District leading up to NTP3.  
We highlight the District as the governing body approving design submittals and permitting applications. As both owner 
and approver the District is highly motivated to quickly review submission, as to not inhibit progress. This is reflected in 
the 10-day review period, which much shorter when compared to other government processes across North America.  
The Developer has demonstrated a strong level of understanding of the permitting requirements and outlined suitable 
timelines for the accrual of such permits. 
 Responsibility for Governmental Approvals 
5.4.1  RESPONSIBILTIES OF THE DEVELOPER  
The Developer is solely responsible for obtaining all Governmental Approvals required under the Agreement, except 
District-Provided Approvals. The Developer is responsible for obtaining amendments or modifications to District -Provided 
Approvals necessary to reflect the Developer’s Final Design or a deviation in construction means and methods, except as 
otherwise set forth in Section 10.5 of the PA (Compliance with NEPA Document). If the necessary amendments or 
modifications are not permitted by the Governmental Entity, the Developer will revise the Final Design or construction 
means and methods at its own risk of delay and cost to satisfy the requirements and conditions of such Governmental 
Entity (Section 10.2 (a) of the PA). Additionally, the PA provides a Relief event in the instance there is any delay in 
obtaining Government Approvals to the extent that such delay Is beyond the reasonable control of any Developer -
Related Entity. Given the scope and simplicity of the Project, this is considered to be low risk.  
5.4.2  RESPONSIBILITIE S OF THE DISTRICT  
District-Provided Approvals are defined as the NEPA Document (Exhibit 1 of the PA).  
The District is responsible for obtaining all District-Provided Approvals as of the Setting Date, which is defined as 30 days 
before the Technical Proposal Due Date. The District will maintain, renew, replace, extend or arrange necessary 
amendments to the District-Provided Approvals throughout the Term, as necessary (Section 10.2 (b) of the PA).  
If requested, the District must cooperate with the Developer in relation to any application for a Governmental Approval 
by the Developer where the District is needed to obtain, renew, replace, extend or arrange necessary amendments to 
such Governmental Approval. If the District provides assistance to the Developer with respect to obtaining such 
Governmental Approval, the Developer shall reimburse the District for its reasonable third -party costs associated with 
the provision of such assistance within 30 days of receiving an invoice from the District (Section 10.3 of the PA).  
5.4.3  COMPLIANCE WITH NEPA DOCUMENT  
Throughout the Project Term, the Developer must always comply with the NEPA Document . If modifications or 
supplements to the NEPA Document are required after the Commercial Closing Date, the District will be responsible for 
gathering the necessary documentation using the data and information provided by the Developer. If the need for such 
supplement or modification arises from a District Change, Compensation Event or Relief Event, the District shall bear the 
costs of preparing the necessary documentation. In all other cases, the Developer will bear the costs of preparing the 
necessary documentation (Section 10.5 of the PA). Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
27 
 
 Zoning Classification Pertaining to the Street Light Network 
The Developer’s Design Work shall be performed in a manner consistent with the anticipated levels of nighttime 
pedestrians and vehicular traffic. While performing the Work, the Developer must consider these anticipated levels and 
respect the following Zoning Classifications (Section 1.5.2 of the Technical Provisions): 
• Commercial Zone: this zone corresponds to a densely developed business region of the District. The land use in 
this zone will attract a heavy, consistent volume of nighttime vehicular traffic and/or pedestrian traffic; 
• Residential Zone: this zone corresponds to an area of the District containing a mix of residential buildings and 
small commercial establishments. The land use in this zone will attract a low volume of nighttime vehicular and 
pedestrian traffic; 
• Intermediate or Mixed Zone: this zone corresponds to an area of the District that contains characteristics of both 
Residential Zones and Commercial Zones. The land use in this zone will attract a moderate volume of nighttime 
pedestrian traffic; and 
• Other land uses: Offices, industrial parks, and other facilities not included into the above classifications will 
belong to the classification that reflects the areas anticipated nighttime pedestrian traffic levels. 
 Title Encumbrances and Licenses 
License Agreements have executed for all Pole Attachments in the District. The agreements are between the District and 
the Potomac Electric Power Company and/or between the District and Verizon Washington, DC Inc (Exhibit 20 of the PA), 
and grant the Developer the right to install Elements (i.e., Arms and Luminaires) on Poles that are not owned by the 
District. 
The Developer reserves the right to perform Work on the Project Site and coordinate with all third parties that have 
Project Site Access, in accordance with the PA terms and License Agreements (Section 8.3  of the PA).  
The District is required to provide the Developer with a copy of each License Agreement and any relevant amendments. 
It is the responsibility of the Developer to comply with the License Agreement terms throughout the Project Term . If a 
Utility Owner is required to be paid a fee in connection with any Work, the Developer will provide written notice to the 
District describing the relevant Work and corresponding fee prior to undertaking such Work. The Developer will promptly 
undertake such Work and pay the corresponding fee to the Utility Owner in accordance with the License Agreement. The 
Developer’s payment of fees to a Utility Owner constitutes a District Change which is included as a Compensation Event 
under the PA (Section 12.5 of the PA).  
 Electric Utility Service 
The District is responsible for procuring and paying the costs of the Project’s electric utility service, throughout the Term  
(Section 8.6 of the PA). 
 Conclusion 
We note the following: 
• The Developer’s risk related to regulatory approvals has been passed through to the D&C Contractor through 
the D&C Contract;  
• The District shall grant the Developer Project Site Access on the Commercia l Closing Date; 
• The Developer acknowledges that the District holds Access rights observe and inspect the Work and monitor 
compliance with PA obligations in a manner that does not interfere with the Developer’s Work or health and 
safety policies and procedures; Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
28 
 
• Utility Owners and third parties are responsible for applying for permits to modify or add attachments or fixtures 
to one or more Poles throughout the Term, as requested by the relevant Governmental Authority or Licensor;  
• The risk related to obtaining all Governmental Approvals is transferred to the Developer, except for District-
Provided Approvals, which are defined as the NEPA Document; 
• The Developer must adhere to the Zoning Classifications outlined in the Technical Provisions;  
• Regulatory approval requirements are consistent with similar projects in the United States and pose no 
significant challenge to the Developer; and 
• The Developer’s risk regarding permitting obligations is considered to be low. The Project has limited permitting 
obligations compared to similar project across North America. The majority of the permitting obligations are 
completed at early stages leading up to NTP3 and not critical to the schedule. The District has outlined a rapid 
review process for the review of permits and design submission.

6.0 Site Conditions 
 Introduction 
The Developer shall perform the following tasks to familiarize itself with the Project Site (Section 3.1(m) of the PA):  
• Familiarize itself with the Existing Street Light Network and the surrounding locations bas ed on the Disclosed 
Information; and 
• Investigate and review the Disclosed information and all other available public records.  
Based on the investigation, review and examination of the Disclosed Information and available public records, the 
Developer shall be familiar with and accept the physical limitations and conditions of the Project Site and Work 
requirements. BTY highlights that this Project contains minimal to no anticipated site work. 
The Developer acknowledges that (Section 9.1 of the PA): 
• Neither the District nor any District-Related Entity makes any representations or warranties as to the relevance, 
completeness, accuracy or fitness for any purpose of the Disclosed Information;  
• The Disclosed Information is for reference purposes only and is not mandatory or binding on the Developer; and 
• The Developer shall not rely on the Disclosed Information to describe the existing Project Site conditions, design, 
engineering or asset management solutions or directions, or defining means or methods for complying with the 
requirements of the Agreement, the Governmental Approvals or Applicable Law.  
The District will not have any liability to the Developer with respect to the inaccuracy, omission, lack of fitness for any 
purpose or inadequacy in the Disclosed Information. The Developer failing to satisfy itself on the conditions of the 
existing Project Site will not constitute relief from their responsibilities described in the PA . Additionally, the Developer 
must not incur additional expenses to the District for the rectification of the site conditions.  
Similarly, the Developer will not be provided with relief from their obligations described in the PA or make claims that the 
Disclosed Information provided by the District is insufficient. If the Developer r equires further information regarding the 
accuracy and/or adequacy of the Disclosed Information, they will be required to do so at the expense of their own money 
and time (Section 9.5 of the PA).  
During the Developer’s performance of the Conversion Work, the Developer may submit a Differing Asset Notice should 
the District provided Asset Inventory and Condition Report discovered to be either: not included in the Asset Inventory 
and Condition Report; or in a physical condition that is worse than ascribed for such Element in the Asset Inventory and 
Condition Report. The process requires the Developer’s submission within 10 Business Days following such discovery, 
which the District will respond within 30 days of receipt of such Differing Asset Notice. The Dist rict will outline the 
repaired, rehabilitation or replacement required for each Element and whether the Developer is entitled to a District 
Change which will provide relief in the form of D&C Contract Price and/or Project Baseline Schedule adjustments (Sec tion 
9.6 of the PA).  
 Due Diligence 
The Developer will be deemed to have satisfied itself as to (Section 9.3 of the PA):  
• The assets to which it will receive rights (including any existing structures, Poles, Utilities or work on, over or 
under such assets); 
• The nature and extent of the risks assumed by the Developer under the Agreement, including with respect to:  
• Geotechnical, climatic, geological, hydrological, ecological, environmental and other physical conditions of each 
Project Site; 
• The ground and subsoil; Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
30 
 
• The materials to be excavated (natural or otherwise); 
• The form and nature of each Project Site; 
• The risk of injury or damage to property near to or affecting each portion of each Project Site and to perform its 
obligations defined under the Agreement; 
• The access to and through each Project Site for the purpose of performing its obligations defined under the 
Agreement; 
• The precautions, times and methods of working necessary to prevent, mitigate or reduce any nuisance or 
interference (public or private) being caused to third parties; and 
• The scope of the Disclosed Information. 
The Project does not include any geotechnical report or environmental assessment given that the Project does not 
include any anticipated significant underground works. The geotechnical and environmental risks are negligible. All 
conditions are known and included in the Asset Condition Report provided by the District.  
6.2.1  CONTAMINATION   
Hazardous Materials are defined as any element, chemical, compound, mixture, material or substance who se presence 
on the environment is prohibited, controlled or regulated under Applicable Law. This includes “hazardous substances” as 
defined under CERCLA (Exhibit 1 of the PA). 
The Developer assumes the sole responsibility for the management, treatment, handling, storage, monitoring, 
remediation, removal, transport and disposal of any Hazardous Materials in, under or on any Project Site encountered 
throughout the Term. This responsibility holds whether the District is designated as the “generator” or “arranger” of such 
Hazardous Materials, pursuant to Section 13.2 of the PA (Generator Status) (Section 13.1 of the PA). 
The Developer is solely responsible for the management, treatment, handling, storage, monitoring, remediation, 
removal, transport and disposal of any Developer Hazardous Materials Release throughout the Term. Before any 
Remedial Action is carried out for which the Developer has responsibility for (other than a Developer Hazardous 
Materials Release) that would inhibit the District’s ability to determine the nature and extent of such Hazardous 
Environmental Condition, the Developer shall provide the District with an opportunity to inspect areas and locations that 
require Remedial Action within a reasonable time period.  
If there is a sudden Hazardous Materials Release, the Developer may implement the minimum actions to ensure the 
release is contained, without issuing prior notice to the District. In this case, the District is required to alert the District 
within two Business Days of the sudden Hazardous Materials Release and its location. The Developer must assume the 
responsibility for obtaining and maintaining all Governmental Approvals associated with the relevant Remedial Actions. 
The Developer must comply with all Governmental Approvals and Applicable Laws associated with the Remedial Actions 
for Hazardous Materials Releases.  
A Compensation Event will occur in the following scenarios regarding Project Site contamination (Exhibit 1 of the PA): 
• If a Hazardous Materials Release on, into, from or under any Project Site following the Commercial Close Date 
causes a Hazardous Environmental Condition and does not constitute a Developer Hazardous Materials Release; 
and 
• The discovery of any Extraordinary Hazardous Environmental Condition (any lead paint on Poles, sludge, fuel, 
petroleum or petroleum byproduct in any manhole, the presence of which constitutes a Hazard ous 
Environmental Condition), including any Remedial Action relating thereto, during the Conversion Work period or 
D&C Period). 
When carrying out a Remedial Action that is the subject of a Compensation Event, the Developer must take steps, as 
required by the District, to protect and preserve the District’s potential claims of contribution and indemnity, statutory or 
otherwise, against all potentially responsible parties (Section 13.1 of the PA). Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
31 
 
The Developer will bear all costs and expenses associated with the rectification of Hazardous Materials on the Project 
Site, throughout the Term, with the exception of specific provisions described in the PA. Given the nature of the work, 
the risk related to Hazardous Materials is considered to be low.  
 Utilities 
A Utility is defined as any privately, publicly or cooperatively owned line, facility, communication distribution system, 
power, electricity, light, heat, gas, oil, crude products, water, steam, waste, a stormwater and sanitary system or other 
similar commodities like wireless telecommunications, television transmission signals and publicly owned fire or police 
signal systems that directly or indirectly serve the public. The definition of Utility does not include the following (Exhibi t 1 
of the PA):  
• Streetlights and traffic signals; and 
• Intelligent transportation systems and intelligent vehicle highway systems facilities.  
An Undisclosed Utility is defined as any Utility Present on the Project Site that was not previously identified or was 
incorrectly shown, identified or described in the Disclosed information, in each case excluding any Utility that : 
• Was installed on a portion of the Project site after access was granted to the Developer to the relevant portion 
of the Project Site; and 
• Could have reasonably been identified or discovered prior to the Setting Date by an appropriately qualified and 
experienced contractor or engineer exercising due diligence and Good Industry Practice based on the Disclosed 
Information, any publicly available and/or reasonably discoverable information records and any access to the 
Project Site granted prior to the Setting Date. 
A Compensation Event will occur if any Undisclosed Utility is discovered during the Conversion Work period.  
If any agreement with a Utility Owner is expected to provide revenue with respect to the use or occupation by a Utility of 
a space in any Project Site, the revenue incurred shall be for the benefit of the District and not the Developer (Section 
12.4 of the PA).  
6.3.1  UTILITY ADJUSTMENTS  
The Developer will be responsible for (Section 12.1 of the PA): 
• Performing all Utility Adjustments necessary to accommodate the Work at its own cost and expense; and  
• Performing its obligations under the Agreement in accordance with the Technical Provisions.  
The District will be responsible for:  
• Subject to Applicable Law, providing the Developer the benefit of any provision in recorded Utility or other 
easements affecting the Project that require the easement holders to relocate at the expense of such easement 
holder; and 
• Assisting the Developer in obtaining the benefit of all rights the District has under any recorded Utility or 
easement affecting the Project, provided such assistance does not include the District’s participation in or 
initiation of legal actions. 
If the Developer has used diligent efforts to obtain the cooperation of a Utility Owner, as necessary for the Utility 
Adjustments, and the Utility Owner (Section 12.3 of the PA):  
• Will not undertake or permit a Utility Adjustment in a manner consistent with t he timely completion of the 
Work, or in accordance with Applicable Law, any Governmental Approval or the Project Documents; or  
• Will not cooperate in a timely manner to provide the agreed-upon work or approvals. Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
32 
 
Then, in addition to the above scenarios, if there is any other dispute that arises between the Developer and a Utility 
Owner with respect to the Project, the Developer must issue a notice to the D istrict that may include a request for the 
District to assist in resolving the dispute or in otherwise obtaining the Utility Owner’s timely cooperation. Following the 
delivery of the notice or assistance request to the District, the Developer shall continue to use diligent efforts to pursue 
the Utility Owner’s cooperation. 
6.3.2  BETTERMENTS  
A Betterment is described as the upgrading of a Utility in the course of any Utility Adjustment that is not attributable to 
the D&C Work and is solely made for Utility Owner’s benefit, at their request. The following are not considered 
Betterments (Exhibit 1 of the PA): 
• Any upgrading required for accommodation of the Project; 
• Replacement devices or materials of equivalent standards, although not identical;  
• Replacement of devices or materials no longer regularly manufactured in an equivalent or next higher grade or 
size; 
• Any upgrading required under Applicable Law; 
• Replacement devices used for reasons of economy; 
• Any upgrading required by the Utility Owner’s applicable design and construction standards; and  
• Any discretionary decision by a Utility Owner contemplated within a particular standard described in the 
preceding point. 
The Developer will notify the District if it receives a request from a Utility Owner to undertake a Betterment. The 
Developer is not permitted to undertake any Betterment without receiving the prior written co nsent of the District. The 
District will issue a District Change Request if the District requires the Developer to undertake a Betterment (Section 12.2 
of the PA). 
6.3.3  LICENSE AGREEMENTS FOR POLE ATTACHMENTS  
License Agreements are defined as:  
• The License Agreement for Pole Attachments in the District of Columbia dated as of January 12, 2021 between 
the District and Potomac Electric Power Company; and 
• The License Agreement for Pole Attachments in the District dated as of December 18, 2020 between the District 
and Verizon Washington, DC Inc. 
The District will provide the Developer with a copy of the following documents: 
• Each executed License Agreement, prior to the Setting Date; and 
• Any amendment to a License Agreement within 7 days of the execution of such amend ment. 
In the event of a License Agreement’s early termination, the District will inform the Developer in writing within 7 days of 
the early termination, describing the District’s next steps with respect to addressing the consequences of such 
termination. The Developer is protected against the termination of a License Agreement un der Compensation Event (m) 
in the PA.  
The License Agreements will accommodate all Work performed on Poles not owned by the District. We highlight that the 
Developer will not need to interface with Pepco or Verizon when performing the Conversion Work on the Poles not 
owned by the District as the License Agreements permit the Developer to replace the lighting fixtures without 
notification and approval.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
33 
 
The Expansion Work will be performed in coordination with the Asset Management Services and such risk will be passed 
through to the Asset Management Contractor via the AM Contract. The Expansion Work allowance is capped with 
limitations set per Calendar Year on the amount to be performed, and any unused amounts carry forward into 
subsequent years, including into the Final Calendar Year of the Term. Any further Expansion Work required by the District 
would constitute a Compensation Event under the PA. The Expansion Work limitations are  well defined in Exhibit 10 of 
the PA. The Asset Management Contractor has included appropriate allowance for the Expansion Work, which is  
estimated at $300,000 USD (index-linked), has conservatively assumed 100% of the Expansion Work allowance is utilized 
each Calendar Year for the purposes of its budget. 
 Conclusion 
Based on the information provided by the Developer, and our review of the PA, we are of the following opinion: 
• The Developer will be responsible for accepting the physical limitations and conditions of the Project Site and 
Work requirements following its Due Diligence and review of the Disclosed Information; 
• The Developer’s due diligence includes the standard market practise with investigations of the geotechnical, 
climatic, geological, hydrological, ecological, environmental and other physical conditions of each Pr oject Site 
based on the information’s provided by the District; 
• The Developer assumes the sole responsibility for the management, treatment, handling, storage, monitoring, 
remediation, removal, transport and disposal of any Hazardous Materials in, under or on any Project Site 
encountered throughout the Term; 
• The PA provides the Developer with appropriate protection against the discovery of unknown Project Site 
contamination or Undisclosed Utilities that constitute Compensation Events;  
• The Developer will bear all costs and expenses associated with the rectification of H azardous Materials on the 
Project Site, throughout the Term, with the exception of specific provisions described in the PA;  
• The Developer shall perform all Utility Adjustments necessary to accommodate the Work at its own cost and 
expense; and 
• BTY highlights that this Project does not include any anticipated geotechnical report or environmental 
assessment given that the Project does not include any underground works. The geotechnical and 
environmental risks are negligible.

 D&C Contractor’s Schedule Approach & Methodology and Schedule Assessment  
The D&C Contractor’s proposed schedule has been developed based on their experience with similar projects, historic 
data and input from regional subcontractors. We note the following: 
• D&C Contractor and its proposed subcontractors (namely, DCI, BConstrux and A. Wash and Associates) have 
experience with the relevant permitting authorities permitting bodies and view the permit requirements as 
achievable;  
• We highlight that the Project Baseline Schedule’s Critical Path should be viewed as the design, procurement and 
permitting works leading up to NTP3, which is approximately 4-months following the proposed Financial Close 
Date. To mitigate schedule risk the D&C Contractor plans to proceed with design, permitting and procurement 
at an early stage during the preferred proponent stage;  
• The current schedule contemplates a 6-month period from early works to NTP3 at which point the design, 
permits and procurements will be complete;  
• Following NTP3, the Project Baseline Schedule does not include an activity-linked Critical Path to Project Final 
Completion, but is broken down into 142 Street Light Bundles which are among the 8 Wards of the District; 
• The Project Bundles are divided so that they may be completed in 14-day periods, per Section 2.5(C)(iii) of the 
Technical Provisions; 
• With a target of three Street Light Bundles every two weeks, multiple Street Light Bundles will be completed 
each month.  Throughout the D&C Period the Project Baseline Schedule will be monitored on a two-week basis 
as the Street Light Bundles are completed and sign-off by the District and their Independent Engineer. A Project 
Status Schedule Update will be completed on a monthly basis, which will be built using a Work Breakdown 
Structure outline with each Project Bundle broken down into its respective Work components. The schedule will 
be resource and cost-loaded. The submittal and review periods are considered to be standard for this type of 
project. The schedule update procedure outlined in Section 2.3.1 of the Technical Provisions is considered to be 
well detailed and achievable. We consider this clarity to be an effective mitigant to ensure scheduling 
transparency throughout the D&C Period; 
• Should the Street Light Bundles lag in completion, the D&C Contractor has the ability to accelerate via adding in 
additional crews. Due to the nature of the Project, multiple crews can work unobstructed at numerous points. 
The schedule management should be viewed as a productivity check, which is naturally tested in two -week 
cycles in accordance with the Street Light Bundles’ specifications. Additionally, the D&C Contractor’s 
subcontractors have the capacity and capability to quickly increase the crew size should it be required ; 
• The D&C Contractor has discussed schedule with the proposed subcontractors and the local subcontractors  
have substantiated the achievability of the schedule;  
• The schedule may also be accelerated through additional shifts and working hours, within the applicable labor 
laws; 
• The schedule was developed using a productivity approach, using conservative estimat es based on historic 
installations on a per unit basis. These assumptions were confirmed using local subcontractors with over 60 
years’ combined experience working with the District; 
• The schedule was developed with the following labor assumptions: 
• seven two-man crews performing conversion works; Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
36 
 
• seven two-man crews performing rehabilitation works;  
• traffic management and other speciality labor such as arborist, historical consultants, etc will be available on an 
as needed basis;  
• Smart City Work, which will be fully subcontracted by the D&C Contractor, will include the completion of 2-3 
WAP installations per day; 
• The peak man-power is 50 approximately personnel, which will be fairly consistent throughout the D&C Period. 
We highlight that the majority of the trades are not “highly” skilled and that the subcontractors may easily scale 
up as needed. Labor will be locally sourced and labor supply is considered to be of minimal risk;  
• The current two main subcontractors are able to perform both the conversion work s and rehabilitation works, 
interchangeably. Therefore, should a subcontractor be underperforming the D&C Contractor has the ability to 
de-scope the underperforming subcontractor and up-scope other subcontractors if necessary to maintain 
required production rates;  
• The schedule is based on standard 5-day, 40-hour week shifts; however, the D&C Contractor is in discussion 
with the District to swap specific shifts to nights. This will enable less interfere with the public for high traffic 
areas;  
• The schedule includes standard holidays and weather delay days. We highlight the Technical Provisions provide 
generous schedule and Noncompliance relief for Severe Weather Events (Section 10.2.2 of the Technical 
Provisions); 
• The long lead procurement items have been identified, which includes poles and the luminaires. Due to the 
mass quantities required, the D&C Contractor has contacted major suppliers and manufacturers directly. The 
Project Baseline Schedule has been conservatively developed using quoted productivity capabilities from the 
manufacturers;  
• Luminaires, poles (including historical specialty poles), and arms have a lead time of approximately 4-months 
from time of order. The Developer is mitigating this in the D&C Period by completing the design early to get 
orders placed in advance of NTP3 and placing deposits to get in the supplier queues. Additionally, the D&C 
Contractor is directly placing orders with suppliers to ensure the process is managed and not relying on 
subcontractors for orders. For Asset Management Services, a similar approach is taken by Asset Management 
Contractor, initial material supply is being managed in the mobilization budget with purchasing completed 
directly by the Asset Management Contractor and storage for poles has been considered to keep a strong 
backlog of supplies available; 
• The current supplier for the District’s luminaires is Signify, with multiple distributions out of the US. Signify is a 
large company more than capable on delivering for this Project, but if the Developer cannot get commitments 
prior to award of the supply contract, there are multiple other vendors capable of completing the Project;  
• Poles/Arms are all sourced domestically, mostly in Wisconsin, and multiple suppliers have provided pricing. It 
should be noted that the D&C Contractor and Asset Management Contractor (both ENGIE companies) provide 
considerable leverage given their relationship and buying power with global suppliers;  
• The Developer is able to provide a rough order of magnitude estimate based on the known productivity rates for 
the suppliers/manufacturers and used this to size the procurement window (with additional contingency) ; 
• We highlight that the production rates during the winter period are slower due to the seasonal effect and safety 
during installations; 
• The D&C Contractor is self performing approximately 20% of the labor. The remaining majority of the work will 
be performed by regional subcontractors that have extensive work experience with the District;  
• The sequencing and duration of activities is considered reasonable for the size and complexity of the Project. 
The overall construction duration for the Project is reasonable; and 
• Given the simple complexity of the Work and the flexibility of the D&C Contractor to adjust productivity, we 
consider the approach and development methodology to the Project Baseline Schedule to be holistic and 
suitable for this stage of development. We consider the over schedule risks to be minimal.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
37 
 
 Substantial Completion Process 
The conditions for Substantial Completion of each Street Light and Smart City Bundle include that (Section 17.1 & 17.2 of 
the PA): 
• The Developer has completed the D&C Work, except with respect to any Punch List items;  
• The relevant Street Light Bundles is operational, meets the required Performance Requirements and all ancillary 
items are in place; 
• All commissioning procedures for the RMCS and AMIS are in place; and  
• All manufacturers warranties have been delivered. 
The Developer will must provide written notice to the District and the IE when it anticipates satisfying all of the Smart City 
Bundle Substantial Completion Conditions in respect of the relevant Smart City Bundle at least 15 Business Days prior to 
the anticipated date so that the IE can commence its review. The written notice will include: 
• A list of all requirements that will be achieved to allow the IE to issue the Certificate of Smart City Bundle 
Substantial Completion in respect of such Smart City Bundle; and 
• A proposed Punch List. 
No later than 10 Business Days prior to satisfying all Smart City Bundle Substantial Completion Conditions, the Developer 
will meet with the IE and the District to confirm the list of requirements is in accordance with the PA. The Developer will 
then provide written notice to the IE and the District once it has satisfied all of the Conditions. Within 15 Business Days of 
receiving such notice, the IE will inspect the Smart City Bundle and review documentation. If all the applicable Conditions 
have been satisfied, the IE will then issue a Certificate of Smart City Bundle Substantial Completion. 
This framework is considered reasonable and typical for North American P3 projects.  
 Project Final Completion 
The conditions for Project Final Completion include that (Section 17.3 of the PA): 
• The Project is fully operational; 
• Each of the following components of the Improved Street Light Network has completed a period of continuous 
performance of not less than 30 consecutive days: (i) One Street Light Bundle in each Ward of the District, (ii) 
with respect to each type of network communications technology incorporated into the RMCS, 10% of the total 
number of Lighting Units, (iii) 10% of the total number of “Posttop” Lighting Units, and (iv) 10% of the total 
number of “Cobrahead” Lighting Units (“continuous performance” means that the relevant component operates 
such that no Noncompliance Points would accrue if Performance Requirements and Payment Mechanism were 
in effect during such 30-day period); 
• RMCS and AMIS has been commissioned in accordance with TP Section 8 (Demonstration, Testing and 
Commissioning); 
• All ancillary items are in place; and 
• All manufacturers warranties have been delivered. 
The Developer will must provide written notice to the District and the IE when it anticipates satisfying all of the Project 
Final Completion Conditions at least 15 Business Days prior to the anticipated date so that the IE can commence its 
review. The written notice will include a list of all requirements that will be achieved to allow the IE to issue the 
Certificate of Project Final Completion. 
No later than 10 Business Days prior to satisfying all Project Final Completion Conditions, the Developer will meet with 
the IE and the District to confirm the list of requirements is in accordance with the PA. The Developer will then provide 
written notice to the IE and the District once it has satisfied all of the Conditions. Within 10 Business Days of receiving Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
38 
 
such notice, the IE will inspect the items on the Punch List and review documentation. If all the applicable Conditions  
have been satisfied, the IE will then issue the Certificate of Project Final Completion. 
This framework is considered reasonable and typical for North American P3 projects , and given the scope of the Project is 
considered easily achievable for the D&C Contractor. 
 Demonstration, Testing and Commissioning 
The requirements for Demonstration, Testing and Commissioning Work are detailed in Section 8 of the TP.  
The Developer will define Demonstration, Testing and Commissioning Plans (“Plans”) for Lighting Units and Systems, 
specifically the AMIS and RMCS. No Luminaire, Lighting Fixture or RMCS component will be installed without a defined 
Plan and execution of said Plan. The Developer will also establish a Program for Independent Verification and V alidation 
(“IV&V”) for all software or systems-related Project requirements, specifically the AMIS and RMCS. The Commissioning 
processes and Independent Verification and Validation (“IV&V”) process will be conducted by independent contractors at 
the expense of the Developer (Section 8 of the Technical Provisions).  
The Developer will meet the Comprehensive Demonstration requirements, which means that the Developer will 
demonstrate each LED Luminaire type in its proposal to the District and receives its appr oval and is a condition precedent 
to the Commercial Closing Date. The Comprehensive Demonstration protocol is described in Exhibit 7.   
This framework is typical for North American P3 projects. 
 Long Stop Deadline  
 
The Long Stop Deadline is the date that is 180 days after the Guaranteed Project Final Completion Date. 
It is a Developer Event of Default (or “Non-Completion Default”) if the Developer fails to achieve Project Final Completion 
by the Long Stop Deadline (Section 44.1 of the PA). 
It is a D&C Contractor Event of Default if Project Final Completion is not achieved within 90 days after the Guaranteed 
Project Final Completion Date (the “D&C Long Stop Deadline”). 
The Collateral Agent (“CA”) may provide a Step-in Notice at any time during any CA Cure Period or Event of Default. In 
relation to any Non-Completion Default, the CA Cure Period Completion Date is up to 270 days after the CA receives the 
relevant District CA Termination Notice, provided there are no outstanding unperformed payment obligations of the

Developer (90 days, plus up to 180 days if there is a reasonable prospect of achieving Project Final Completion within 270 
days of such notice). 
The Long Stop Deadline is considered short at 6-months duration compared with recent P3 projects in North America, 
where we typically see 12-month longstop date. However, given the (i) shorter construction schedule relative to other P3 
projects, (ii) the D&C Contractor’s ability to accelerate the schedule with no critical path after the achievement of NTP3, 
and (iii) the simplicity of the D&C Work, based on the schedule requirements outlined in the Technical Provisions, the 6-
month longstop date is appropriate.  
 Conclusion 
We note the following: 
• The Long Stop Deadline is considered short at 6-months duration compared with recent P3 projects in North 
America, where we typically see 12-month longstop date. However, given the (i) shorter construction schedule 
relative to other P3 projects, and (ii) the absence of a critical path after the achievement of the first NTP3 and 
the D&C Contractor’s ability to accelerate the schedule, and (iii) the simplicity of the D&C Work, based on the 
schedule requirements outlined in the Technical Provisions, the 6-month longstop date is appropriate;  
• The Developers risk related to project schedule has been transferred through the D&C Contract to the D&C 
Contractor;  
• The Substantial Completion and Final Completion framework outlined in the PA are considered to  be reasonable 
and typical for North American P3 projects;  
• The D&C Contractor has demonstrated a good understanding of the requirements and has proposed a suitable 
schedule for this stage of the Project; and 
• Given the simple complexity of the Work and the flexibility of the D&C Contractor to adjust productivity, we 
consider the over schedule risks to be minimal. The Project Baseline Schedule is well structured with well 
implied logic, based on a review of the PDF submit with the Developer’s Technical submi ssion and through 
discussion with the D&C Contractor. We consider the Project can be delivered within the timeframe allowed by 
the D&C Contractor.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project
