The luminaire specifications are stock equipment with minor infrastructural upgrades that are becoming increasingly 
common with major lighting manufacturers, e.g., wireless lighting control and f uture proofing devices.  
The construction pricing was benchmarked to two recently completed street lighting projects on the west coast. The 
projects were smaller in nature, but the assessment scaled up for quantities and regional adjustment factors were 
applied. The benchmarks were also adjusted to fit the specifications outlined in the Technical Provisions. In addition, 
prices were benchmarked for the specified luminaires, poles, brackets and various pole bases with Signify luminaires and 
other local manufacturers and found that the relevant range pricing falls in the mid to upper end as for the benchmark 
range for municipal grade street lighting budgets in the utilitarian to decorative category.  
The D&C Contractor has performed similar street lighting projects globally and has a strong internal pricing expertise. The 
D&C Contractor has acquired direct quotes from subcontractors, manufacturers and suppliers to supplement their 
internal data. The accuracy of the pricings is reflected by their recent experience. This provides additional comfort that 
the Project can be executed for the proposed price. 
 Costing Methodology 
We note the following pricing methods utilized, which are typical of construction procurement in North American and 
the P3 market. We note the following: Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
42 
 
• The D&C Contractor developed pricing in-house using a bottom-up approach with direct quotes from 
subcontractors, suppliers and manufacturers;  
• The D&C Contractor performed a formal request for qualification (“RFQ”) process where multiple vendors both 
regional and prominent vendors submitted price quotes for the materials as to minimize costs over the 15-year 
period;  
• Salaries and benefits, include standard burdens. The wages for the full-time equivalent employees are governed 
by the Applicable Law, being: 1) The Living Wage Act of 2006 and the Federal Prevailing Wage Rates (Exhibit 26 
of the PA); 
• The quotes received from the subcontractors are valid until 2nd Quarter 2022. The two major subcontractor 
quotes were confirmed to be held for Financial Close;  
• Appropriate contingencies have been added to commodity-based items given the recent fluctuations. A 10% 
contingency was applied to steel. We note that luminaires have relatively stable prices and have minor 
adjustments based on an 18-month cycle based on technology improvements;  
• The D&C Contractor has included pricing for the on-the-job trainee and apprenticeship participation during the 
performance of the D&C Work (Section 11 Exhibit 27 of the PA); 
• The D&C Contractor has based conservatively based their rehabilitation work based on the Asset Inventory and 
Condition Report provided by the District. In practice, the D&C Contractor will be able to utilize plate repairs to 
reduce costs of replacing entire poles throughout the D&C Period; and  
• The majority of the work (80%) is being subcontracted, which enabled the D&C Contractor to the expertise of 
the local subcontractors whom have worked with the District for over 20 years provid ing extensive regional 
experience and knowledge of local rates.

 Payments from the Developer to the D&C Contractor 
The Developer will make monthly progress payments to the D&C Contractor on account of the DBC Price on the dates 
established in the D&C Payment Schedule in accordance with the payment submission and certification process (Section 
24 of the D&C Contract).  
The D&C Contractor Payments will submit a Payment Application for a one calendar month period on the last calendar 
day of each month for the D&C Work to the Developer and the DBC Payment Certifier. The Developer, the D&C 
Contractor and the DBC Payment Certifier will review the preliminary Payment Application within five Business Days of 
receipt. Within two Business Day following such review the D&C Contractor will submit the revised Payment Application 
to the Developer. Within five Business Days of receipt of each complete and satisfa ctory Payment Application, the DBC 
Payment Certifier will issue a Payment Certificate. Within the payment limitations of the D&C Payment Schedule the 
Developer will cause the D&C Contractor Payments to be made within eight Business Days proceeding satisfac tion 
certification (Section 24 of the D&C Contract). 
The level of detail required to substantiate each payment and the timelines ascribed are achievable. The overall payment 
process is comparable to similar payment process seen across North American P3 projects. We consider the risks related 
to payment process to be insignificant.

 D&C Contractor Replacement Analysis 
We have calculated the premium cost to replace the D&C Contractor at various key points in the  Construction Schedule. 
We selected scenarios that would not be covered by supervening events, and thus not allowing relief under either the PA Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
44 
 
or D&C Contract. If we exclude supervening events, we consider that a worst-case scenario is a default by the D&C 
Contractor of its responsibilities under the D&C Contract resulting in a replacement of the D&C Contractor and 
subsequent delay to the Project while its replacement is sought. 
We acknowledge that there are various alternative strategies that could potentially mitigate the conditions utilized for 
our replacement scenarios; however, as we are trying to describe the worst -case scenario, we have chosen not to factor 
such mitigating strategies into our analysis. In particular, the assumed initial delay periods at time of replacement are 
assumed to carry through to completion of the Project by a replacement contractor, with no acceleration or 
opportunities to re-sequence activities considered. In reality, a replacement contractor would have the ability to 
accelerate the work by scheduling weekend work, double shifting or by re-sequencing activities.  
Our analysis considers the security package described previously as providing e nhancement monies available to the 
Developer for financial damages and mitigation of its losses. 
For our analysis we have considered the following cost categories that would increase the eventual construction cost on 
the Project realized upon the delayed achievement of original Project Final Completion. We consider that these premium 
costs, over the original Construction Price, are the additional costs to the Project against which the available security 
package monies should be tested: 
• The legal and technical advisory due diligence costs incurred during the run up period to D&C Contractor default 
and eventual replacement; 
• The costs for additional Developer personnel to administer the defaults and to arrange for a replacement 
design-build contractor. We have allowed salaries for 2 additional Developer personnel over the course of the 
replacement period; 
• The design team will be required to review completed work-in-place on the Project and develop a new tender 
package for potential replacement contractors to bid on; 
• The replacement contractor will have to mobilize and establish a presence on the Project as well as establish 
appropriate bonding and insurances; 
• We have considered the general economic conditions of the construction industry in Washington D.C. and 
potential inflation of construction costs from the bid submission until re-engaging with a replacement 
contractor; 
• Over and above the cost of escalation, we have considered that there will be an expectation of increased fee 
(profit) by the replacement contractor. Given the significant value of the Construction Price, we have considered 
that a range of 1% to 5% (depending on the scenario being considered) of the cost to complete would be an 
appropriate fee premium. We consider that as the cost to complete decreases over time, the premium charged 
by a replacement contractor as a percentage of revised contract price increases. We highlight that a profit 
margin is already included in the value of the Construction Price and so this fee premium would add to that 
already existing profit margin; 
• We have considered that prior to the actual incident of default the payable accounts for all trade contractors, 
suppliers, material men and direct labor are current. We have considered that in the event of default the 
contractor will not pay (or be unable to pay) a proportion of the payable amounts due in the month of the 
default. We have assumed the subcontracted portion of the previous month’s progress payment has not been 
paid by the D&C Contractor in the default month and so will be a cost to the Project. We have applied this to the 
direct costs only from the previous payment period;  
• We have considered that despite the event of default, all work prior to the event has been inspected and 
approved. However, we have allowed 10% of the previous quarter’s progress payment as a cost to the 
Developer to remedy any deficient work (less indirect costs); and 
• We consider that inserted delays as a result of the replacement process are not recoverable by the replacement 
contractor and result in the same delay to achieving Project Final Completion. We have therefore applied 
liquidated damages assessed by Developer over the full delay period.  
The tables below provide a description of the 5 different scenarios noting the short -term and long-term premium are 
costs that we anticipate may arise at the time of replacement or shortly thereafter.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project

We have analyzed the adequacy of the security from two perspectives: total and liquid. The total security adequacy 
evaluates the ability of the full security package to cover the premium cost to replace the D&C Contractor. The liquid 
security analysis looks at the adequacy of the D&C Contractor’s liquid security to cover the immediate costs (i.e. those 
required to be paid in the first three months following insolvency). 
In the event that the D&C Contractor defaults, the credit enhancement package outlined in this section is mor e than 
adequate to cover both the short-term and total replacement premiums in our D&C Contractor replacement scenarios.  
We note that the Developer has capitalized 100% costs (including debt service, Asset Management Services costs, and 
other costs) from equity contributions from the Guaranteed Project Final Completion Date to the Long Stop Deadline. As 
such, this structure does not rely on Availability Payments for the first six months after the initial Guaranteed Project 
Final Completion Date, reducing D&C Contractor’s liability for liquidated damages that would otherwise be payable if 
Developer was relying on Availability Payments immediately after Project Final Completion.  
The Developer will be including an enforceable holdback mechanism into the Design-Build Agreement that will be 
generally consistent with the following: If at any time during the D&C Period, should the D&C Contractor’s schedule 
demonstrate that the Project Final Completion Date will be delayed more than three weeks after the scheduled Project 
Final Completion Date, the Developer, D&C Contractor and LTA will meet to determine the additional costs of 
accelerating the D&C Work to meet the scheduled Project Final Completion Date, the amount of which will  be withheld 
from the D&C Contractor’s next monthly billing, (the “Acceleration Cost”) or provided as security by way of a letter of 
credit by D&C Contractor.  
The D&C Contractor will be entitled to invoice or reduce a letter of credit for the Acceleration Cost as work is completed, 
provided that if the schedule demonstrates that D&C Contractor will meet the scheduled Project Final Completion Date 
on time and to the mutual satisfaction of the Developer and LTA, acting reasonably, any remaining Acceleration Cost will 
be released to D&C Contractor. In lieu of liquidated damages linked to the Guaranteed Project Final Completion Date , the 
Acceleration Cost mechanism is considered to provide the appropriate incentive for the D&C Contractor to maintain 
schedule and achieve timely Project Completion.    
The proposed Construction Liquidity Reserve Account provides sufficient cash coverage of the  short-term premium 
identified under each scenario of our D&C Contractor replacement analysis.  
The scenarios tested represent a worst-case scenario in which the D&C Contractor is in default and fails to meet its 
obligations under the Construction Contractor resulting in its replacement. In our opinion, the likelihood of this occurring 
is extremely low given the strength and reputation of the D&C Contractor members who are best situated to deliver this 
Project given their knowledge and expertise. However, we perform this analysis to test the proposed security package in 
a worst-case scenario.

9.0 Asset Management Services 
 Introduction 
The Developer must deliver Project-specific Asset Management Services throughout D&C Period commencing on NTP3 
and the ~13-year Post Project Final Completion Period (Section 18.1 of the PA).  
The Developer’s responsibilities include Asset Management Services in accordance with the approved Asset Management 
Plan and Handback Requirements. Lifecycle requirements are considered to be minimal to none, and fall within the 
bounds of normal routine Asset Management Services.  
The Developer’s responsibilities include the following: 
• Ensure the Street Light Network is in full compliance with the TP, including that it achieves the  performance 
requirements (“PR”) detailed in Appendix 13 and maintaining Elements at the Minimum Acceptable Condition 
(i.e. at or above a “Fair” condition) for the duration of the Project Term.  
• Establish a self-monitoring and self-reporting program (and interface with the District’s work  order management 
system (currently Cityworks). 
• Monitor and manage the Lighting Units 24 hours per day, seven days per week.  
• Perform activities necessary, and as directed by the District, for Make Safe Work (which is the remediation of 
safety hazards to the Public), District Direct Requests and Administrative Redirect (where the Developer is 
required to redirect any existing or planned Work or perform additional Work) from NTP3 through the end of 
the Term. 
• Provide AM services for the remainder of the Term for the net increase in Lighting Units added to the Street 
Light Network as part of Expansion Work regime detailed in the PA (Expansion Work Allowance is 300 additional 
Lighting Units per Calendar Year, with a maximum of 900 Unused Units that may be carried  forward, and 450 
that can be carried forward to the final calendar year of the Term). 
• Except in the failure of the signal feeding into the WAP, which is the responsibility of the District, the Developer 
is responsible for the proper connection of power and grounding for all WAPs and Developer-installed fiber for 
underground root WAPs. 
• Ensure that each Project Element will meet the minimum PR set forth in the TP as of the Handback Date.  
The PR will apply to AM Work on: 
a) The Existing Street Light Network within 90 days following NTP3 and will be in accordance with Table Appendix -1 
(PR for Existing Network); 
b) The Improved Street Light Network upon Substantial Completion of a Project Bundle and will be in accordance 
with Table Appendix-2 (PR for Improved Network and Expanded Network) and Table Appendix-3 (PR for AMIS & 
RMCS); and 
c) Upon acceptance of Lighting Units in the Expanded Street Light Network and will be in accordance with Table 
Appendix-2 and Table Appendix-3. 
The Developer is responsible for all AM Work on the Street Light Network, with the exception of Lighting Units that are (i) 
specifically excluded, as detailed in the TP, and (ii) located outside the Project Limits and are owned and maintained by 
other parties, and for which AM Work is the responsibility of the District. 
AM Work will be performed on (i) the Existing Street Light Network from the NTP3 until the end of the Term, and (ii) the 
Improved Street Light Network and the Expanded Street Light Network from the time of improved or expanded Lig hting 
Unit installation until the end of the Term. 
The Assessment Management Services are as follows (Section 10 of the Technical Provisions):   Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project

 Assessment of Assessment Management Contract Price 
The Asset Management Contractor has developed the Asset Management cost model using a bottom-up approach with a 
combination of quotes from the two preferred major subcontractors from using historical data provided by the District 
regarding costs and volumes of replacements for components. The Asset Management Contractor’s methodology in 
developing the AM Contract price is consistent with their approach on other North American P3 projects and considered 
appropriate. We note the following: 
• The Asset Management Contractor’s in-house staff will consist of a team of 19 personnel;  
o Roles include:   
▪ Asset management lead 
▪ Planner / scheduler 
▪ Admin coordinator  
▪ Master electrician 
▪ Journeyman electrician  
▪ Approximately 7 personal for preventive maintenance and re-grouting 
▪ Subconsultants on an ad-havoc basis, which include civil engineer, electrical engineer, 
historical preservation consultant, arborists, etc.  
• we highlight that the systems utilized for the Asset Management Services are off the shelve solutions with well -
understood technologies. The specialized components for the AMIS systems will be subcontracted to a speciality 
service provider; 
• The blended profit and contingency is considered reasonable for the Project. The current profit and contingency 
figures are mixed into the price, to confirm range with Asset Management Contractor ;  
• The labour costs (i.e. personnel) and burden assumptions provided are within the reasonable range of 
expectations for a Project of this scope; 
• Given the regional climate, labor roles would typically only be staffed for 10 months of the year. The Asset 
Management Contractor’s staffing plan includes 12 month employment to increase staff retention;  
• Approximately 50% of the AM Contract price will be for subcontracted Asset Management Services;  
• The cost model falls within the anticipated range for pricing; 
• Shifts will be standard Monday to Friday say shifts for the in-house staff;  
• The subcontractors will have availability 24/7/365 to respond to the performance requirements needs outlined 
in the Technical Provisions;  
• The subcontractors’ yards are located within reasonable distance to timely make-safe any Noncompliance issues 
that may occur within the application rectification periods;  
• The spare parts orders and stock are based off historical replacements using actual data provided by the District;  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
51 
 
• We highlight that there are numerous stocks of different poles type; however, approximately 11 different pole 
orientations make up 98% of the total pole stock. This will allow sufficient inventory to be stored in yards to 
avoid long pole procurement lead times. The latter 2% of pole stock stem from historical poles which temporary 
solutions would be implemented while the poles would be place for special order;  
• Due to the condensed schedule, the Asset Management Contractor will mobilize within 4 -months of the 
Financial Close Date and will conduct their operational development during the preferred proponent stage. Early 
mobilization cost items will include: labor, AMIS development, vehicles, lease hold improvement, staffing and 
operational development;  
• The PA includes standard cost escalation based on the United States Consumer Price Index for All Urban 
Consumers;  
• The Asset Management Contractor plans to purse advance purchase order at the preferred proponent stage to 
hedge against any commodity based price risk, specific to the steel poles; and 
• Developer’s Asset Management Plan is well developed at this stage of procurement and outlines a detailed 
approach to fulfill the performance requirements outlined in the Technical Provisions. This detailed approach is 
reflected in the AM Contract price. 
 Handback Arrangements 
On the Termination Date the Developer at no charge to the District will (Section 19.1 of the PA):  
• Hand back the Project to the District;  
• Ensure that the Project meets all Handback Requirements; and 
• Transfer to the District all the Developer’s right, title and interest in and to the Key Assets.  
The Developer will prepare a Handback Condition Assessment for the Improved Streetlighting Network  two years before 
the end of the Term (the “Handback Period”). The Handback Condition Assessment will be completed at the latest of 240 
days following the commencement of the Handback Period (Section 12 of the Technical Provisions).  
The Developer will establish the Handback Reserve Account on the first Business Day of the Handback Period. Within 60 
days of delivery of the Handback Condition Assessment, the Developer will deliver to the District a report setting out its 
calculations of the Handback Reserve Amount, being the estimated cost to improve, repair, renew or replace the Project 
determined in conjunction with the Independent Engineer. The Developer will fund the Handback Account within 30 days 
of receipt of such calculations. The Developer may provide a Handback Letter of Credit in lieu of funding the full 
Handback Reserve Amount (Section 19.3 of the PA).  
The Handback Requirements are as follows (Section 12 of the Technical Provisions): 
• Each Element of the Project will meet the minimum Performance Requirements and the minimum Condition 
Rating. 
• All Elements not meeting such requirements will be repaired, replaced, overhauled, refurbished or rehabilitated 
as appropriate prior to the end of Term.  
• The Developer will coordinate all aspects of the Handback Work Plan with the District.  
• The final condition assessment is to be conducted by the Independent Engineer. The Independent Engineer will 
assess the entire asset inventory no later than 16 months prior to the end of the Term, thereafter, assessing 25% 
of the assets every two months following. The Independent Engineer will use the full condition assessment to 
calculate the Handback Reserve Amount based on the results. 
The Handback Work Plan is considered to be a Non-Discretionary Submittal required to be approved by the District 
(Section 19.2 of the PA).  
The Handback Requirements are typical of P3 projects and are achievable for a well -resourced Asset Management 
Contractor.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
52 
 
 Payments from the Developer to Asset Management Contractor 
The Asset Management Contractor will be entitled to receive the following payments from the Developer:  
• The AM Mobilization Payments – which is the sole payment to the Asset Management Contractor from the 
Financial Closing Date to the Project Final Completion Date. The payments will be made on a monthly basis 
within the fifth Business Day of the immediate subsequent month; and 
• The Total Post Project Final Completion Period Payments – which is a monthly payment outlined in Section 24 of 
the AM Contract and Exhibit 14 of the AM Contract. The Developer will pay the Asset Management Contractor 
within 45 days after its receipt of a properly submitted invoice. The payment will be calculated as follows:  
o Total Post Project Final Completion Period Payment = (monthly AM payment + monthly l ifecycle 
payment * INF – Unavailability Deductions – Noncompliance Deductions – Energy Performance 
Charge]. 
 Plans and Performance Review  
Prior to NTP3, the Developer will submit the Asset Management Plan for review 45 days prior to the beginning of each 
Fiscal Year. The District will jointly review and approve such plan to include changes to operating protocols, annual Asset 
Management Work and Renewal Work (Section 10.11 of the Technical Provisions).  
The Developer will submit three iterations of the Asset Management Plan, being the draft Asset Management Plan, final 
Asset Management Plan and the Asset Management Plan updates conducted on an annual basis  (Section 10.11 of the 
Technical Provisions).  
The draft Asset Management Plan will be submitted within 120 days of NTP1. The final Asset Management Plan will be 
completed within the following 90 days. This plan will be updated 45 days prior to the beginni ng of each Fiscal Year after 
NTP3 throughout the Term (Section 10.11(g) of the Technical Provisions).  
The Developer will implement the final Asset Management Plan which will include the long -term integrity and ongoing 
operational serviceability of the Project, specific replacement/refurbishment plans, key assumptions and annual cost 
provisions for which the Developer is responsible. 
Replacement of elements, equipment and systems must be consistent such that on the Termination date, each Element 
will have the minimum Performance Requirements and Condition Rating set out in the Technical Provisions. Handback 
requirements are typical of P3 projects and present no significant challenge. 
 Asset Management Security Package 
Security Details 
Asset Management 
Contractor Liability Cap 
150% of the average Annual Asset Management Contractor Fee, consisting of a 75% 
“in-contract” limit of liability (with reset mechanism) and a 75% termination limit of 
liability.  
Liquid Security  Asset Management Contractor Letter of Credit equal to 35% of the average Annual 
Asset Management Contractor Fee. 
 Asset Management Contractor Replacement Scenarios 
We have carried out an indicative analysis of the potential cost of replacing the Asset Management  Contractor. The 
intent of the analysis is to provide an estimate of the potential liabilities arising out of a default of the Asset Managemen t 
Contractor under the AM Contract and to demonstrate whether the levels of security proposed by the Asset 
Management Contractor are sufficient to cover the replacement premiums. We consider a replacement of the Asset 
Management Contractor to be a worst-case scenario against which to test the proposed security package.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
53 
 
We highlight this analysis as a conservative approach to test the Asset Management Contractor security package. In 
practice, should the Asset Management Contractor default, rather than replacing the Asset Management Contractor with 
a new Asset Management Contract, we would instead expect the Developer to step-in and manage the Asset 
Management Services itself for the remainder of the Term. Given that the Asset Management Contractor will self-
perform only approximately 13% of the Annual Asset Management Contractor Fee, we would expect the Developer to 
follow such approach and retain subcontracted services for the vast majority of the work.  
For clarity, liabilities accrued prior to the termination effective date (including Deductions) will be subject to the annual  
liability cap and liabilities associated with the termination itself, including costs of replacing the Asset Management 
Contractor, increased costs of performance and performance Deductions that are incurred by the new operator 
subcontractor after the termination effective date will be subject only to the termination liability cap. 
We have assessed the potential additional costs that would result from a termination event that would be attributable to 
the termination liability cap. For this analysis, we have made the following assumptions:  
• It will take three months to replace the Asset Management Contractor.  
• The Asset Management Contractor is removed from the Project upon accumulation of the necessary 
Deductions, outlined in the AM Contract. We assume the Asset Management Contractor termination event 
occurs within the first year of the Term, which we consider a worst-case scenario. In addition to the 
replacement costs in year two, we have further demonstrated costs to replace assuming replacements that 
occur in later years of the Term, in years 4, 7 and 10. These are provided to demonstrate how the total cost to 
replace declines as the term progresses. We have structured our analysis of the average Annual Asset 
Management Contractor Fee.  
• Allowances are included for procurement, Developer management, legal fees, and diligence fees. These will be 
incurred over the three-month period. 
• The original mobilization costs of the Assessment Management Contractor based on provided inputs are being 
carried at $450,000. 
• We assume that during the replacement period, Deductions are incurred at a poor level of performance while a 
replacement Asset Management Contractor is procured and phased in.  
• A premium applied by the replacement Asset Management Contractor of 10% of the Annual Asset Management 
Contractor Fee has been allowed for within our analysis as a premium for services that a replacement Asset 
Management Contractor may require to take over the Project.  
• Future premiums are discounted to net present value utilizing a discount rate of 2%.  
We note that we consider the occurrence of a default scenario of the Asset Management Contractor in the first year of a 
new contract to be highly unlikely. We would consider the occurrence of extremely poor performance leading to a 
termination event in the first year of the term as highly unlikely considering the broad experience and well established 
strong reputation of the Asset Management Contractor. 
The replacement analysis for the first year of replacement was developed as follows:  
  Default Year Replacement Year 1 Default Notes 
1 Asset Management Contractor Deductions $62,500 Conservative figure at 
this stage 
2 Procure new Asset Management Contractor $100,000 Spent over 3 months 
3 Developer management/advisors $100,000 Based on 3 months 
4 Diligence by District $50,000 Spent over 3 months 
5 Misc. Professional Fees $75,000 Spent over 3 months 
6 Replacement Asset Management Mobilisation $450,000 10% of original 
mobilisation amount 
7 
Replacement Asset Management Premium (10% of 
annual cost) $1,021,000 Premium for tainted 
project Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
54 
 
  Replacement Premium for 1st Year $1,858,500 18% 
 Letter of Credit $3,572,946 35% 
We highlight that a 10% premium is included in the initial replacement year, and 5% premium per year for the remainder 
of the term. As the future premiums are an additional cost associated with the replacement of the Asset Management 
Contractor, its yearly occurrence should be considered in conjunction with the initial cost of replacement. If we apply a 
5% premium to the Annual Asset Management Contractor Fee having fixed price risk and apply a discount rate of 2% per 
annum, we can summarize the long-term replacement premium at different points in the term.  
Long Term Premium Long Term Premium 
As % of Annual Asset 
Management Contractor 
Fee 
Default in Year 1 $6,060,000  59% 
Default in Year 4 $5,090,000  50% 
Default in Year 7 $3,260,000  32% 
Default in Year 10 $1,970,000  19% 
   
Considering the cost of future premiums for years following each replacement scenario, we highlight that the proposed 
termination liability cap to be provided by the Asset Management is more than sufficient to accommodate that total cost 
of replacement. In particular, we highlight that the 75% termination liability cap provides adequate coverage in the 
worst-case scenario in Year 1.  
 Conclusion 
We note the following: 
• The Developer’s risk related to the Asset Management Services is passed to the Asset Management Contract via 
the AM Contract; 
• Asset Management Services requirements are typical of the current comparable modernization projects in the 
P3 market. The requirements are reasonable and achievable for an experienced Asset Management Contractor; 
• Asset Management Contractor has a substantial client bank covering many locations throughout North America 
delivering a vast array of hard and soft services, to a wide range of diverse clients providing reassurance that 
they are more than capable to meet and manage the requirements of the service deliverables of this Project;  
• Lifecycle requirements are typical of the P3 market. The requirements are rea sonable and achievable for an 
experienced Asset Management Contractor; 
• Handback requirements are typical of infrastructure P3 projects and present no significant challenge;  
• The proposed security package is considered to be market and sufficient when teste d against BTY Asset 
Management Contractor replacement scenarios. We highlight that in practise we would expect the Developer to 
step-in as the replacement Asset Management Contractor in a highly unlikely replacement scenario event ; and 
• The AM Contract price demonstrates that the O&M Work can be provided for an annual cost of $10,208,418. 
The overall blended rate for profit and contingency is considered to be reasonable for this type of P3 Project .

Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
55 
 
10.0 Payment Mechanism 
 Introduction 
The Payment Mechanism is detailed in Exhibit 14 of the PA and describes the regime for Unavailability Events and 
Noncompliance Events. 
The Payment Mechanism process is considered to be more lenient when compared with similarly procured P3s in North 
America. The Payment Mechanism includes both Noncompliance Deductions during the D&C Period and Unavailability 
Deductions during the Asset Management Period. The Cure Periods are generous in length and the values ascribed to the 
Noncompliance Deductions are reasonably low.  
The Developer has passed the Payment Mechanism risk through to the D&C Contractor and Assessment Management 
Contractor through the D&C Contract and AM Contract respectively. 
 Payments 
The Developer will receive the Milestone Payments, the Smart City Milestone Payments and the Availability Payments 
from the District.  
10.2.1  MILESTONE PAYMENTS  
The four Milestone Payments are as follows (Exhibit 14 of the PA): 
• 50% Street Light Bundle Substantial Completion, for an amount of $21,970,000; 
• Project Final Completion, for an amount of $22,220,000; 
• 50% Smart City Bundle Substantial Completion, for an amount of $2,075,000; and 
• Smart City Bundle Substantial Completion, for an amount of $2,075,000.  
The Developer will not be entitled to early payment for Milestones should they be completed ahead of relevant 
Milestone Date identified in the Project Baseline Schedule. The District will pay the Developer such Milestone Payments 
within sixty days of receipt of a Milestone Payment Invoice that is reasonably satisfactory to the District (Section 24.4 of 
the PA). 
The Milestone Payment will be made in accordance with the following formula (Section 1 Exhibit 14 of the PA).  
MP = MMP – MPD 
where:  
MP    is the Milestone Payment;  
MMP   is the amount equal to the Maximum Milestone Payment; and 
MPD   is the Milestone Payment Deduction. 
The calculation of the Milestone Payments is in line with similar P3 standards and pose no undue risk from a technical 
perspective. 
10.2.2  AVAILABILITY  PAYMENTS  
Availability Payments will be made to the Developer on a quarterly basis (AP Q) following the Project Final Completion 
Date in accordance with the following formula (Section 3 Exhibit 14 of the PA).  
APQ = ((K x MAPQ x NF) + (1-K) x MAP+Q+)) – EPCQ – A1Q – A2Q – A3Q Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
56 
 
where:  
K   is the 43.00%, as bid by the Developer, representing the percentage portion of the MAP Q linked to 
Escalation. 
MAPQ  is the Maximum Availability Payment (quarterly), $25,668,465 (to be updated on the Financial Closing Date),  
value which includes costs necessary to design, construct, finance, and provide asset management services 
for the Improvement Street Light Network, calculated on a pro-rata basis as necessary.  
INF  is the Escalation Rate calculated by dividing the Escalation Rate in the preceding July divided by the 
Escalation Rate for the month in which the Setting Date occurs.  
EPCQ Energy Performance Charge is the amount by which the Actual Electricity Consumption exceeds the 
Developer’s Expected Electricity Consumption in the relevant Calendar Quarter, multiplied by the Set 
Electricity Price. The District will pay invoices from the relevant Utility Owner for Actual Electricity 
Consumption per Calendar Quarter during the Term.   
A1Q is the Unavailability Deductions (quarterly). 
A2Q  is the Noncompliance Deductions (quarterly). 
A3Q  is the Deductions (quarterly) that occur from the failure to cure any or all occurrences of such 
Noncompliance Events within parameter detailed in the section below.  
The EPCQ is calculated as follows. 
EPCQ = SEP x INF x (AECQ - EECQ) x Z 
where: 
SEP    is the Set Electricity Price ($0.061/kWh). 
AECQ   Actual Electricity Consumption, of the Improved Street Light Network, expressed in kWh.  
EECQ  is the Expected Electricity Consumption, of the Improved Street Light Network, expressed in kWh, as bid by 
the Developer. 
The District will pay the Developer such Availability Payments within sixty days of receipt of a Quarterly Disbursement 
Invoice that is reasonably satisfactory to the District (Section 24.4 of the PA). 
 Market Testing 
The PA does not require benchmarking and market testing. 
 Deductions  
The Developer will be subject to Deductions in accordance with the Noncompliance Events Tables and Unavailabili ty 
Events outlined in Exhibit 14 of the PA.  
10.4.1  UNAV AILABILITY EVENTS, A1 Q 
Unavailability Events occur during any closure that are not included as Permitted Closures or considered under 
supervening events, which apply to both the D&C and O&M Periods (Section 11.1 of the Technical Provisions). 
The Unavailability Events are calculated by multiplying the number of 15-minute intervals for each Unavailability Event, 
rounded up to the nearest 15-minute interval, by the Unavailability Deduction for the applicable Roadway Classification, 
and Peak/Off-Peak designation. Each event will be calculated on a separate and distinct basis. The Unavailability 
Deductions per 15-minuite interval during the Term are outlined in the table below (Section 4 Exhibit 14 of the PA).  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project

Permitted Closure shall not constitute an Unavailability Event, Permitted Closures include (Section 11.1 of the Technical 
Provisions):  
• A Compensation Event;  
• Conversation Work within a Project Site with a Traffic Control Plans respective that Project Site , which has been 
approved by the District. The permitting working hours are similar to those seen on comparable projects across 
North America;  
• Planned Maintenance or response to a Planned Outage for which the District has approved the Closure;  
• An Emergency or an Incident; or 
• Permissible Unplanned Maintenance.

The Permitted Closures schedule described above is considered to be more lenient for a dense metropolitan city.  
The Developer and the District are aware of the importance of managing and approving the Traffic Management Plans 
which are set to be completed by NTP2. This will ensure the site-specific traffic control plans are all pre-approved for 
Conversion Work and only will need periodic updates if regulations change in the Improved Streetlight Network term  
(noting this would be a Relief Event under the PA). 
The Unavailability Event Deduction calculations are considered to be appropriately prescriptive when compared to 
similar natured project in North America. The availability measurements are unrelated to the Lighting Units themselves, 
but are rather strictly related to roadway closures that are not Permitted Closures, which significantly de-risks the nature 
of this deduction regime. This Deduction mechanism and the deduction values are considered to be market.  
10.4.2  NONCOMPLIANCE EVENTS, A2 Q 
Two Noncompliance Events Tables are utilized for separate periods throughout the Project’s Term, being during the D&C 
Period and following the Post Project Final Completion Period.  
The Noncompliance Event Tables describe obligations which the Developer must adhere to. Failure to comply with such 
obligations will result in the accrual of Non-Compliance Points should the Developer fail to rectify such Noncompliance 
Event within the allocated cure period. The respective Noncompliance Points will be incurred again for every interval of 
recurrence that the Noncompliance Event should remain active.  
The Developer will incur a $1,000 (Indexed) Noncompliance Deductions per Noncompliance Point assigned.  
The D&C Period has 33 separate Noncompliance Events criteria and the Post Project Final Completion Period has 32 
separate Noncompliance Events criteria. The Noncompliance Points assigned range from 1 -4 per event and the cure 
periods 0- 10 Business Days.  
10.4.3  DEDUCTION A3 Q  
An additional $2,000 (Indexed) Deduction will be applied if Noncompliance Events should occur in the following 
scenarios: 
• Three Noncompliance Events occur in a single day, commencing on the occurrence of the fourth Noncompliance 
Event; and 
• Four Noncompliance Events occur in a rolling consecutive seven-day period, commencing on the firth 
occurrence of each subsequent Noncompliance Event in that seven-day period.  
This additional Deduction mechanism to is a common tool used by authorities across North America prevent poor 
overlying performance.  
Please refer to the payment mechanism calibration section below regarding commentary on the nature of the 
Deductions in the PA.

The performance oversight mechanisms are appropriately sized to promote reasonable performance for the 
requirements outline in the Noncompliance Tables and Unavailability Events. The Developer has set a 75% threshold for 
default event triggers between the Developer ascribed threshold in the PA and the Applicable Contractor threshold 
ascribed in the D&C Contract and AM Contract. This is an appropriate threshold providing the Developer with sufficient 
opportunity to make adjustments with the Applicable Contractor prior to triggering a PA default event.   
 Reserve Funding 
As a condition precedent to the District’s issuance of NTP1, the Developer will submit written notice to the District 
requesting the District to pay $250,000 into the Reserve Account, being the Initial Reserve Funding Amount. The Initial 
Reserve Funding Amounts to be paid by the District within 60 days receipt of such notice (Section 25.2 of the PA). 
Along with each Milestone Payment Invoice, the Developer will submit written notice to the Contracting Officer 
requesting that the District pay into the Reserve Account an amount equal to $1,250,000, being the MP Reser ve Funding 
Amount. 
Along with each Quarterly Disbursement Invoice, the Developer will submit written notice to the Contracting Officer 
requesting that the District pay five percent of the Availability Payment, excluding any Deductions, into the Reserve 
Account.  
 Supervening Events 
The PA defines supervening events as Compensation Events and Relief Events. 
If the Developer should become aware that there may be a delay to the Project Final Completion Date, the Developer will 
promptly within 10 Business Days of being coming aware provide notice to the District outlining the delay and specifying 
the reason for the delay. Within 30 days of submitting a notice to the District, the Developer will submit an estimate of 
the likely effect of such delay on the Project Status Schedule Update, including the impacts on the commencement of the 
Conversion Work or achievement of Project Final Completion. A Detailed Compensation Event Notice will be submitted 
by the Developer to the District within 60 days of the Developer becoming aware of such event influencing the Work 
(Section 27 & 28 of the PA).  
The Developer will use Reasonable Efforts to mitigate the delay and any other consequences of any Compensation Event. 
The burden of proving both the occurrence of Compensation Event and the resulting direct and adverse impacts lay on 
the Developer (Section 28 of the PA). 
The below table outlines the various forms of relief for each type of Supervening Event:  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project

10.7.1  COMPENSATION EVENTS  
If a Compensation Event occurs, the Developer will be entitled to the following relief (Section 28.1 of the PA): 
• An extension of the Guaranteed Project Final Completion Date or, following the Guaranteed Project Final 
Completion Date, the Long Stop Deadline;  
• Relief from compliance with its obligations under this Agreement; 
• Compensation for any Change in Costs that the Developer has incurred or will incur as a direct result of such 
Compensation Event; or  
• Compensation for any Finance Costs in accordance with Finance Costs during a District Delay Period. 
Compensation Events are defined as (Exhibit 1 of the PA): 
a) Any material breach of an obligation under the Project Agreement by the District;  
b) Any violation of Applicable Law by the District; 
c) Any Qualifying Change in Law;  
d) The issuance by the District of any Directive Letter; 
e) Any Required Action taken by the District in the circumstances District’s right to step-in without Developer 
breach;  
f) Any suspension of the Conversion Work, the Smart City Work, or the Asset Management Services that constitute 
a Compensation Event; 
g) Any material damage or interruption to, or interference with, the Work caused by any Governmental Entity, the 
District of Columbia Office of Public-Private Partnerships, the District of Columbia Department of Transpiration, 
the Office of the Chief Technology Officers, or contractors of any of the foregoing entities, or in the case of any 
additional attachments or fixatures, any Utility Owner or any third party, on or in the vicinity of any Proj ect Site;  
h) The issuance of any preliminary or permanent injunction or temporary restraining order or other similar order, 
legal restraint or prohibition by a Governmental entity of competent jurisdiction under Applicable Law that 
materially and adversely affects the District’s or the Developer’s performance under the PA;  
i) The discovery of any Hazardous Materials on or under any Project Site at any time after the Commercial Closing 
Date, including any Remedial Action related, but only to the extent that the presence of such Hazardous 
Materials: i) constitutes a Hazardous Environmental Condition; and ii) does not constitute a Developer 
Hazardous Materials Release.  
j) The issuance of any preliminary or permanent injunction or temporary restraining order or other s imilar order, 
legal restraint or prohibition by a Governmental Entity of competent jurisdiction under Applicable Law that 
materially and adversely affects the District’s or the Developer’s performance un the PA;  
k) Any a) amendment or variation to the terms and conditions of any District-Provided Approval after the Setting 
Date not previously agreed to by the Developer (including any variation to the terms and conditions of any Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
63 
 
extension or renewal of any final District-Provided Approval), as compared to those version of such District-
Provided Approvals provided to the Developer (including in draft form) as part of the Disclosed Information prior 
to the Setting Date or b) any invalidation or cancellation, in whole or in part, of any District -Provided Approval 
after the Setting Date;  
l) The exercise by the District of its right under the District’s right to uncover (Section 15.4 of the PA);  
m) A i) failure by a Licensor to comply its obligations under its License Agreement or any amendment thereto in 
such a manner that would result in a delay to the Conversion Work or Asset Management Services of more than 
30 days; or ii)any amendment to a License Agreement or termination thereof that materially and adversely 
affects the Developer’s performance under the PA; 
n) Knockdowns, where the Developer will receive compensation for Knockdowns exceeding the Knockdown 
Thresholds outlined in Exhibit 10 of the PA; 
o) The discovery of any Undisclosed Utility during the performance of the Conversion Work;  
p) Vandalism; 
q) The circumstance when the District directs the Developer to pursue one or more Expansion Directives in excess 
of the Expansion Work Allowance (Section 32.11 of the PA);  
r) The discovery of any Combination Pole for which the Developer is required to install separate conduit, as 
outlined in the Technical Provisions; or  
s) An act of Terrorism, 
provided in each case, the extent attributable to any breach of the Agreement, Applicable Law or any Governmental 
Approval by, or any negligent act or negligent omission of, a Developer-Related Entity. 
10.7.2  RELIEF EVENTS  
If a Relief Event occurs, the Developer will be entitled to the following relief (Section 29.1 of the PA): 
• An extension of the Guaranteed project Final Completion Date or, following the Guaranteed Project Final 
Completion Date, the Long Stop Deadline; 
• Relief from compliance with its obligations under the PA; 
• Relief from any rights of the District for Developer Default; or 
• Compensation for any Finance Costs during a District Delay Period as a result of the occurrence o f an Uninsured 
Relief Event. 
Relief Events are defined as (Exhibit 1 of the PA): 
a) Any Change in Law that is not Qualifying Change in Law; 
b) Any Force Majeure Event; 
c) Any flood; 
d) Any fire, explosion or earthquakes; 
e) Any tornado, named windstorm and ensuring storm surges, or unusually adverse weather; 
f) Any riot or civil commotion; 
g) Any blockade or embargo; 
h) Any industry-wide official or unofficial strike, generally affecting the lighting industry or a significant sector of it;  
i) Any accidental loss or damage to a Lighting Unit or Project Site not caused by the Developer;  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
64 
 
j) Any delay in obtaining any Governmental Approval to the extent that such delay is beyond the reasonable 
control of any Developer-Related Entity;  
k) Any failure by a Utility Owner to cooperate with the Developer in relation to a Utility Adjustment in such manner 
that would result in a delay to the D&C Work (as set out in the Project Baseline Schedule) of more than thirty 
(30) days; provided that the Developer will have continued to satisfy the Conditions to Assistance with such 
Utility Owner for the duration of such failure to cooperate by the Utility Owner;  
l) The failure of any Utility Owner having operational jurisdiction in the area in which any Lighting Unit is located to 
provide and maintain electric utility service to the Street Light Network upstream of the connection point to the 
relevant Lighting Unit required for the Developer to perform its obligations un the Project Agreement; or  
m) Compliance by the Developer with an order or direction of a Governmental Entity in an emergency, 
provided in each case, the extent attributable to any breach of the Agreement, Applicable Law or any Governmental 
Approval by, or any negligent act or negligent omission of, a Developer-Related Entity. 
If a Relief Event causes the Developer to fail to achieve Project Final Completion by the Guaranteed Project Final 
Completion Date, it will be extended. Similarly, if the Relief Event occurs following the Guaranteed Project Final 
Completion Date and causes a delay in achieving Project Final Completion, the Long Stop Deadline will be extended 
(Section 29.1 of the PA).   
Float will not be available to the District to absorb for delays caused by the Compensation Events related to the following: 
(a) Material Breach by District, (b) Violation of Applicable Law by District, (d) Directive Letter, (e) Required Action, (f) 
Suspension, (h) Safety Compliance and (k) Amendment to District-Provided Approval, provided the failure of the 
Developer to achieve Project Final Completion earlier than the Guaranteed Project Final Completion Date shall not be the 
basis of a claim for damages for delay. 
If a Party is unable to comply with any material obligation due to a Relief Event for a period of equal to the greater of (i)  
one hundred twenty days after the date such Force Majeure Event occurred and (ii) the maximum indemnity period for 
delay in start-up coverage with respect to any insurance the Developer is required maintain under the Project Agreement 
applicable to such Relief Event, such event will constitute an Extended Relief Event. If the Parties are unable to agree on 
appropriate terms to mitigate the effects of the Extended Relief Event, then either Party may deliver notice a 
Supervening Event Termination Notice to terminate the Project Agreement (Section 30 of the PA).  
If either party delivers a Supervening Termination Notice during the Design -Build Period, the PA will terminate on the 
date of termination stated in the Supervening Event Termination Notice. 
The PA provide reasonable consideration periods for the Party receiving a Supervening Termination Notice.  
10.7.3  FORCE MAJEURE EVENTS  
Force Majeure Events are defined as (Exhibit 1 of the PA): 
a) War, civil war, invasion, violent act of foreign enemy or armed conflict;  
b) Nuclear, chemical or biological contamination unless the source or cause of the contamination is brought to or 
near a Project Site by the Developer or a Developer-Related Entity, or is a result of any breach by the Developer 
of the terms of the Project Agreement; 
c) Ionizing radiation unless the source or cause of the ionizing radiation is brought to or near a Project Site by the 
Developer or a Developer-Related Entity, or is a result of any breach by the Developer of the terms of the 
Project Agreement; or 
d) Epidemic, pandemic, or Quarantine Restriction.  
If a Party is unable to comply with any material obligation due to a Force Majeure Event for a period of one hundred 
twenty days or the maximum indemnity period for delay in start-up coverage after the date such Force Majeure Event 
occurred, such event will constitute an Extended Relief Event. If the Parties are unable to agree on appropriate terms to Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
65 
 
mitigate the effects of the Extended Force Majeure Event or the Extended Relief Event, then either Party may d eliver 
notice a Supervening Event Termination Notice to terminate the Project Agreement (Section 30 of the PA).  
If either party delivers a Supervening Event Termination Notice during the Design-Build Period, the PA will terminate on 
the date of termination stated in the Supervening Event Termination Notice. 
The PA provide reasonable consideration periods for the Party receiving a Force Majeure Termination Notice.  
 Payment Mechanism Calibration 
The Performance and Measurement Table located in Appendix A & B of Exhibit 14 of the PA outlines performances 
standards and metrics for the D&C Period and Post Project Final Completion Period. Failure to perform within the bounds 
of the metrics can result in the accrual of Noncompliance Points and monetary deductions. The performance categories 
can be divided into the following event types: 
• Notifications; 
• Approvals and Oversight; 
• Project Delivery Protocols; 
• Reporting, Submittals, and Document Management;  
• Performance Requirements;  
• Safety; 
• Administration; 
• Lighting Performance; 
• Asset Condition; 
• Tree Trimming; 
• Appearance; 
• Emergency and Incident Response;  
• Response to District, Patron and Other service Requests;  
• Environmental Preservation;  
• Lighting Asset Inventory and IT Systems; 
• Wireless Access Points;  
• AMIS;  
• RMCS; and 
• Performance Standards. 
In addition, Deductions may be applied during both the construction and operations periods for lane closures, excluding 
Permitted Lane Closures. Refer to Section 10.4.1 of this Report for the Permitted Lane Closure details.   
We have carried out an analysis of the payment mechanism for the Deductions based on the monthly Noncompliance 
Points accumulation: 
Performance of the Work has been tested against three different levels of performance over a 12-month period. For our 
analysis, we have gone line-by-line through each of the Noncompliance Events and assessed the number of times per 
year that the D&C Contractor and/or Asset Manager may fail to meet the requirements constituting a Noncompliance 
Event. For each level of performance, we have then assessed the probability that the D&C Contractor and/or AM Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
66 
 
Contractor fails to rectify the Noncompliance Event within the applicable Cure P eriod. To illustrate, at the Poor level of 
performance, the D&C Contractor and/or O&M Contractor would be more likely to fail to rectify the Noncompliance 
Event within the Cure Period than it would at the Reasonable level of performance.  
We have performed our analysis at the following three levels of performance: 
• Reasonable: This equates to D&C Contractor and/or AM Contractor rates of noncompliance at about 0.5-1.5%. 
While we would expect that the O&M Contractor would operate at a better performance than this, it is not 
unreasonable to see failure rates at these levels. 
• Unlikely: This equates to D&C Contractor and/or AM Contractor rates of noncompliance at about 2-4%. We 
would consider this a “worst case scenario” based on the strength and experience of the D&C Contractor or AM 
Contractor. 
• Poor: This equates to D&C Contractor and/or AM Contractor rates of noncompliance at about 7-10%. It is 
unlikely that the D&C Contractor or AM Contractor would ever operate at these levels; however, we calibrate all 
of the payment mechanisms we analyze at this level in order to establish a relative ranking between projects of 
similar type. 
The table below provides the corresponding dollar values for Deductions in 4 payment periods, on an annual basis, at the 
performance levels described above. 
Deduction Category – D&C Period Reasonable Unlikely Poor 
Noncompliance Event Deductions $7,700 $25,400 $63,400 
Unavailability Event Deductions  $0 $42,000 $98,400 
Total Annual Deductions (rounded) $7,700 $67,400 $161,800 
NCE equivalent 8 67 162 
 
Deduction Category – Post Project Final 
Completion Period Reasonable Unlikely Poor 
Noncompliance Event Deductions $5,100 $10,100 $37,900 
Unavailability Event Deductions  $0 $10,500 $24,600 
Total Annual Deductions $5,100 $20,600 $62,500 
NCE equivalent 5 21 63 
Based on the above analysis, we consider the deduction thresholds/triggers in the PA to be appropriately sized and does 
not present a significant risk to the Lenders. At the reasonable level off performance described above, the D&C 
Contractor, AM Contractor and the Developer would remain well within any oversight threshold previously described.  
The D&C Contractor and Asset Management Contractor has provided contingency sufficient to absorb Deductions 
described in the poor scenarios above. We consider the deduction regime to present a low risk. This is consistent with 
other P3 projects in North America. 
 Conclusion 
We note the following: 
• The Developer has passed the Payment Mechanism risk through to the D&C Contractor and Assessment 
Management Contractor through the D&C Contract and AM Contract respectively; 
• Payment mechanism provisions are consistent with recently procured P3 projects;  
• The D&C Contractor and Asset Management Contractor has a wide range of experience on with performance -
based contracts; Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
67 
 
• The Payment Mechanism process is considered to be more lenient when compared with similarly procured P3’s 
in North America. The Payment Mechanism includes both Noncompliance Deductions during the D&C Period 
and Unavailability Deductions during the Asset Management Period. The Cure Periods are generous in  length 
and the values ascribed to the Noncompliance Deductions are reasonably low;  
• The Delay and Supervening Events are consistent with similar projects procured in North America and provide 
the Developer with appropriate schedule protection and debt service relief. 
• The Developer has set a 75% threshold between Developer default events and default with the Applicable 
Contractor. This is a sufficient threshold providing opportunity to make adjustments with the Applicable 
Contractor prior to triggering a PA default event; and 
• We consider the deduction thresholds in the PA to be appropriately sized and does not present a significant risk 
to the Lenders. At the reasonable level off performance, the D&C Contractor, Asset Management Contractor 
and the Developer would remain well within the oversight performance monitoring thresholds.

11.0 Equator Principles 
 Introduction 
The Equator Principles were established by the project finance sector to document a benchmark for the consideration of 
environmental and social risk factors during the due diligence process of assessing requests for project finance.  
The risk management considers business, credit, environmental and reputation risk. The Equator Principles allow 
financial institutions to assess, document, mitigate and report upon the particular project and its potential risks. Although 
the initial rationale for the Equator Principles was directed at finance for emerging markets and Lesser Developed 
Countries it has become best practice to consider the Principles for all projects. 
There are ten basic Equator Principles that were documented in 2013; these are presented following.  
1) Review and Categorization 
2) Social and environmental Assessment 
3) Applicable Social and Environmental Standards 
4) Environmental and Social Management System and Equator Principles Action plan 
5) Stakeholder Engagement 
6) Grievance Mechanism 
7) Independent Review 
8) Covenants 
9) Independent Monitoring and Reporting 
10) Reporting and Transparency 
The Equator Principles were developed with the active participation of the World Bank Group’s International Finance 
Corporation (“IFC”) and require projects to be categorized according to three levels of impact on the social and 
environmental landscape: 
Category Description 
A Potential significant adverse social or environmental impacts that are diverse, irreversible or 
unprecedented 
B Projects that are defined by the IFC as having potential limited adverse social or environmental impacts 
that are few in number, generally site-specific, largely reversible and readily addressed through well 
proven mitigation measures 
C These are projects that are defined by the IFC as having minimal, or no, social or environmental 
impacts 
We note that the IFC have typically categorized electronic modernization projects as Category C or, when delivered in 
non-OECD countries, potentially Category B.  
 D.C. Street Lighting Modernization Project: Application of Equator Principles 
The environmental and project approval processes for new social infrastructure projects in the United States meet with 
all the requirements of the Equator Principles.  
We consider that the proposed development will have a very low negative social/environmental impact. Si nce the Project 
will improve the on existing lighting networks and increases connectivity throughout the city , it is considered that the 
overarching social impact is positive.  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
69 
 
We are of the opinion that the Project should be designated Category C rather t han Category B for the following reasons: 
• The planning has occurred in an OECD country with well-established regulation; 
• No significant negative social impacts are perceived that warrant specific analysis;  
• Construction activities will be appropriately controlled by means of conscientious and well-developed regulations; 
the Project will be delivered by a team that is well experienced in meeting required regulatory process;  
• The Project is being delivered under the auspices of a state regulated authority and meeting state requirements will 
be high on their agenda; 
• Following completion of the Project there will be minimal new emissions and the potential for sanitary and storm -
water pollution will be remote. 
 Conclusion 
The appropriate categorization for the Project is Category C where the Project has “minimal or no social or 
environmental impacts” as defined by the IFC guidelines. 
  Prepared for Plenary Infrastructure DC | Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number V2.4 | March 31, 2022 
70

3.0 Project Description  
 
The  District  has  more  than  71,000  lights  installed  on  streets,  alleys  and  public  spaces.  Many  of  the  District’s 
streetlights currently use energy‐inefficient incandescent and high‐pressure sodium light bulbs. This project will 
convert the District’s streetlight bulbs to more reliable and energy efficient light emitting diode (LED) light bulbs. 
In addition, the project will modernize
  streetlights to include a remote monitoring and control system and expand 
the  District’s  broadband  WiFi  coverage  in  priority  corridors  to  serve  as  a  platform  for  other  “Smart  City” 
technology, particularly in undeserved neighborhoods. A private entity under a long‐term, performance‐based 
contract  will  install,  maintain,  and  repair  the  District’s  streetlights  an d  install  the  District’s  “Smart  City” 
technologies.

regulatory fines and penalties as well as credit monitoring expenses with 
limits sufficient to respond to these obligations.

12 Contractor’s Property

7.7 Property Insurance/Builder’s Risk 
 
The policy will, upon its inception, cover both builder’s risk and the operating property exposure, until such time 
as all the bundles have been comp
 leted.  The Developer has advised that there will be a series of bundles carried 
out, each with approximately 500 poles per bundle.  Upon the completion of this work, the policy would roll into 
a standard operating policy, which is, in our opinion, acceptable.  
 
 Cost of carrying financing i ncluded  in Delay in Start‐up / Business Interruption with a 12 month 
indemnity period.  The limits will be $25,000,000 during the D&C Work for DSU and a limit of 
$1,000,000 for business interruption.  Upon review of the penalty provisions under the Project 
Agreement with respect to the business interruption the Develope r has determined that $1,000,000 is 
their maximum probable exposure.  This approach is, in our opinion, acceptable.  
 Breach of conditions / non‐vitiation

policies.  
 
8.0 Availability of Insurance 
The insurance requirements during the DB period are achievable in the current insurance marketplace, though 
we do note that the breadth of the project‐specific cyber liability market has grown increasingly thin in the wake 
of the pandemic and recent cyber attacks. To mitigate this, Developer has carried an appropriately conservative 
premium  estimate  base
d  on  recent  project‐specific  cyber  liability  placement  costs,  and  should  this  market 
materially deteriorate after bid submission, plans to discuss alternative arrangements with the District consistent 
with the Uninsurable Risk construct set forth in the Project Agreement.

10.0 Review of Lenders’ Technical Advisor’s Report 
 
INTECH has reviewed the Lenders’ Technical Advisor’s Report, version 1.5. There is nothing contained within the 
report which will adversely affect the placement of the insurances.

11.0 Review of Design‐Construction Contract 
 
The insurance requirements are set out in Section 35 of Part H of the Design‐Construction Contract, the D&C 
Contractor  shall  obtain  and  maintain,  or  cause  to  be  obtained  or  maintained,  the  D&C  Contractor  Insurance 
Policies  identified  in  Exhibit  9  (Required  Insurance)  in  accordance  with  the  requirements  set  out  in  Exhibit  9 
(Required In
surance) and those additional insurance policies which are required to be obtained and maintained 
by Applicable Law (the foregoing insurance being, collectively, the “D&C Contractor Insurances”). 
 
The Developer shall obtain and maintain, or cause to be obtained or maintained, the insurance policies 
identified in exhibit 9 (Required Insurance) of the Project Agreemen t except to the extent that such insurance 
obligations are D&C Contractor Insurances or required to be provided by the Asset Management Contractor 
pursuant to the Asset Management Contract, the (“Developer Insurances”), provided that the Developer shall 
not be required to provide any insurance that is not required to be provided p ursuant to article 36 (Uninsurable 
Risks and Unavailable Insurance Terms) of the Project Agreement. 
 
During the D&C Period, the D&C Contractor alone shall be responsible for any deductibles, self‐insured 
retentions or retentions in connection with any claims made under or through: (a) any Developer Insurances; 
and (b) th
e D&C Contractor Insurances; provided that if any such claim arises in whole or in part by reason of an 
act or omission of the Developer or any Developer‐Related Entity, then the Developer shall be responsible for 
the deductible or self‐insured retention portions to the extent of its responsibility. 
 
During the D&C Period, 
 
(i) the D&C Co
ntractor shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of the Developer 
Insurances and the D&C Contractor Insurances in the event such increase arises from, or is a result 
of, (in whole or in part) the actions or omissions, or claims history, of the Asset  Management 
Contractor, any Asset Management Contractor‐Related Entity, the D&C Contractor or any D&C 
Contractor‐Related Entity; 
(ii) the Developer shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of the Developer 
Insurances and the D&C Contractor Insurances in the event such increase arises from, or is a result 
of, (in
 whole or in part) the actions or omissions, or claims history, of the Developer or any 
Developer‐Related Entity; and 
(iii) the  Developer  shall  be  responsible  for  any  increase  in  premiums  in  respect  of  the  Developer 
Insurances and the D&C Contractor shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of 
the D&C Contr
actor Insurances, in each case, in the event that such increase arises from, or is a 
result of, insurance market conditions generally or otherwise unless specifically specified herein to 
the contrary. 
This approach is, in our opinion, appropriate to protect the Fina nce Parties and the Project. 
 
12.0 Asset Management Contract 
 
The insurance requirements are set out in Section 35 of Part H of the Asset Management Contract, the Asset 
Management Contractor shall obtain and maintain, or cause to be obtained or maintained, the Asset 
Management Contractor Insurance Policies identified in Exhibit 9 (Required Insurance) in accordance with the 
requirements set out in Exhibit 9 (Re quired Insurance) and those additional insurance policies which are  
 21
required to be obtained and maintained by Applicable Law (the foregoing insurance being, collectively, the 
“Asset Management Contractor Insurances”). 
 
The Developer shall obtain and maintain, or cause to be obtained or maintained, the insurance policies 
identified in exhibit 9 (Required Insurance) of the Project Agreement, except to the extent that such insurance 
obligations are Asset Mana
gement Contractor Insurances or required to be provided by the D&C Contractor 
pursuant to the D&C Contract, the (“Developer Insurances”), provided that the Developer shall not be required 
to provide any insurance that is not required to be provided pursuant to article 36 (Uninsurable Risks and 
Unavailable Insurance Terms) of the Project Agreemen
t. 
 
During the Asset Management Period, the Asset Management Contractor alone shall be responsible for any 
deductibles, self‐insured retentions or retentions in connection with any claims made under or through: (a) any 
Developer Insurances; and (b) the Asset Management Contractor Insurances; provided that if any such claim
  
arises in whole or in part by reason of an act or omission of the Developer or any Developer‐Related Entity, 
then the Developer shall be responsible for the deductible or self‐insured retention portions to the extent of its 
responsibility. 
 
During the Asset Management Period: 
 
(i) the Asset Man
agement Contractor shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of 
the Developer Insurances and the Asset Management Contractor Insurances in the event such 
increase arises from, or is a result of, (in whole or in part) the actions or omissions, or claims 
history, of the Asset Ma nagement Contractor, any Asset Management Contractor‐Related Entity, 
the D&C Contractor or any D&C Contractor‐Related Entity; 
(ii) the Developer shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of the Developer 
Insurances and the Asset Management Contractor Insurances in the event such increase arises 
from, or is a result of, (in whol e or in part) the actions or omissions, or claims history, of the 
Developer or any Developer‐Related Entity;and  
(iii) the Developer shall be responsible for any increase in premiums in respect of the Developer 
Insurances and the Asset Management Contractor shall be responsible for any increase in 
premiums in re
spect of the Asset Management Contractor Insurances, in each case, in the event 
that such increase arises from, or is a result of, insurance market conditions generally or otherwise 
unless specifically specified herein to the contrary. 
This approach is, in our opinion, appropriate to protect the Finance Parties and the Project. 
 
13.0 Review of Term Sheet 
 
INTECH has reviewed the Term Sheet. Under Section 75 of Part C the Developer shall be required to ensure that 
all necessary insurance is taken out and maintained in accordance with the PA and such additional insurance as 
may be required by the LIA as set out in the report of the LIA provided to Age
 nts and in the Finance Documents. 
All  insurance  shall  be  underwritten  by  insurers  acceptable  to  the  Finance  Parties  and  the  LIA.  The  Finance 
Documents shall include customary provisions for a transaction of this nature with respect to endorsements and 
other requirements of the Finance Parties with respect to insurance policies. Notwithst anding the foregoing, the 
Developer shall comply with the requirements of the LIA as set out in its report provided to Agents, including any 
requirements in addition to those set forth in the PA. 
  
 22
In our opinion the insurance covenants in the Term Sheet are acceptable and appropriately protect the Project 
and the Finance Parties.

18.0 Insurers’ Financial Strength  
 
Section 35 of the PA states the following regarding Eligible Insurers: 
 
The Developer shall ensure that all Insurance Policies are obtained from insurers: 
 
(i) All  the  Developer’s,  the  D&C  Contractor’s,  and  the  Asset  Management  Contractor’s  insurance 
required in connection with the Project Agreement shall be written by insurance companies with 
an A.M. Best Insu
rance Guide rating of at least A‐ VII (or the equivalent by any other rating agency); 
and 
(ii) That are authorized to do business in the District of Columbia.  
 
In our opinion the insurer requirements are appropriate to protect the Project and we anticipate no difficulties 
in placing in
surance  with reputable insurers.
