20.0 COVID‐19 / Pandemic Coverage 
 
Under the Project Agreement an epidemic, pandemic or Quarantine Restriction is treated as a Force Majeure 
Event.  In the current insurance market there is no insurance coverage for pandemic / epidemic related claims, 
including supply chain issues. 
 
In  the  event  there  is  a  delay  due  COVID‐19  or  another  pandemic,  relief  must  be  fo und  under  the  Project 
Agreement, as insurance policies worldwide have excluded any pandemic coverage since the onset of COVID‐19.  
 24
 
 
21.0 Uninsurable Risks  
 
Section 36 of the Project Agreement states the following in respect of Uninsurable Risks: 
 
If a risk usually covered by construction all risks, third party liability, all risks property or statutory insurances, in 
each case that is required under this Agreement, becomes an Uninsurable Risk, the Developer shall notify the 
District promp
tly (and in any even within fifteen (15) Business Days) after the earlier of: 
 
(i) The Developer becoming aware that the risk is likely to be an Uninsurable Risk; and 
(ii) The risk becoming an Uninsurable Risk, 
And, in any event, at least five (5) Business Days before expiration or cancellation of any existing insu rance with 
respect to that risk (irrespective of the reason for the same). The Developer shall provide the District with such 
information as the District reasonably requests regarding the Uninsurable Risk. 
 
If  both  Parties,  agree,  or  it  is  determined  pursuant  to  the  Dispute  Resolution  Procedures,  that  a   risk  is  an 
Uninsurable Risk, the District and the Developer shall consider in Good Faith: 
 
(i) Alternative  insurance  packages  and  programs  that  provide  coverage  as  comparable  to  that 
contemplated  in  Article  35  (Insurance  and  Reinstatement)  as  is  possible  under  then‐existing 
insurance market conditions; and 
(ii) Other means by which the risk should  be managed or shared (including considering the issue of self‐
insurance by either Party). 
If the District and the Developer do not agree on how to manage or share the relevant Uninsurable Risk withing 
ten (10) days of the date on which the Developer provides notices under Section 36. 1(a) (Uninsurable Risks), the 
District  may  refer  the  matter  to  a  mediator  acceptable  to  the  District  and  the  Developer  (acting  reasonably) 
instead of using the Dispute Resolution Procedures. 
 
22.0 Insurance Benchmarking under the Project Agreement 
 
The Project Agreement contains no provisions with respect to the benchmarking of insurance premiums.  As the 
Developer will be carrying the insurance budget for their insurances, the risk lies with the Developer to maintain.  
The  Developer  has  budgeted  for  increases  in  insurance,  as  well  as  inflation,  to  insulate  them  from  this  risk 
throug
hout the project term.  This approach is, in our opinion, acceptable.  
 
In  the  event  that  the  Asset  Manager  or  D&C  Contractor  are  directly  responsible  to  cause  an  increase  in  the 
insurance premiums, this will be dropped down to them accordingly, as is customary.  
   
 25
 
 
23.0 Supervening Events under the Project Agreement 
23.1 Compensation Event 
 
The Compensation Events are defined in Exhibit 1 of the PA as shown below: 
Type of Event  Insurable? 
(a) any material breach of an obligation under this 
Agreement by the District, including without limitation, 
any breach by the District of: 
(i) Article 7 (Review of Submittals); 
(ii) Section 8.3 (Access to Project Sites); 
(iii) Section 12.1(b) (Utility Adjustments); 
(iv) Section 12.3(b) (Failure of Utility Owners to 
Cooperate); and 
(v) Article 16 (Notices to Proceed); 
 
No 
(b) any violation of Applicable Law by the District;  No 
(c) any Qualifying Change in Law;  Potentially:  In  the  event  of  damage  or 
destruction  to  the  Project,  coverage  is 
available  for  the  increased  costs  of 
construction due to the enforcement of a by‐
law 
(d) the issuance by the District of any Directive Letter;  No 
(e) any Required Action taken by the District in the 
circumstances described in Section 50.4 (Step‐In 
without Developer Breach); 
No 
(f) any suspension of the Conversion Work, the Smart City 
Work, or the Asset Management Services that 
constitutes a Compensation Event pursuant to 
Section 15.3 (Suspension of Conversion Work and/or 
Smart City Work) or Section 18.5 (Suspension of Asset 
Management Services); 
No 
(g) any material damage or interruption to, or interference 
with, the Work caused by any Governmental Entity, the 
District of Columbia Office of Public‐Private 
Partnerships, the District of Columbia Department of 
Transportation, the Office of the Chief Technology 
Officers, or contractors of any of the foregoing entities, 
or in the case of any attachments or fixtures pursuant to 
Section 8.5 (Accommodation of Additional Attachments 
and Fixtures), any Utility Owner or any third party, on or 
in the vicinity of any Project Site; 
No 
  
 26
(h) the issuance by the District of any Safety Compliance 
Order that does not arise as a direct result of any 
Developer‐Related Entity’s failure to comply with one or 
more Safety Standards; 
No 
(i) the discovery of any Hazardous Materials on or under 
any Project Site at any time after the Commercial

of competent jurisdiction under Applicable Law that

(k) any (i) amendment or variation to the terms and 
conditions of any District‐Provided Approval after the 
Setting Date not previously agreed to by the Developer 
(including any variation to the terms and conditions of 
any extension or renewal of any final District‐Provided 
Approval), as compared to those versions of such 
District‐Provided Approvals provided to the Developer 
(including in draft form) as part of the Disclosed 
Information prior to the Setting Date or (ii) any 
invalidation or cancellation, in whole or in part, of any 
District‐Provided Approval after the Setting Date; 
No 
(l) the exercise by the District of its right under Section 
15.4(b) (Right to Uncover) in the circumstances 
described in Section 15.4(e) (Right to Uncover); 
No 
(m) a (i) failure by a Licensor to comply with its obligations 
under its License Agreement or any amendment thereto 
in such a manner that would result in a delay to the 
Conversion Work (as set out in the Project Baseline 
Schedule) or the Asset Management Services (as set out 
in the Renewal Work Schedule) of more than thirty (30) 
days; provided that the Developer shall have continued 
to satisfy the Conditions to Assistance with Licensor for 
the duration of such failure to cooperate by the Licensor 
or (ii) any amendment to a License Agreement or 
termination thereof that materially and adversely 
affects the Developer’s performance under this 
Agreement; 
No  
 27
(n) Knockdowns;  Potentially:  Physical  loss  or  damage  is 
covered  under  Builders  Risk  and  Property 
insurance  policies  subject  to  standard 
exclusions 
(o) the discovery of any Undisclosed Utility during the 
performance of the Conversion Work; 
No 
(p) Vandalism;  Partially:  Physical  damage  resulting  is 
insurable  under  Builders  Risk  and  Property 
insurance policies 
(q) the circumstance described as a Compensation Event in 
Section 32.11(e) (Expansion of Street Light Network); 
No 
(r) the discovery of any Combination Pole for which the 
Developer is required to install separate conduit 
pursuant to Section 1.4.7(c) (Combination Poles) of the 
Technical Provisions; or 
No 
(s) an act of Terrorism,  Yes:  Terrorism  coverage  is  commercially 
available  under  a  Builders  Risk,  Property 
and/or  standalone  insurance  policy.  The  
Developer will not be placing coverage since 
terrorism is considered  Compensation Event 
under  the  PA,  which  is,  in  our  opinion 
acceptable. 
23.2 Relief Events 
 
The Relief Events are defined in Exhibit 1 of the PA as shown below: 
 
Type of Event  Insurable? 
(a) any Change in Law that is not a Qualifying Change in 
Law; 
No 
(b) any Force Majeure Event;  See Force Majeure below (Section 19.3)  
(c) any flood;  Yes: Flood coverage is available under a 
Builders Risk and/or standalone Flood 
insurance policy 
(d) any fire, explosion or earthquakes;  Yes: Insurable under Builders Risk and 
Property insurance policies 
(e) any tornado, named windstorm and ensuing storm 
surges, or unusually adverse weather; 
Yes: Insurable under Builders Risk and 
Property insurance policies 
(f) any riot or civil commotion;  Partially:  Physical  damage  resulting  is 
insurable  under  Builders  Risk  and  Property 
insurance policies 
(g) any blockade or embargo;  No  
(h) any industry‐wide official or unofficial strike, generally 
affecting the lighting industry or a significant sector of 
it; 
No  
 28
(i) any accidental loss or damage to a Lighting Unit or 
Project Site not caused by the Developer; 
Yes:  Physical  loss  or  damage  to  the  Project 
Site  and  roads  servicing  the  site  may  be 
covered  under  a  Property  and/or  Builders 
Risk policy. 
(j) any delay in obtaining any Governmental Approval to 
the extent that such delay is beyond the reasonable 
control of any Developer‐Related Entity; 
No 
(k) any failure by a Utility Owner to cooperate with the 
Developer in relation to a Utility Adjustment in such 
manner that would result in a delay to the D&C Work 
(as set out in the Project Baseline Schedule) of more 
than thirty (30) days; provided that the Developer shall 
have continued to satisfy the Conditions to Assistance 
with such Utility Owner for the duration of such failure 
to cooperate by the Utility Owner; 
No 
(l) the failure of any Utility Owner having operational 
jurisdiction in the area in which any Lighting Unit is 
located to provide and maintain electric utility service to 
the Street Light Network upstream of the connection 
point to the relevant Lighting Unit required for the 
Developer to perform its obligations under this 
Agreement; or 
No 
(m) compliance by the Developer with an order or direction 
of a Governmental Entity in an Emergency, 
No 
  
Except  in  each  case,  to  the  extent  attributable  to  any  breach  of  this  Agreement,  Applicable  Law  or  any 
Governmental Approval by, or any negligent act or negligent omission of, a Developer‐Related Entity. 
 
Importantly, we note that any Relief Event that is not covered by insurance required by the Project Agreement 
entitles Developer to recover F
inance Costs, per the definition of “Uninsured Relief Event”, which is defined as 
follows: 
A Relief Event for which insurance is not available to the Developer in the worldwide insurance or reinsurance 
market on terms required in this Agreement with reputable insurers of good standing. 
23.3 Force Majeure 
 
The
 Force Majeure Event are defined in Exhibit 1 of the Project Agreement as shown below: 
 
Type of Event  Insurable? 
(a) war, civil war, invasion, violent act of foreign enemy or 
armed conflict; 
No  
(b) nuclear, chemical or biological contamination unless the 
source or cause of the contamination is brought to or 
near a Project Site by the Developer or a Developer‐
No  
 29
Related Entity, or is a result of any breach by the 
Developer of the terms of this Agreement; 
(c) ionizing radiation unless the source or cause of the 
ionizing radiation is brought to or near a Project Site by 
the Developer or a Developer‐Related Entity, or is as a 
result of any breach by the Developer of the terms of 
this Agreement; or 
No 
(d) epidemic, pandemic, or Quarantine Restriction.  No 
 
   
 30
 
24.0 Conclusion 
 
The insurance requirements contained in the Project Agreement contain the scope of coverage, limits and quality 
of insurers appropriate to protect the Project and are in line with other project agreements of this scope, when 
the additional requirements set out in sections 7 and 15 of this report are incorporated into the policies. 
 
The
 Developer has confirmed they will add the additional extensions and Lenders protection to the policies, as 
recommended in this report herein.  We can confirm that the Lenders will be appropriately protected under the 
policies, once bound. 
Accordingly, we recommend proceeding to bid submission. 
We would be happy to review our comments with any of the interested parties. 
 
Yours truly, 
 
 
INT
ECH Risk Management GmbH

DISTRICT OF COLUMBIA

CONTINUING DISCLOSURE AGREEMENT 
This Continuing Disclosure Agreement (the "Agreement") dated May 5, 2022 is executed and delivered by 
the District of Columbia, a body corporate and politic of the District of Columbia, existing under the Constitution and 
laws of the United States of America, in connection with the issuance by the District of Columbia, a body corporate 
and politic (the "Bond Issuer") of (a) the District of Columbia Priv ate Activity Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street 
Lighting Project), Series 2022A (Tax-Exempt, AMT) (Green Bonds), in an aggregate principal amount of 
$144,485,000 (the “ Series 2022A Bonds ”), and (b) the District of Columb ia Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street 
Lighting Project), Series 2022B (Federally Taxable) (Green Bonds), in an aggregate principal amount of $15,210,000 
(the “Series 2022B Bonds,” and together with the Series 2022A Bonds and any Additional Parity Bonds issued under 
this Bond Indenture, the “Bonds”). 
Section 1. Purpose of the Agreement.   This Agreement is being execut ed and delivered by the District 
for the benefit of the Beneficial Owners (as defined below) of the Bonds and in order to assist the Underwriters (as 
defined below) in complying with the Rule (as defined below) by undertaking to provide certain annual financial 
information and material event notices required by the Rule (collectively, "Continuing Disclosure"). 
Section 2. Definitions.  Unless otherwise indicated next to each capitalized term, the following 
capitalized terms shall have the following meanings: 
"Beneficial Owner" shall mean, while the Bonds are held in a book-entry only system, the actual purchaser 
of each Bond, the ownership interest of which is to be recorded on the records of the direct and indirect participants 
of DTC, and otherwise shall mean the holder of Bonds. 
"Bond Counsel" means Orrick, Herrington & Sutcliffe LLP or such other firm of attorneys designated by the 
Mayor, the experience in matters relating to the issuance of obligations by states and their political subdivisions of 
which is nationally recognized. 
"Bond Indenture" shall mean the Trust Indenture dated May 1, 2022, between the Bond Issuer and the Bond 
Trustee, as it may be amended or supplemented from time to time. 
"Bond Issuer" shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof. 
"Bond Resolution" shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof.  
"Bond Trustee" shall mean U.S. Bank Trust Company, National Association, in its capacity as trustee under 
the Indenture. 
"Bondholder" of a Bond means the registered owner of such Bond as shown in the registration records of 
the Bond Trustee.   J-1-2  
 
 
"Bonds" shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof. 
"Borrower " shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof and for the avoidance of 
doubt, shall include its permitted successors and assigns.  
"Business Day" means any day other than a Saturday, a Sunday or a day on which offices of the United 
States government or the District of Co lumbia are authorized to be closed or on which commercial banks in New 
York, New York, or the city and state in which the Bond Trustee or the Collateral Agent, as applicable, is located are 
authorized or required by law, regulation or executive order to be closed. 
"Collateral Agency Agreement " shall mean the Collateral Agency Agreement dated as of May 5, 2022, 
between the Collateral Agent, the Securities Intermediary, the Bond Trustee and the Borrower, as amended, restated, 
supplemented or otherwise modified from time to time. 
"Commission" shall mean the United States Securities an d Exchange Commission, or any successor body 
thereto. 
"Dissemination Agent" means Digital Assurance Certification, LLC, or any successor Dissemination Agent 
designated in writing by the District. 
"District" shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof. 
"DTC" means The Depository Trust Company, New York, New York, and its successors and assigns. 
"EMMA" shall mean the Electronic Municipal Market Access system and the EMMA Continuing Disclosure 
Service of MSRB, or any successor thereto approved by the SEC, as a repository for municipal continuing disclosure 
information pursuant to the Rule. 
"Exchange Act" shall mean the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. 
"Listed Events" shall mean any of the events listed in Section 4(a) of this Agreement. 
"Loan Agreement" shall mean the Loan Agreement, dated as of May 1, 2022, by and among the Bond Issuer, 
as lender, and the Borrower, as borrower, as it may be amended or supplemented from time to time. 
"MSRB" shall mean the Municipal Secur ities Rulemaking Board, or any succes sor thereto. On July 1, 2009, 
the MSRB became the sole repository to which the Borrower must electronically submit Annual and Quarterly Reports 
pursuant to Section 3 hereof and material event notices pursuant to Section 4 hereof. Reference is made to Securities 
and Exchange Commission Release No. 34-59062, December  5, 2008 (the "Release") relating to EMMA, which 
became effective on July 1, 2009. To the extent applicable to this Agreement,  the District shall comply with the 
Release and with EMMA, as amended or supplemented from time to time. As of the date of this
 Agreement, the 
address and telecopy numbers of the MSRB are as follows: 
Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board 1900 
Duke Street, Suite 600 
Alexandria, Virginia 22314 
Tel: (703) 797-6600 
Fax: (703) 797-6700 
"Official Statement" shall mean the Official Statement dated April 12, 2022 prepared in connection with the 
offering and sale of the Bonds.  
"Person" means any natural person, firm, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, state, political 
subdivision of any state, other public body or other organization or association.  J-1-3  
 
 
"Project" shall have the meaning assigned to it in the Official Statement. 
"Project Agreement" shall mean, the Project Agreement, dated March 7, 2022, between the District and the 
Borrower, and all attachments, exhibits and schedules thereto, as may be further amended or supplemented from time 
to time. 
"Rule" shall mean Rule 15c2-12 adopted by the Commission under the Exchange Act, as the same may be 
amended from time to time. 
“Series 2022A Bonds” shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof.  
“Series 2022B Bonds” shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof. 
"Underwriters" shall mean TD Securities (USA) LLC and Wells Fargo Bank, National Association. 
Section 3. Annual Financial Information.  
(a) Commencing with the fiscal year ending September 30, 2022, the District shall provide to the MSRB 
no later than February 28, 2023, and no later than each succeeding February 28 thereafter, audited financial statements 
prepared in accordance with generally accepted accounting principles ("Annual Financial Information").  If the audited 
financial statements are not available by the date of the required filing, the District shall provide audited financial 
statements, when and if available to the MSRB.  
(b) The Annual Financial Information may be included by specific reference to other documents 
available to the public on the MSRB internet website (presently www.msrb.org) or filed with the SEC. 
(c) The District shall provide, or have the Dissemination Agent provide on its behalf, in a timely manner 
to the MSRB, notice specifying any failure of the District to provide the Annual Disclosure by the date specified. 
Section 4. Reporting of Material Events.   
(a) So long as any Bonds are outstanding, The District shall provide  the Dissemination Agent for filing 
with the MSRB by and through EMMA in a timely manner not in excess of ten (10) Business Days after the occurrence 
of the event, notice of the occurrence of any of the following events with respect to the Bonds: 
(1)  Bankruptcy, insolvency, receivership or similar event of the District;  
(2) The consummation of a merger, consolidation, or acquisition involving the District or the 
sale of all or substantially all of the assets of the District, other than in the ordinary course 
of business, the entry into a definitive agr eement to undertake such an action or the 
termination of a definitive agreement relating to any such actions, other than pursuant to 
its terms, if material; 
(3) incurrence of a financial obligation of the District, if material, or agreement to covenants, 
events of default, remedies, priority rights, or other similar terms of a financial obligation 
of the District, any of which affect security holders, if material; and 
(4) default, event of acceleration, termination even t, modification of terms, or other similar 
events under the terms of a financial obligation of the District, any of which reflect financial 
difficulties. 
Section 5. Report by Dissemination Agent .  Concurrently with the delivery to the MSRB of any 
information required pursuant to Sections 3 and 4 above, the Dissemination Agent  shall provide evidence to the 
District  (which evidence may be in the form of a confirmation email from EMMA confirming submission) that it has 
filed such information with the MSRB.  J-1-4  
 
 
Section 6. Termination of Reporting Obligation . The District obligations under this Agreement shall 
terminate upon the defeasance, prior redemption or payment in full of all of the Bonds. The District shall provide or 
cause to be provided to the Dissemination Agent with written notice that their obligations under this Agreement have 
terminated and a written request that the Dissemination Agent file a copy of such notice with the MSRB.  
Section 7. Amendment.  The District may modify its obligations hereunder without the consent of the 
Bondholders, provided that this Agreement as so modified complies with the Rule as it exists at the time of 
modification.  The District, or the Dissemination Agent, on its  behalf, shall within a reasonable time thereafter send 
to the MSRB a description of such modification(s).  In addition, the District may modify or terminate its obligations 
hereunder without the consent of the Bondholders, provided that such modification or termination is given effect to 
comply with regulations of the SEC or the MSRB in co nnection with the imposition of continuing disclosure 
requirements replacing in whole or in part the provisions of the Rule. 
(a)  If the District fails to comply with any covenant or obligation regarding Continuing 
Disclosure specified in this Agreement, any Bondholde r or beneficial owner of Bonds then outstanding may, 
by notice to the District, proceed to
 protect and enforce its rights and the rights of the Bondholders and 
beneficial owners of the Bonds by an  action for specific performance of the District’s covenant to provide the 
Continuing Disclosure; provided that any Bondholders and b eneficial owners of the Bonds seeking to require  
compliance with this Agreement shall first provide to the District at least 30 days’ prior  written notice of the 
District’s failure, giving reasonable details of such failure, following  which notice, the District shall have 30 days 
to comply. 
(b) Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, any failure of the  District to comply 
with any obligation regarding Continuing Disclosure specified in this  Agreement (i) shall not be deemed to 
constitute an event of default under the Bonds or the  resolutions or other documents providing for the issuance 
of the Bonds and (ii) shall not give rise to any right or remedy other than that described in Section 7(a) above. 
Section 9. Additional Information.  Nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to prevent the District 
from disseminating any other information, using the means of dissemination set forth in this Agreement or any other 
means of communication, or including any other information in addition to the Continuing Disclosure required by this 
Agreement. Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, the District shall not incur any obligation to continue to 
provide, or to update, such additional information or data.   
Section 10. Beneficiaries. This Agreement shall inure solely to the benefit of the Underwriters, the 
Beneficial Owners and the Dissemination Agent, and shall create no rights in any other person or entity. 
Section 11. Submission of Documents to the MSRB . Unless otherwise required by law, all documents 
provided to the MSRB pursuant to this Agreement shall be provided to the MSRB in an electronic format and shall 
be accompanied by identifying information, in each case as prescribed by the MSRB. 
Section 12. Counterparts.  This Agreement may be executed in several counterparts, each of which shall 
be an original and all of which shall constitute but one and the same instrument. 
Section 13. Governing Law.  This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the 
laws of the District of Columbia. 
[signatures on the following page]  J-1-5

This Continuing Disclosure Agreement (the "Agreement") dated May 5, 2022 is executed and delivered by 
Plenary Infrastructure DC LLC (the " Borrower") and Plenary Americas USA Ltd., as dissemination agent (the 
"Dissemination Agent"), in connection with the issuance by the District of Columbia, a body corporate and politic 
(the " Bond Issuer ") of (a) the District of Columbia Private Activity Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street Lighting 
Project), Series 2022A (Tax-Exempt, AMT) (Green Bonds),  in an aggregate principal amount of $144,485,000 (the 
“Series 2022A Bonds”), and (b) the District of Columbia Revenue Bonds (DC Smart Street Lighting Project), Series 
2022B (Federally Taxable) (Green Bonds), in an aggregate principal amount of $15,210,000 (the “ Series 2022B 
Bonds,” and together with the Series 2022A Bonds and any Additional Parity Bonds issued under this Bond Indenture, 
the “Bonds”). The Bonds are being issued pursuant to a resolution adopted by the Council of the District of Columbia 
on February 1, 2022 (the "Bond Resolution") and the Bond Indenture (as defined below). The District of Columbia, 
a body corporate and politic of the District of Columbia, existing under the Constitution and laws of the United States 
of America, acting by and through the District of Columbia Office of Public-Private Partnerships, for and on behalf 
of the District of Columbia Department of Transportation and the Office of the Chief Technology Officer (the 
"District") and the Borrower have entered into the Project Agreement (as defined below), pursuant to which the 
Borrower has agreed to perform the Work (as defined in the Project Agreement) required to complete the Project (as 
defined below). The proceeds of the Bonds are being loaned to the Borrower pursuant to the Loan Agreement (as 
defined below) for the purpose of paying a portion of the Project Costs (as defined below) which is being undertaken 
pursuant to the Project.  
In consideration of the mutual promises and agreements made herein, the receipt and sufficiency of which 
consideration is hereby mutually acknowledged, the parties hereto agree as follows: 
Section 1. Purpose of the Agreement.   This Agreement is being executed and delivered by the 
Borrower and the Dissemination Agent for the benefit of the Beneficial Owners (as defined below) and in order to 
assist the Underwriters (as defined below) in complying with the Rule (as defined below). The Borrower acknowledges 
that the Bond Issuer, the Bond Trustee and the Dissemination Agent have undertaken no responsibility with respect 
to any reports, notices or disclosures provided or required under this Agreement (except for the Dissemination Agent's 
obligation to file with the MSRB (as defined below) reports provided by the Borrower pursuant to this Agreement and 
other obligations expressly described herein), and the Bond Issuer, the Bond Trustee and the Dissemination Agent 
have no liability to any person, including any Beneficial Owner, with respect to any such reports, notices or 
disclosures. 
Section 2. Definitions.  Unless otherwise indicated next to each capitalized term, the following 
capitalized terms shall have the following meanings: 
"Annual Operating Budget" is defined in Section 3(a)(ii) hereof. 
"Annual Report" shall mean, collectively, the filings described in Section 3(a) hereof.  J-2-2

behalf of the Borrower), a list of whom shall be provided  by the Borrower to the Dissemination Agent prior to the 
date hereof (which list may be amended by the Borrower from time to time in writing). 
"Beneficial Owner" shall mean, while the Bonds are held in a book-entry only system, the actual purchaser 
of each Bond, the ownership interest of which is to be recorded on the records of the direct and indirect participants

"DTC" means The Depository Trust Company, New York, New York, and its successors and assigns.  J-2-3  
 
 
"EMMA" shall mean the Electronic Municipal Market Access system and the EMMA Continuing Disclosure 
Service of MSRB, or any successor thereto approved by the SEC, as a repository for municipal continuing disclosure 
information pursuant to the Rule. 
"Exchange Act" shall mean the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended. 
"Financial Model" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"Fiscal Quarter" means the three month period commencing on the first day of the first, fourth, seventh and 
tenth month of each Fiscal Year and ending on the last day of the third, sixth, ninth and twelfth month, respectively, 
of such Fiscal Year.  
"Fiscal Year" means a consecutive 12-month period starting on January 1 and ending on December 31, or 
any other 12-month period which the Borrower designates as its fiscal year. 
"IFRS" means the International Financial Reporting Standards.  
"Listed Events" shall mean any of the events listed in Section 4(a) of this Agreement. 
"Loan Agreement" shall mean the Loan Agreement, dated as of May 1, 2022, by and among the Bond Issuer, 
as lender, and the Borrower, as borrower, as amended, restated, supplemented or otherwise modified from time to 
time. 
"LTA" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"Maintenance Capex" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"Material Adverse Effect" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"MSRB" shall mean the Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board, or any successor thereto.  
"Person" means any natural person, firm, corporation, partnership, limited liability company, state, political 
subdivision of any state, other public body or other organization or association. 
"Project" shall have the meaning assigned to it in the Official Statement dated April 12, 2022 prepared in 
connection with the offering and sale of the Bonds. 
"Project Agreement" shall mean, the Project Agreement, dated March 7, 2022, between the District and the 
Borrower, and all attachments, exhibits and schedules ther eto, as amended, restated, supplemented or otherwise 
modified from time to time. 
"Project Costs" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"Project Final Completion Date" shall have the meaning given to it in the Project Agreement. 
"Quarterly Report" shall mean, collectively, the filings described in Section 3(b) hereof. 
"Revenues" shall have the meaning given to it in the Collateral Agency Agreement. 
"Rule" shall mean Rule 15c2-12 adopted by the Commission under the Exchange Act, as the same may be 
amended from time to time. 
“Series 2022A Bonds” shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof.  
“Series 2022B Bonds” shall have the meaning set forth in the introductory paragraph hereof.  J-2-4  
 
 
"Underwriters" shall mean TD Securities (USA) LLC and Wells Fargo Bank, National Association. 
"US GAAP" means, at any time, generally accepted accounting principles in effect and consistently applied 
in the United States (including the opinions and pronouncements of the Accounting Principles Board and the American 
Institute of Certified Public Accountants and the opinions and pronouncements of the Financial Accounting Standards 
Board or such other principles as may be approved by a significant segment of the accounting profession in the United 
States). 
Section 3. Provision of Annual Reports and Quarterly Reports.  
(a) For each Fiscal Year, the Borrower shall furnish or cause to be furnished to the Dissemination Agent 
for filing with the MSRB by and through EMMA: 
(i) no later than 120 days following the end of the Fiscal Year, commencing with the Fiscal 
Year ending December 31, 2022, the Audited Financial Statements of the Borrower prepared in accordance 
with US GAAP or IFRS, unless any such Audited Financial Statement shall not be available by such time, in 
which case the unaudited annual financial statements shall be provided and the Audited Financial Statements 
shall be delivered to the MSRB by and through EMMA when available; 
(ii) within ten (10) days following acceptance by Borrower's management but in no event less 
than thirty (30) days prior to the commencement of each Fiscal Year, an annual operating plan and a budget 
on a cash flow basis, including Revenues, operating and maintenance expenditures and Maintenance Capex, 
in each case, with respect to Project, Senior Debt (as defined in the Collateral Agency Agreement), required 
reserve account deposits, other costs and any projected distributions (collectively, an " Annual Operating 
Budget"), each prepared by the Borrower in good faith and accompanied by  an officer's certificate of the 
Borrower to the effect that such of ficer has no reason to believe that it is incorrect or misleading in any 
material respect, based upon information then known by such officer and certifying to its knowledge that no 
Event of Default (as defined in the Collateral Agency Agreement) has occurred and is continuing, except as 
set forth in such certificate, provided, however, that, in the event the Annual Operating Budget provides for 
operations and maintenance expenditures or Maintenance Capex in excess of 10% of the amounts set forth 
in the Financial Model, such Annual Operating Budget shall be accompanied by the approval of the LTA 
approving any such excesses; and 
(iii) no later than one hundred and twenty (120) days following the end of each Fiscal Year, 
commencing with the Fiscal Year e nding December 31, 2022, a variance re port for the Fiscal Year setting 
forth in comparative form the figures for the corresponding period contained in (i) the Annual Operating 
Budget, and (ii) the Financial Model as of the Closing Date (such detail to include a brief narrative 
explanation of the reasons for any such variance of 5% or more). 
(b) The Borrower shall furnish or cause to be furnis hed to the Dissemination Agent for filing by the 
Dissemination Agent with the MSRB by and through EMMA: 
(i) no later than sixty (60) days after the end of  each first three Fiscal Quarters, commencing 
with the Fiscal Quarter ending June 30, 2022, the unaud ited balance sheet of the Borrower as of the end of 
such Fiscal Quarter and the related unaudited statements of operations, cash flows and changes in members' 
capital of the Borrower for such Fiscal Quarter; 
(ii) prior to the Project Final Completion Date, on a monthly basis, no later than the 25
th 
Business Day of each calendar month following the month to which it refers, the monthly progress report 
prepared by the D&C Contractor under and pursuant to Section 2.3.4 of Exhibit 5 (Technical Provisions) to 
the D&C Contract, as reviewed by the LTA; and 
(iii) on a semi-annual basis during the Post Project Final Completion Period (as defined in the 
Project Agreement), no later than (i) ninety (90) days after the end of the Fiscal Year and (ii) ninety (90) days 
after the end of the Fiscal Quarter ending on June 30th of each Fiscal Year, an operating report showing, in  J-2-5  
 
 
each case, with respect to the Project, (A) an operating report (including analysis of deductions incurred) and 
(B) the variances for such period between the actua l Revenues and actual Project Costs incurred, and the 
projected Revenues and budgeted Project Costs respectivel y for the same period as set forth in the Annual 
Operating Budget, together with a brief narrative explanation of the reasons for any such variance of 10% or 
more. 
(c) Any or all of the items listed above may be incorporated by reference from other documents, 
including official statements of debt issues with respect to which the Borrower is an "obligated person" (as defined by 
the Rule), which have been filed with the MSRB by and through EMMA or the Commission. If the document 
incorporated by reference is a final o fficial statement, it must be availabl e from the MSRB. The Borrower or the 
Dissemination Agent (at the direction of the Borrower), as applicable, shall clearly identify each such other document 
so incorporated by reference. 
(d) If the Borrower is unable to provide or cause to be provided to the Dissemination Agent the 
information required in this Section 3 by the applicable specified dates, the Dissemination Agent shall, in a timely 
manner, send a notice in substantially the form attached as Exhibit A to the MSRB. 
Section 4. Reporting of Material Events.   
(a) The Borrower shall give, or cause to be given, notice to the Dissemination Agent for filing with the 
MSRB by and through EMMA in a timely manner not in excess of ten (10) Business Days after the occurrence of the 
event, notice of the occurrence of any of the following events with respect to the Bonds: 
(1) principal and interest payment delinquencies; 
(2) non-payment related defaults, if material; 
(3) any unscheduled draws on debt service reserves reflecting financial difficulties; 
(4) unscheduled draws on credit enhancement facilities reflecting financial difficulties; 
(5) substitution of credit or liquidity providers or their failure to perform;  
(6) adverse tax opinions, the issuance by the Internal Revenue Service of proposed or final 
determinations of taxability, Notices of Proposed Issue (IRS Form 5701-TEB) or other 
material notices or determinations with respect  to the tax status of the Bonds, or other 
material events affecting the tax status of the Bonds; 
(7) modifications to rights of the holders of the Bonds, if material; 
(8) bond calls, if material, and tender offers; 
(9) defeasances; 
(10) release, substitution, or sale of property securing repayment of the Bonds, if material; 
(11) rating changes; 
(12) bankruptcy, insolvency, receivership or similar event of the Borrower; 
(13) the consummation of a merger, consolidation, or acquisition involving the Borrower or the 
sale of all or substantially all of the assets of the Borrower, other than in the ordinary course 
of business, the entry into a definitive agr eement to undertake such an action or the 
termination of a definitive agreement relating to any such actions, other than pursuant to 
its terms, if material;   J-2-6  
 
 
(14) appointment of a successor or additional trustee or the ch ange of name of a trustee, if 
material;  
(15) incurrence of a financial obligation of the Borrower, if material, or agreement to covenants, 
events of default, remedies, priority rights, or other similar terms of a financial obligation 
of the Borrower, any of which affect the holders of the Bonds, if material; and 
(16) default, event of acceleration, termination even t, modification of terms, or other similar 
events under the terms of a financial obligation of the Borrower, any of which reflect 
financial difficulties. 
(b) The Borrower shall furnish or cause to be furnis hed to the Dissemination Agent for filing with the 
MSRB by and through EMMA in a timely manner not in excess of ten (10) Business Days after the occurrence of the 
event, notice of the occurrence of any of the following events with respect to the Bonds: 
(1) insurance claims in excess of $5,000,000; 
(2) the filing of any actual litigation, suit or action, or the delivery of any written claim which 
seeks to enjoin or stop the construction or operation of the Project, which seeks damages 
(not covered by insurance) in excess of $5,000,000, or which, if resolved, the Borrower 
could reasonably expect to have a Material Adverse Effect; and 
(3) any general economic event or condition, which the Borrower could reasonably expect to 
result in a Material Adverse Effect. 
(c) If the LTA reasonably determines at any time that the Project Final Completion Date will be delayed 
beyond the Lenders' Long Stop Deadline, the Borrower shall develop in consultation with the LTA a remedial plan 
(an "Initial Remedial Plan ") satisfactory to the LTA, act ing reasonably, that demonstrates that the Borrower will 
achieve the Project Final Completion Date by no later than the Lenders' Long Stop Deadline. The Borrower shall 
submit the Initial Remedial Plan to the LTA as soon as possible and by no later than fifteen (15) Business Days after 
such determination. The Borrower shall concurrently with (or as soon as practicable following) the submission of such 
Initial Remedial Plan to the LTA in accordance with the prece ding sentence, furnish or cause to be furnished to the 
Dissemination Agent for filing with the MSRB by and through EMMA such Initial Remedial Plan. Any amendments 
to the Initial Remedial Plan must be satisfactory to the LTA, acting reasonably. Such Initial Remedial Plan may be 
the same as that which may be required under the Project Agreement and such remedial plan required under the Project 
Agreement shall supersede any Initial Remedial Plan; provid ed that the remedial plan of the Borrower under the 
Project Agreement must provide that the Project Final Completion Date is achieved by no later than the Lenders' Long 
Stop Deadline. 
(d) If there is any material breach by the Borrower of  the Initial Remedial Plan, the Borrower shall be 
required to develop, in consultation with the LTA, a further remedial plan (a " Final Remedial Plan") satisfactory to 
the LTA, acting reasonably, which will again be required to demonstrate that the Borrower will achieve the Project 
Final Completion Date by no later than the Lenders' Long Stop Deadline. The Borrower shall submit the Final 
Remedial Plan to the LTA as soon as possible and no later than ten (10) Bu siness Days after the occurrence of a 
material breach of the Initial Remedial Plan. The Borrower shall concurrently with (or as soon as practicable 
following) the submission of such Final Remedial Plan to the LTA in accordance with the preceding sentence, furnish 
or cause to be furnished to the Dissemination Agent for filing with the MSRB by and through EMMA such Final 
Remedial Plan. Any amendments to the Final Remedial Plan must be satisfactory to the LTA, acting reasonably. Such 
Final Remedial Plan may be the same as that which may be required under the Project Agreement and such remedial 
plan required under the Project Agreement shall supersede any Final Remedial Plan; provided that the remedial plan 
of the Borrower under the Project Agreement must provide that the Project Final Completion Date is achieved by no 
later than the Lenders' Long Stop Deadline. 
Section 5. Report by Dissemination Agent .  Concurrently with the delivery to the MSRB of any 
information required pursuant to Sections 3 and 4 above, the Dissemination Agent  shall provide evidence to the  J-2-7  
 
 
Borrower  (which evidence may be in the form of a confirmation em ail from EMMA confirming submission) that it 
has filed such information with the MSRB. 
Section 6. Termination of Reporting Obligation. The Borrower's obligations under this Agreement shall 
terminate upon the defeasance, prior redemption or payment in  full of all of the Bonds. The Borrower shall provide 
or cause to be provided to the Dissemination Agent with written notice that their obligations under this Agreement 
have terminated and a written request that the Dissemination Agent file a copy of such notice with the MSRB. If 
obligations of the Borrower under this Agreement are assumed in full by some other entity, such entity shall be 
responsible for compliance with this Agreement in the same manner as if it were the Borrower, and the Borrower shall 
have no further responsibility hereunder. 
Section 7. Amendment; Waiver.   
(a) Notwithstanding any other provision of this Agreement, the Borrower and the Dissemination Agent 
may amend this Agreement (and the Dissemination Agent shall agree to any amendment so requested by the Borrower 
or the Borrower Representative (as defined in the Collateral Agency Agreement) to the extent that such amendment 
does not adversely affect the Dissemination Agent) or wa ive any provision hereof, provided that the following 
conditions are satisfied: 
(i) if the amendment or waiver relates to the provisions of Section 3 or Section 4 hereof, it 
may only be made in connection with a change in circumstances that arises from a change in legal 
requirements, change in law, or change in the identity, nature or status of the Borrower or type of business 
conducted; 
(ii) the undertakings herein, as proposed to be amended or waived, would, in the opinion of 
Bond Counsel, have complied with the requirements of the Rule at the time of the primary offering of the 
Bonds, after taking into account any amendments or interpretations of the Rule, as well as any change in 
circumstances; and 
(iii) the proposed amendment or waiver (i) is approved by the Beneficial Owners or the Bond 
Trustee (as applicable) in the manner provided in the Bond Indenture for amendments to the Bond Indenture 
or the Collateral Agency Agreement for amendments to the Collateral Agency Agreement (as applicable), or 
(ii) does not, in the opinion of the Bond Counsel, materially impair the interests of the holders of the Bonds. 
(b) In the event of any amendment to the type of financial or operating data provided in an Annual 
Report provided pursuant to Section 3 hereof, or any change in accounting principles reflected in such Annual Report 
or Quarterly Report, pursuant to the provisions hereof, the annual financial information containing the amended 
operating data or financial information shall explain, in narrative form, the reasons for the amendment and the impact 
of the change in the type of operating data or financial information being provided.  
(c) If an amendment is made to the undertaking specifying the accounting principles to be followed in 
preparing financial statements, the annual financial information for the year in which the change is made shall present 
a comparison between the financial statements or information prepared on the basis of the new accounting principles 
and those prepared on the basis of the former accounting principles.  The comparison shall include a qualitative 
discussion of the differences in the accounting principles and the impact of the change in the accounting principles on 
the presentation of the financial information, in order to provide information to investors to enable them to evaluate 
the ability of the Bond Issuer to meet its obligations.  To  the extent reasonably feasib le, the comparison shall be 
quantitative.  A notice of the change in the accounting principles shall be sent to EMMA. 
Section 8. Defaults.   
(a) If the Borrower fails to comply with any covenant  or obligation regarding continuing disclosure 
specified in this Agreement, any Bondholder or Beneficial Owner then outstanding may, by notice to the Borrower, 
proceed to protect and enforce its rights and the rights of the Bondholders and Beneficial Owners of the Bonds by an 
action for specific performance of the Borrower’s covenant to provide the continuing disclosure; provided that any  J-2-8  
 
 
Bondholders and Beneficial Owners of the Bonds seeking to require compliance with this Agreement shall first 
provide to the Borrower at least thir ty (30) days’ prior writte n notice of the Borrower’s failure, giving reasonable 
details of such failure, following which notice, the Borrower shall have thirty (30) days to comply. 
(b) Notwithstanding anything herein to the contrary, any failure of the Borrower to comply with any 
obligation regarding continuing disclosure specified in this Agreement (i) shall not be deemed to constitute an event 
of default under the Bonds, the Bond Resolution, the Bond Indenture, the Loan Agreement, the Project Agreement, 
the Collateral Agency Agreement or any other document ex ecuted in connection with th e Project or the Bonds and 
(ii) shall not give rise to any right or remedy other than that described in Section 8(a) above. 
Section 9. Additional Information. Nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to prevent the Borrower 
from disseminating any other information, using the means of dissemination set forth in this Agreement or any other 
means of communication, or including any other information in any Annual Report or Quarterly Report or notice of 
occurrence of a Listed Event, in addition to that which is required by this Agreement. If either the Borrower chooses 
to include any information in any Annual Report or Quarte rly Report or notice of occurr ence of a Listed Event, in 
addition to that which is specifically required by this Ag reement, the Borrower shall have no obligation under this 
Agreement to update such information or provide it to the Dissemination Agent to include it in any future Annual 
Report or Quarterly Report or notice of occurrence of a Listed Event.  
Section 10. Dissemination Agent's Rights and Duties.  The duties and responsibilities of the 
Dissemination Agent hereunder shall be determined solely by the express provisions of this Agreement, and no further 
duties or responsibilities shall be implied. Neither the Dissemination Agent (i) shall be liable for any error in judgment 
or for any act done or step taken or omitted by it in good faith, or for any mistake of fact or law, or for anything which 
it may do or refrain from doing in connection therewith, ex cept for its own gross negligen ce or willful misconduct, 
(ii) shall be obligated to take any legal action or othe r action hereunder, which might in its judgment involve any 
expense or liability unless it has been furnished with indemnification satisfactory to it.  The Dissemination Agent shall 
be entitled to consult with counsel satisfactory to it, and the advice or opinion of such counsel shall be full and complete 
authorization and protection in respect of any action taken, suffered or omitted by it here under in good faith and in 
accordance with the opinion of such counsel. The Dissemination Agent may rely and shall be protected in acting or 
refraining from acting upon any written notice, instruction or  request furnished to it hereunder and believed by it to 
be genuine and to have been signed or presented by the proper party or parties. The Dissemination Agent shall not 
have any liability under, or duty to inquire into, or investigate the validity, accuracy or content of any such document. 
The Dissemination Agent shall not incur any liability for following the instructions herein contained or expressly 
provided for, or written instructions given by the other parties hereto. 
The Borrower shall pay or reimburse the Dissemination Agent for its fees and expenses for the Dissemination 
Agent's services provided in accordan ce with this Agreement, in accordance with the sc hedule of fees of the 
Dissemination Agent provided to the Borrower.  
The Borrower hereby indemnifies the Dissemination Agent and its officers, directors, employees, agents and 
attorneys in fact (each, an "Indemnitee") from and against any and all losses, claims, damages, liabilities and related 
expenses, including the reasonable fees, charges and disbursements of any counsel for any Indemnitee, incurred by or 
asserted against such Indemnitee arising out of the performance by the parties hereto of their respective obligations 
hereunder; provided that the Borrower shall not be liable for the payment of any portion of such losses, claims, 
damages, liabilities or related expenses that are found by a final and non-appealable decision of a court of competent 
jurisdiction to have resulted from any Indemnitee's negligence or willful misconduct. The agreements in this Section 
shall survive the termination for any reason of this Agreement. 
In the administration of this Agreement, the Dissemination Agent may execute any of its powers and perform 
its duties hereunder directly or through agents or attorneys and may consult with counsel, accountants and other skilled 
persons to be selected and retained by it. The Dissemination Agent shall not be liable for anything done, suffered or 
omitted in good faith by it in accordance with the advice or opinion of any such counsel, accountants or other skilled 
persons. The Dissemination Agent may resign and be discharged of its duties and obligations hereunder by giving 
notice in writing of such resignation specifying a date when such resignation shall take effect. Any corporation or 
association into which the Dissemination Agent in its individual capacity may be merged or converted or with which 
it may be consolidated, or any corporation or association resulting from any merger, conversion or consolidation to  J-2-9  
 
 
which the Dissemination Agent in its individual capacity shall be a party, or any corporation or association to which 
all or substantially all the corporate trust business of the Dissemination Agent in its individual capacity may be sold 
or otherwise transferred, shall be the Dissemination Agent under this Agreement without further act. 
Section 11. Beneficiaries.  This Agreement shall inure solely to the benefit of the Bond Issuer, the 
Borrower, the Dissemination Agent, the Underwriters, and Beneficial Owners, and shall create no rights in any other 
person or entity. 
Section 12. Submission of Documents to the MSRB . Unless otherwise required by law, all documents 
provided to the MSRB pursuant to this Agreement shall be provided to the MSRB in an electronic format and shall 
be accompanied by identifying information, in each case as prescribed by the MSRB. 
Section 13. Counterparts.  This Agreement may be executed in several counterparts, each of which shall 
be an original and all of which shall constitute but one and the same instrument. 
Section 14. Governing Law.  This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the 
laws of the State of New York.

communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be delivered by hand or overnight courier service, mailed by

Person authorized to give the same on behalf of such party (other than to verify that the signature on a facsimile is the 
signature of a person authorized to give such communication on behalf of such party); and the Dissemination Agent 
shall have no liability for any losses, liabilities, costs or expenses incurred or sustained by any other party or any 
Person as a result of such reliance upon or compliance with such notices, instructions and directions. The other parties 
hereto agree to assume all risks arising out of the use of Electronic Means to submit communications to the 
Dissemination Agent, including without limitation the risk of the Dissemination Agent acting on unauthorized 
instructions, and the risk of interception and misuse by third parties.

APPENDIX K 
 
BOOK ENTRY ONLY SYSTEM 
 
 
The Depos
itory Trust Company (" DTC"), N ew York, New York, will act as securities d epository for 
the Series 2022 Bonds. The Series 2022 Bonds will be issued as f ully-reg
istered bonds registered in t he name 
of C ede & Co. (DTC's partnership nominee) or such other n ame as  may be requested by an authorized 
representative of DTC. One fully-registered bond cer tificate will be issued for each maturity of the Series 2022 
Bonds, each in the aggregate p rincipal amount of the Series 2022 Bonds of such maturity, and will be deposited 
with DTC. If, however, t he aggregate principal amount of any m aturity exceeds $500 m illion, one c ertificate 
will be issued with res pect t o eac h $500 million of  p rincipal amo unt, and an additiona l certific ate w ill be 
issued with respect to any remaining principal amo unt of such maturity. 
 
DTC, the world 's largest securities d epository, is a limited-purpose trus t company o rganized under the 
New York Banking Law, a " banking organization" within the meaning of the New York Banking L aw, a m ember 
of the Federal Reserve System, a " clearing corporation " within the meaning of the N ew York Uniform 
Commercial Code, and a " clearing a gency" registered pursuant to the provisions of Section 17A of the 
Securities Exchange Act of 1934. DTC holds and pro vides asset s ervicing for over 3.5 million issues of U.S. 
and non-U.S. equity issues, corporate and municipal debt issues, and m oney market instruments (from o ver 
100 countries) that DTC's participants (" Direct Participants") deposit w ith DTC.  DTC a lso facilitates the post-
trade settleme nt among Di rect Participants of sales and other securities t ransactions in deposited securities, 
through el ectronic computerized book-entry transfers a nd pledges b etween Direct Partici pants' accounts. This 
eliminates the need for physical movement of securities certificates. Direct Participants include both U.S. and 
non-U.S. sec urities bro kers and dealers, banks, trust companies, clearing c orporations, a nd certain o ther 
organizations. DTC is a who lly-owned subsidiary of The D epository Trust &  Clearing Corporation (" DTCC"). 
DTCC is the holding company for DTC, National Securities Clearing Corporation a nd Fixed In come Clearing 
Corporation, a ll of which a re registered clearing agencies. DTCC is owned by the u sers of its regulated 
subsidiaries. Access to the DTC system is also available to others such as both U.S. and non-U.S. securities 
brokers a nd dealers, banks, t rust companies, and clearing c orporations that clear through or maintain a 
custodial relationship with a Direct Participant, either d irectly or indirectly ( "Indirect Participants" and together 
with the Direct Participants, the "Participants"). DTC has a St andard &  Poor's rating of AA+. The DTC Rules 
applicable to its Participants a re on file with the Securities and Exchange C ommission. More information about 
DTC can be found at www.dtcc.com. 
 
Purchases of t he Series 2 022 Bonds under the D TC system must be made by or through Direct 
Participants, which w ill re ceive a credit for the Series 2022 Bonds on DTC's records. The ownership interest of 
each actual purchaser of Series 2022 Bonds (" Beneficial Bondholder") is in turn to be recorded on the Direct 
and Indirect Participants' records. Beneficial Bondholders will not receive written c onfirmation from DTC of 
their purchase. Beneficial Bondholders are, however, expected to receive written co nfirmations providing details 
of the transaction, as well as periodic statements of their holdings, from the Direct or Indirect P articipant t hrough 
which the Beneficial Bondholder entered into the transaction. Transfers of ownership in terests in the Series 2022 
Bonds a re to be accomplished by entries made on the books of Direct a nd  Indirect P articipants acting on b ehalf 
of Beneficial Bondholders. Beneficial Bondholders will n ot receive certificates representing th eir ownership 
interests in the Series 2022 Bonds, except  in the event that use of the b ook-entry system f or the Series 2022 
Bonds is discontinued. 
 
To facilitate subsequent transfers, all Series 2022 Bonds deposited by Direct Participants with DTC are 
registered in t he name of D TC's partnership nominee, Cede & Co., or such other na me as m ay be requested 
by an a uthorized representative of DTC. The deposit of Series 2022 Bonds with DTC and their  registration in 
the name of Cede & Co. or such other DTC nominee do not affect any change in beneficial ownership. DTC has 
no knowledge of the actual Beneficial Bondholders of the Series 2022 Bonds; DTC's records reflect only the 
identity of the Direct Participants to wh ose accounts such Series 2022 Bonds are credited, which may or may 
not be the B eneficial Bondholders. Th e Direct an d Indirect  Participants will re main res ponsible for keeping 
account of their holdings on behalf of their customers.  
K-2 
Conveyance of notices and ot her communications by DTC to Direct Participants, by Direct Participants 
to Indirect Pa rticipants, a nd by Direct Particip ants and  Indirect P articipants to Beneficial Bondholders will be 
governed by arrangements among them, subject to any statutory or regulatory requirements as may be in effect 
from time to time. Beneficial Bondholders may wish to take certain steps to augment the transmission to them 
of n otices of significant events with respect to the Series 2022 Bonds, such as redemptions, tenders, defaults, 
and proposed amendments to the bond documents.  For example, B eneficial Bondholders may wish to a scertain 
that the nominee holding the Series 2022 Bonds for their benefit has agreed to obtain and transmit notices to 
Beneficial Bondholders. In the al ternative, Beneficial Bondholders may wish to provide their names and 
addresses to the registrar and request that copies of no tices be provided di rectly to them.  
 
Redemption notices shall be sent to DTC. If less than a ll of the Series 2022 Bonds within a maturity 
are being redeemed, DTC's practice is to determine by lot the amount of the interest of each Direct Participant 
in such issue to be redeemed.

r DTC nor Cede & Co. (n or any other DTC nominee) will consent or vote with respect to the

Under its u sual procedures, DTC m ails an Omnibus Proxy to the Bond Issuer as soon as possible after the 
Record Date. The Omnibus Proxy assigns Cede & Co.'s consenting or voting rights t o those Direct Participants 
to whose a ccounts the Series 2022 Bonds are credited on the Record Date (identified in a listing attached to the 
Omnibus Proxy). 
 
Redemption p roceeds, and principal and interest payments on the Series 2022 Bonds will be made to

practice is to credit Di rect P articipants' accounts upon DTC's recei pt of funds and corresponding det ail 
information f rom the Bond Tr ustee or the Bond Issue r, on  the date payable in accordance with their 
respective holdings shown on DTC's r ecords. P ayments by Participants to Beneficial B ondholders will be 
governed by s tanding instructions and customary pra ctices, as is the case w ith securities held for the accounts 
of c ustomers in bearer form or registered in " street name," and will be the r esponsibility of such Participant 
and not of DTC, the Bond Trustee , the Bond Issuer or the Borrower, subject to any s tatutory or regulatory 
requirements as may b e in effect fro m time to time. Payment of redemption proceeds, and principal and 
interest to Cede & C o. (or such ot her nominee as may be requested by an authorized representative of DTC) is 
the r esponsibility of t he Bond Trustee . D isbursement of such p ayments to Direct Participants will be  the

responsibility of Direct and Indirect Participants.

1.0 Introduction 
1.1 Project Description 
The District of Columbia Office of Public-Private Partnerships (the “District” or “OP3”), in conjunction with the 
District of Columbia Depart of Transportation (“DDOT”) and the Office of the Chief Technology (“OCTO”),  has 
issued an RFP for the design, build, installation, finance and maintenance (“DBFM”) of upgrades to the entire 
existing streetlight network in the District of Columbia (the “Project”). The District selected Plenary Americas US 
Holdings Inc. (“Plenary”), Kiewit Development Company (“KDC”) and Phoenix Infrastructure Group, LLC 
(“Phoenix,” and collectively with Plenary and KDC, the “Sponsors”) to enter into a Project Agreement (“PA”) for 
the Project. The Developer is a special purpose vehicle, Plenary Infrastructure DC (the “Developer”), for the 
purpose of accepting the PA obligations to design, build, finance and maintain the Project over the 15 -year Term. 
The Project is being procured through a Public-Private Partnership (“P3”) method, which is now a common 
method of procurement in North America. P3s have been gaining significant traction and acceptance in the 
United States in recent years as a viable method to deliver large capital construction projects.  
1.2 Mandate 
The Project Lenders are considering issuing “Green Bonds” as part of the Project financing package. BTY hav e 
been retained by the Lenders to assess whether bonds issued in relation to the Project could reasonably qualify 
as “Green Bonds”. Please see section 2.1 for further details on Green Bonds and their accepted definition.  
1.3 Project Scope 
The Project is located in Washington, D.C. The existing streetlight network consists of 72,051 primary and alley 
streetlights, select overhead guide signs, lighting for approximately 30 “Welcome to Washington, D.C.” signs and 
lighting for navigation, underpasses, bike trails and tunnels.  
The Developer is responsible for the following:  
• The conversion of existing non-LED District-owned lights to LED technology (approx.75,000);  
• The design and installation of remote monitoring and control systems for the streetlight network  (239 new 
wireless access points); 
• Upgrading poles and associated infrastructure;  
• The delivery of long-term asset management of the streetlight system, in accordance with the Project 
Agreement;  
• The installation of smart city technology (“SCT”) in addition to the District’s fiber optic network (“DC-Net”) 
for the delivery of wi-fi and sensor technologies; and  
• The financing of the improvements to the District’s existing streetlight network and SCT installation.  
DC-Net provides wireless services to all Government constituents throughout the District and is managed by 
OCTO and will serve in the District’s planned SCT solutions. The Project’s procurement efforts will be led by OP3. 
OP3 will be working closely DDOT and OCTO throughout the procurement process.  
The electricity for the current streetlight network is supplied by Washington Gas and Electric (“WGES”) and 
distributed through the Potomac Electric Power Company (“Pepco”) network. The distribution network consists 
of overhead (50%) and underground powerlines (50%). Pepco and Verizon both own portions of the poles (37% 
and 9% respectively) and retain operations and maintenance responsibilities for their respective poles. The 
District retains ownership of the remaining 54% of the poles. All arms and fixtures are owned by DDOT and asset 
management responsibilities for these items are included in the Project.  Project Lenders on behalf of Plenary Infrastructure DC | Green Bond Assessment – Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number 1.0 | March 31, 2022

Management Contract, Interface Agreement, or the Key Contracts noted above as determined by the context of 
the section being read.

do not provide legal, insurance or tax advice or opinion.

2.0 DCSL Project Green Bonds 
2.1 Designation as Green Bonds 
The Developer has self-designated the 2022 Bonds as “Green Bonds” based on the Green Bond Principles 
(“GBP”), updated as of June 2021, established as a financial bond instrument where the proceeds or an 
equivalent amount will be exclusively applied to finance or re-finance, in part or in full, new and/or existing 
eligible Green Projects in accordance with the Sustainability Bond Guidelines and the Sustainability -Linked Bond 
Principles. The Technical Advisor believes the Project constitutes an “eligible Green Project” under the GBP 
because it has two key environmental and social purposes:  
• To improve the city’s sustainability efforts by reducing streetlight energy consumption by more than 50%, 
eliminating 38,000 tons of greenhouse gas emissions each year;  
• Reduction of glare and light pollution that could affect wildlife or other light sensitive activities in the 
District;  
• Promote more efficient management systems with remote monitoring and control capabilities;  and 
• Adding 239 Wireless Access Points, expanding the District’s broadband Wi-Fi network to more areas of need 
and integrating smart city technology to track and improve the networks and the networks energy usage.  
The GBP are voluntary process guidelines that recommend transparency and disclosure and are intended to 
promote integrity by clarifying the approach for issuance of a Green Bond. The GBP are comprised of the 
following four components:  
• Use of Proceeds;  
• Process for Project Evaluation and Selection;  
• Management of Proceeds; and  
• Reporting.  
In accordance with the 2021 edition of the GBP, the following sections set fort information relating to the Project 
and the use of proceeds of the 2022 Bonds within the framework of the four components of the GBP.  
2.2 Use of Proceeds 
The green bond utilization of the proceeds towards a green project must provide clear environmentally 
sustainable benefits which, where feasible, will be quantified or assessed by the issuer. The Developer, not the 
Bond Issuer, has taken all action and will undertake all future action concerning the eligibility of the Project using 
green bond proceeds and the utilization of the proceeds of the 2022 Bonds for an eligible green project.  
The Project is expected to generate certain environmental and other benefits as further outlined below and in 
the Technical Advisor Report.  
2.3 Process for Project Evaluation and Selection  
The GBP encourages a high level of transparency in the evaluation and selection process and recommends that a 
green bonds issuer outline the decision-marking process it follows to determine the eligibility of a project using 
green bond proceeds. The Developer, not the Bond Issuer, has taken all action and will undertake all future 
action concerning the eligibility of the Project using green bond proceeds and the utilization of the proceeds of 
the 2022 Bonds for an eligible green project.  Project Lenders on behalf of Plenary Infrastructure DC | Green Bond Assessment – Smart Street Lighting Project 
Report Number 1.0 | March 31, 2022 
127 John Street., Toronto, ON, M5V 2E2 | 604 734 3126 
This report has been prepared at the request of Project Lenders on behalf of Plenary Infrastructure DC and is the exclusive property of BTY 
Group. The information  
must be treated as confidential and not to be disclosed, reproduced or permitted to be disclosed to any party without the prior consent  
of BTY Group. 6 
While the District, not the Developer, developed and obtained environmental approvals for the Project and 
assessed the environmental, social and economic effects of the Project, based on the records of such approvals, 
the Developer has concluded that the Project constitutes an “eligible Green Project” under the GBP.  
Per Green Bond Principles, “Project Evaluation and Selection”, the development, bidding, and award of the 
Project has been carried out with a high level of diligence and transparency at each stage.  
2.4 Management of Proceeds 
Proceeds of the Green Bond will be tracked by the Developer on behalf of Project Lenders generally in 
accordance with the Lenders Financial Proposal. Provided the bonds are still outstanding, the balance of the 
funds generated through the bond issue will be reduced through disbursement to the Project. Prior to 
disbursement, the balance of the funds will be held as cash or as short-term liquid money-market investments 
held by the Developer and the Project Lenders. 
Per Green Bond Principles, “Management of Proceeds”, the proceeds of the bond issue will be tracked by a 
formal process linked to the issuer, the Developer, and the Project Lenders. The process outlined in the Financial 
Submission conforms to this principle. 
Proceeds of the Series 2022 Bonds will be tracked by the Developer. So long as the Series 2022 Bonds remain 
outstanding, the balance of the proceeds will be reduced by amounts disbursed for the Project. Provided the 
bonds are still outstanding, the balance of the proceeds will be generated through the bond issue will be reduced 
through disbursement to the Project. Prior to disbursement, the balance of the funds will be held as cash or 
Qualified Investments. 
2.5 Reporting  
Until the Series 2022 Bonds proceeds have been fully allocated, the Developer will publish annual updates 
regarding the amount of net proceeds expended on the Project. Disclosures will be made through the Electronic 
Municipal Market Access system of the Municipal Securities Rulemaking Board, accessible at 
www.emma.msrb.org.  Once all net proceeds of the Series 2022 Bonds are expended and disclosure regarding 
such expenditures is made, no further disclosures as to expenditure of proceeds will be provided. The Developer 
has committed to a number of design and operational characteristics of the Project in order to advance key 
environmental goals of the Project. The Developer may provide reports and updates to this end, however, such 
reports and updates are provided on a voluntary basis and are not included as part of the Developer’s Continuing 
Disclosure Agreement for the Series 2022 Bonds. 
A key element of the Project is the energy savings target of over 50% against the existing baseline usage which 
primarily utilizes inefficient incandescent and high intensity discharge arc tubes (“HPS”) bulbs. LED lights 
generally last between 35,000 to 50,000 hours which is a 51% improvement from HPS and 96% improvement 
from incandescent bulbs. This will drastically reduce both energy consumption, but also outages and the time 
and effort required to replace street lights. Furthermore, the Developer is held to performance metrics with 
financial penalties to ensure energy performance is uphold throughout the Project term. The Developer will 
maintain a continuous log of the energy performance throughout the Project term.  
Per Green Bond Principles, “Reporting”, the Project has numerous quantitative KPI’s to help measure and justify 
its expected environmental performance, and hence justify the expectation of the Green Bonds investors. The 
requirements of the PA ensure the Project conforms to this principle. 
2.6 Conclusions  
Based on our review, we make the following conclusions 
• The Project conforms to the Green Bond Categories of Energy efficiency, and to a lesser extent, 
Pollution Prevention and Control; and Project Lenders on behalf of Plenary Infrastructure DC | Green Bond Assessment – Smart Street Lighting Project

must be treated as confidential and not to be disclosed, reproduced or permitted to be disclosed to any party without the prior consent  
of BTY Group. 7 
• The Project meets the Core Green Bond principles of Use of Proceeds, Process for Project Evaluation 
and Selection, Management of Proceeds, and Reporting. 
Based on the above, we consider the Project to be appropriate for green financing.
